# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme University Exam pattern**. Marks distribution follows the standard: **Part A = 3 marks** (short answer), **Part B = 14 marks** (with internal choice and two 7-mark sub-parts). All questions align with the **Course Outcomes CO1–CO5** and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy levels.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six workshop safety precautions that must be followed while working in a mechanical workshop.**

#### Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 each = 3 marks):
1. **Always wear personal protective equipment (PPE):** safety goggles, apron, gloves, safety shoes.
2. **Inspect tools and machines before use** to check for damage, wear, or loose parts.
3. **Never operate a machine without reading the instruction manual** or without the instructor's permission.
4. **Keep the work area clean** and free of oil spills, scrap, and clutter to avoid slipping hazards.
5. **Use the correct tool for the job** — never use a spanner as a hammer, etc.
6. **Know the location of fire extinguishers, first-aid kits, emergency stop buttons, and exits** before starting any work.
7. **Avoid loose clothing, jewellery, and long untied hair** near rotating machinery.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a plain bearing and an anti-friction bearing. Give one example of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
| Feature | Plain Bearing | Anti-Friction Bearing |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Principle** | Sliding contact | Rolling contact |
| **Friction** | Higher | Much lower |
| **Speed suitability** | Low to moderate speed | High speed |
| **Lubrication** | Mandatory (oil film) | Grease/oil — periodic |
| **Construction** | Simple cylindrical sleeve | Inner race, outer race, rolling elements, cage |
| **Load capacity** | High radial loads, low speed | Wide range, high speed |
| **Example** | Crankshaft journal bearing in an IC engine | Deep groove ball bearing in an electric motor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key points to include for full marks:** the difference in **contact type (sliding vs. rolling)**, **friction level**, and at least **one correct example** for each.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a Vernier Caliper**. Derive the expression for its **least count**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Vernier caliper has its main scale graduated in **1 mm** and the Vernier scale has **20 divisions** that coincide with **19 mm** on the main scale. While measuring a workpiece:
- The Vernier zero lies **6 mm past** the 22nd mm mark.
- The **12th Vernier division** coincides with a main scale line.
- The instrument has a **zero error of +0.05 mm**.

Find the **true dimension** of the workpiece. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction, Working & Derivation (7 marks):**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A Vernier caliper consists of:
- A **main scale (M.S.)** fixed on the beam, graduated in **1 mm** divisions.
- A **Vernier scale (V.S.)** that slides along the main scale, with **20 divisions** spanning 19 mm (in the L.C. = 0.05 mm version).
- **Fixed jaws** on the left (lower for outside measurement, upper for inside measurement).
- **Movable jaws** on the right that slide with the Vernier.
- A **depth rod** at the right end for measuring depths.
- A **locking screw** to fix the slider position.

**Working (2 marks):**
1. The jaws are closed first to check for **zero error**.
2. The workpiece is held gently between the jaws (for OD) or between the inner jaws (for ID), or the depth rod is extended into a hole (for depth).
3. The slider is moved until the jaws just contact the workpiece; the slider is locked.
4. The **MSR** is read from the main scale just before the Vernier zero.
5. The **VC** is the Vernier division coinciding exactly with a main scale line.
6. The reading is: $\text{Reading} = \text{MSR} + (\text{VC} \times \text{L.C.})$, and corrected for zero error.

**Derivation of L.C. (3 marks):**
Let one M.S. division = $1 \text{ mm}$, and the V.S. has $n = 20$ divisions spanning 19 mm.
$$\text{L.C.} = \frac{\text{MSD}}{n} = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{20} = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

Full derivation (as in Section 3.1):
$$\text{L.C.} = \text{MSD} - \text{VSD} = 1 - \frac{19}{20} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Least Count**
$$\text{L.C.} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Main Scale Reading (MSR)**
The Vernier zero lies 6 mm past the 22nd mm mark:
$$\text{MSR} = 22 + 6 = 28 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Vernier Coincidence (VC)**
$$\text{VC} = 12$$

**Step 4: Vernier Contribution**
$$\text{VC} \times \text{L.C.} = 12 \times 0.05 = 0.60 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Measured Reading**
$$\text{Measured} = 28 + 0.60 = 28.60 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Zero Error Correction**
Zero error is +0.05 mm → **subtract**:
$$\text{True Reading} = 28.60 - 0.05$$

$$\boxed{\text{True Dimension} = 28.55 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a Micrometer (Screw Gauge)**. Derive the expression for its **least count**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A micrometer has a **pitch of 0.5 mm** and the thimble is divided into **50 equal divisions**. The main scale shows **16 mm** and the **0.5 mm mark is exposed** on the lower scale. The thimble reads **32 divisions**. The zero error is **−0.02 mm**. Find the **true thickness** of the part. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction, Working & Derivation (7 marks):**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A micrometer consists of:
- A **C-shaped frame** (anvil side on the left, spindle side on the right).
- A **fixed anvil** on the left against which the workpiece rests.
- A **movable spindle** connected to a precision screw.
- A **sleeve (barrel)** with the main scale, graduated in mm (upper) and 0.5 mm (lower).
- A **thimble** that rotates, with 50 divisions (circular scale).
- A **ratchet stop** to ensure constant measuring force.
- A **locking lever** to fix the spindle position.

**Working (2 marks):**
1. The anvils are brought together to check the **zero error**.
2. The workpiece is held between the anvil and the spindle.
3. The thimble is rotated (using the **ratchet stop** to apply correct force) until the ratchet clicks 3 times.
4. The **MSR** is read from the sleeve (mm + 0.5 mm if visible).
5. The **VSR** is read from the thimble (division aligned with the datum line).
6. Total: $\text{Reading} = \text{MSR} + (\text{VSR} \times \text{L.C.})$, corrected for zero error.

**Derivation of L.C. (3 marks):**
If the pitch is $P$ and the thimble has $n$ divisions, then:
$$\text{L.C.} = \frac{P}{n}$$

For standard metric micrometer: $P = 0.5 \text{ mm}$, $n = 50$:
$$\text{L.C.} = \frac{0.5}{50} = 0.01 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Least Count**
$$\text{L.C.} = \frac{0.5}{50} = 0.01 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Main Scale Reading (MSR)**
- Upper scale: 16 mm
- Lower scale: 0.5 mm visible
$$\text{MSR} = 16 + 0.5 = 16.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Thimble Reading (VSR)**
$$\text{VSR} = 32$$

**Step 4: Thimble Contribution**
$$\text{VSR} \times \text{L.C.} = 32 \times 0.01 = 0.32 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Measured Reading**
$$\text{Measured} = 16.5 + 0.32 = 16.82 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Zero Error Correction**
Zero error is **−0.02 mm** → **add** the magnitude:
$$\text{True Reading} = 16.82 - (-0.02) = 16.82 + 0.02$$

$$\boxed{\text{True Thickness} = 16.84 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of zero error:** Students often **always subtract** the zero error. Remember: subtract for **positive** Z.E., add for **negative** Z.E.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Always write the **unit (mm)** in the final answer. KTU examiners **deduct ½ mark** for missing units.
> 3. **Confusing the two scales of the main scale (micrometer):** Don't forget to include the **0.5 mm** from the lower scale.
> 4. **Forgetting to use the ratchet stop:** In the practical exam, applying force **directly on the thimble** damages the instrument and gives **wrong readings** — supervisors deduct marks.
> 5. **Vernier coincidence identification:** Always choose the **line that coincides *exactly***, not the *nearest*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Workshop Practice & Safety
- ✅ **Always wear PPE** before entering the workshop.
- ✅ **Inspect tools and machines** before use.
- ✅ **Know the location** of fire extinguishers, first-aid kits, and emergency exits.
- ✅ **No horseplay, no jewellery, no loose clothing** near rotating machines.
- ✅ Follow the **5 Pillars of Workshop Safety**: PPE, Tool Inspection, Machine Guarding, Housekeeping, Emergency Awareness.

### Shop Floor Ethics
- ✅ Maintain **discipline, integrity, punctuality, and respect for equipment**.
- ✅ **Record only what you observe** — never copy another student's measurements.
- ✅ **No horseplay** — discipline is non-negotiable in a workshop.

### First Aid Essentials
- ✅ **DRAB sequence:** Danger → Response → Airway → Breathing.
- ✅ **Burns:** Cool under water, do **NOT** apply ice or butter.
- ✅ **Eye injury:** Rinse, do **NOT** rub.
- ✅ **Electric shock:** Switch off mains first; do **NOT** touch victim directly.
- ✅ **Cuts:** Apply pressure, do **NOT** apply a tourniquet for minor cuts.
- ✅ **Fractures:** Immobilize, do **NOT** attempt to reset.

### Hand Tools
- ✅ **Screwdrivers:** Match tip to screw head — never use a flat screwdriver on a Phillips screw.
- ✅ **Spanners:** Pull, don't push. Use the right size.
- ✅ **Allen keys:** Sized in mm; standard sizes 1.5–17 mm.
- ✅ **Cutting pliers:** Cut only what they're rated for — don't cut hard steel with side cutters.

### Mechanical Components
- ✅ **Bearings:** Two main types — **Plain (sliding)** and **Anti-friction (rolling)**. Anti-friction has lower friction.
- ✅ **Bearing code (6XXX):** Last 2 digits × 5 = bore in mm (for 04 and above).
- ✅ **Seals:** Prevent leakage and contamination. Common types: lip, O-ring, gasket, mechanical.
- ✅ **O-rings:** Toroidal elastomer seals; selection based on fluid, temperature, pressure, size.
- ✅ **Circlips:** External (on shaft) and internal (in bore); use circlip pliers.
- ✅ **Keys:** Transmit torque between shaft and hub; common types — sunk, parallel, taper, Woodruff, gib head.

### Measurement Tools — Must-Know Numbers
- ✅ **Vernier Caliper L.C.** = **0.02 mm** (50 div over 49 mm) — most common.
- ✅ **Micrometer L.C.** = **0.01 mm** (pitch 0.5 mm / 50 div).
- ✅ **Micrometer is 2× more precise** than a Vernier.
- ✅ **Bevel Protractor L.C.** = **5 minutes (5′)**.
- ✅ **Always apply zero-error correction** at the end of the calculation.
- ✅ **Use the ratchet stop** on a micrometer for consistent force.

### Formula Quick-Recall
- ✅ **Vernier L.C.** = MSD / n
- ✅ **Micrometer L.C.** = Pitch / n
- ✅ **True Reading** = Measured − Zero Error
- ✅ **Vernier Reading** = MSR + (VC × L.C.)
- ✅ **Micrometer Reading** = MSR + (VSR × L.C.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked *“Explain the principle of ___”* in viva, always **begin with a one-line definition**, **list the parts**, **state the formula for L.C.**, and **mention a real-world application**. This 4-step structure guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carpentry: Understanding carpentry tools and knowledge of at least one model: T-Lap joint, Cross lap joint, Dovetail joint, Mortise joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCESL106 - ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Carpentry: Understanding carpentry tools and knowledge of at least one model: T-Lap joint, Cross lap joint, Dovetail joint, Mortise joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Carpentry?

**Carpentry** is the skilled trade of cutting, shaping, and joining timber and other wood-based materials to construct frameworks, furniture, and architectural structures. In the KTU Engineering Workshop context, it represents one of the most fundamental **manufacturing/model-making trades**, where students learn the basics of **material selection**, **dimensional measurement**, **cutting**, and **joining** using hand tools.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Carpentry is defined as the art and science of working with wood, involving the use of hand tools to shape timber into desired forms and to create strong, durable joints between wooden members without the use of fasteners like nails or screws (in traditional carpentry).*

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego of Wood" Idea

Think of carpentry as building with **wooden Lego blocks**, but with a crucial difference: instead of snapping pre-made blocks together, you must **cut your own blocks** from raw timber and carve special interlocking shapes so they fit perfectly. A carpenter is essentially a "**wood surgeon**" — using sharp tools to cut away exactly the right amount of material so that two pieces of wood become one solid, strong unit.

## 1.2 Classification of Carpentry Tools

Carpentry tools are broadly classified into **five functional categories** based on their purpose:

| S.No. | Category | Primary Function | Example Tools |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Marking & Measuring Tools** | To mark dimensions and lines on timber | Carpenter's square, marking gauge, try square, steel rule, scriber |
| 2 | **Holding & Supporting Tools** | To hold timber firmly during cutting | Bench vice, sash cramp, hand screw, bench hook |
| 3 | **Cutting Tools** | To remove material / cut wood | Hand saw, tenon saw, chisel, mallet, jack plane, smoothing plane |
| 4 | **Striking/Hitting Tools** | To drive chisels or other tools | Wooden mallet, claw hammer |
| 5 | **Miscellaneous/Smoothing Tools** | To smooth, finish, or test | File, rasp, sandpaper, bevel gauge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Tools You MUST Know:**
> For the lab examination, students are expected to know the **name, function, and basic safety use** of the following tools:
> - **Marking:** Try square, marking gauge, carpenter's square, steel rule
> - **Cutting:** Hand saw (cross-cut & rip saw), tenon saw, coping saw, chisel (firmer & mortise), jack plane, smoothing plane
> - **Striking:** Mallet (wooden), claw hammer
> - **Testing/Holding:** Bevel gauge, spirit level, bench vice

## 1.3 Detailed Description of Essential Carpentry Tools

### 1.3.1 Measuring & Marking Tools

**1. Try Square (L-Square)**
- Shaped like the letter "**L**" — has a fixed 90° angle between the **blade** (long steel part) and the **stock** (wooden/metal handle).
- Used to test the **squareness** of edges and to mark **perpendicular lines (90°)** across a timber face.
- Blade length commonly: **150 mm, 200 mm, or 250 mm**.

**2. Marking Gauge**
- Consists of a **stock** (wooden block), a **beam** (wooden/ruler), and an adjustable **marking pin/thumb screw**.
- Used to scribe **parallel lines** along the grain of wood — particularly for marking the **shoulder line** of a joint.
- Two types: **Single-pin gauge** (one line) and **Double-pin gauge** (two parallel lines for tenons).

**3. Steel Rule (Folding Rule / Straight Rule)**
- Standard measuring scale, usually **300 mm (12 inches)** or **600 mm (24 inches)**.
- Marked in **mm and inches** for conversion.

**4. Carpenter's Square (Framing Square)**
- Large L-shaped square with a **24-inch long arm (blade)** and a **16-inch or 18-inch short arm (tongue)**.
- Used for larger timber work and roof framing.

**5. Bevel Square (Sliding Bevel)**
- Has an **adjustable blade** that can be set to any angle using a thumb-screw.
- Used to mark and test **angles other than 90°** — e.g., dovetail angles (typically **1:6 slope = 14°**).

**6. Spirit Level**
- Contains a sealed glass vial of liquid with an **air bubble** to indicate horizontal (level) or vertical (plumb) surfaces.

### 1.3.2 Cutting Tools

**1. Hand Saw (Cross-Cut Saw)**
- Has a **flexible steel blade** with hardened teeth designed to cut **across the wood grain**.
- Teeth are filed in an **alternating pattern** to cut a kerf wider than the blade thickness, preventing binding.
- Common length: **500 mm to 600 mm**.

**2. Rip Saw**
- Designed to cut **along the grain** (ripping).
- Teeth are shaped like **small chisels** in a straight line — each tooth acts like a small gouge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for Students:**
> *Cross-cut teeth = "**X**-shaped points" (zigzag pattern) — used across grain.*
> *Rip saw teeth = "**I**" (straight) like a row of chisels — used along grain.*

**3. Tenon Saw (Back Saw)**
- A small, fine-toothed saw with a **stiffened back** (brass/steel spine) to keep the blade rigid.
- Used for precise cuts like **tenons, shoulders, and dovetails**.
- Length: **250–300 mm**, with 12–14 teeth per inch (TPI).

**4. Coping Saw**
- A thin blade held in a **U-shaped frame** with a handle.
- Blade can rotate 360° to allow cutting **intricate curves** and removing waste from **dovetail joints** and **mortises**.

**5. Chisel (Firmer Chisel)**
- A long steel blade with a **sharpened bevelled edge** at one end and a **handle (wooden/plastic)** at the other.
- Sizes range from **3 mm to 50 mm** in width.
- **Bevel-edge chisels** are used for fine work; **firmer chisels** (thicker) for general work.
- Used in conjunction with a **mallet** for hand-driven cutting.

**6. Mortise Chisel**
- A **heavier, thicker chisel** with a stronger cross-section to withstand the **heavy mallet blows** required for chopping out mortise waste.
- Has a **square or rectangular blade** for clean, straight-sided mortise walls.

**7. Jack Plane & Smoothing Plane**
- A **jack plane** (~350 mm long) is used for **rough smoothing** of timber surfaces.
- A **smoothing plane** (~240 mm long) is used for **final finishing** of surfaces.
- Both have a **blade (iron)** set at an angle (~45°) and a **depth adjustment lever**.

### 1.3.3 Striking & Holding Tools

**1. Wooden Mallet**
- A **cylindrical or rectangular head** of hardwood (beech or maple) with a tapered or straight handle.
- Used to **strike chisels** without damaging the chisel handle or bouncing off.

**2. Claw Hammer**
- A steel-headed hammer with a **flat striking face** and a **curved, split claw** at the back for pulling out nails.
- Head weight: **250 g to 450 g** commonly.

**3. Bench Vice**
- A heavy clamping device fixed to the workbench.
- Used to hold timber firmly while cutting or chiselling.

## 1.4 Introduction to Carpentry Joints

A **joint** is a method of connecting two or more pieces of timber together to form a single structural unit. The strength of any wooden structure depends primarily on the **type of joint** used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandatory Joints (Syllabus Requirement):**
> Students **must** have hands-on knowledge of the construction of **at least one** of the following joints:
> 1. **T-Lap Joint (Corner Halving Joint)**
> 2. **Cross Lap Joint (Cross Halving Joint)**
> 3. **Dovetail Joint (Through or Half-blind)**
> 4. **Mortise & Tenon Joint**
> 
> Most KTU labs perform the **T-Lap Joint** as the standard model.

### 1.4.1 Why are Joints Used Instead of Nails?

Think of a joint like a **handshake between two pieces of wood** — when designed properly:
- It provides **mechanical interlocking** (gluing makes it permanent).
- It uses the **natural strength of wood grain** in compression and shear.
- It produces a **cleaner, more aesthetic** finish without visible fasteners.
- It is **stronger** in tension and torsion than a nailed joint.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall:**
> Many students confuse **"lap"** with **"halving"**. In KTU exams, both terms refer to the same family of joints. The T-lap is also called the **"T-Halving Joint"** or **"Corner Halving Joint"**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Specifications Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Major Joints — Conceptual Breakdown

Each joint is essentially an answer to an **engineering problem**: *How do I connect two wooden members at a particular angle, and have the connection be as strong as the wood itself?*

### 2.1.1 T-Lap Joint (Corner Halving Joint)

**Concept:**
Two wooden members cross each other at a **"T" shape** (90°). Half the thickness of each piece is **removed at the intersection** so the pieces fit flush into one another.

**How it works:**
- Each piece has a **half-lap notch** (slot) cut halfway through its thickness.
- The two notches interlock — like two puzzle pieces slotting together.
- Result: The total height at the joint equals the thickness of **one** piece (not two), giving a **flush, flat surface**.

**Engineering use:**
- Used in **wall framing** where a stud meets a horizontal plate.
- Used in **furniture making** for cross-rails.
- Common in **door/window frame construction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Half-Lap"?**
> The notch depth is **half the thickness (t/2)** of the timber. If the wood is 25 mm thick, each notch is **12.5 mm** deep. The two notches together account for 25 mm — exactly the thickness of one piece.

### 2.1.2 Cross Lap Joint (Cross Halving Joint)

**Concept:**
Two wooden members **cross each other at 90° in the same plane** (both pieces lie flat, like a **"+" sign**).

**How it works:**
- Each piece has a **half-lap notch** cut at its end (or center) exactly as in the T-lap.
- The two notches interlock in a **flat cross (+) pattern**.
- Result: The surface remains **flat and flush** in one plane.

**Engineering use:**
- Used in **trestles, table legs, scaffolding**.
- Used in **drawer dividers** in cabinetry.
- Common in **structural bracing** where members must cross in the same plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **T-Lap vs. Cross Lap — Key Distinction:**
> - **T-Lap:** Members meet at right angles in **different planes** (one horizontal, one vertical → forms a "T").
> - **Cross Lap:** Members meet at right angles in the **same plane** (both flat → forms a "+").
> 
> This is the **most-asked distinguishing question** in KTU viva and Part A.

### 2.1.3 Dovetail Joint

**Concept:**
A joint where one piece has a series of **trapezoidal "tails"** (or "pins") that interlock with a matching set of **"pins"** (or "tails") on the other piece.

**How it works:**
- The angled shape of the "tails" — typically with a slope of **1:6 (≈ 9.5°)** or **1:8 (≈ 7°)** — mechanically prevents the joint from being pulled apart in the direction of the grain.
- The **mechanical interlock** is so strong that the joint is held together purely by geometry — no glue, pin, or nail is needed for primary strength.

**Types of Dovetail Joints:**
1. **Through Dovetail** — both pins and tails are visible from both sides.
2. **Half-Blind Dovetail** — tails are hidden, only pins are visible (used in drawer fronts).
3. **Secret Dovetail (Full Blind)** — completely hidden from view (used in fine cabinetry).

**Engineering use:**
- Considered the **strongest wooden joint** for resisting tensile pull-apart forces.
- Used in **drawer construction, jewelry boxes, fine furniture, crates, and military chests**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Dovetail So Strong?**
> Imagine a row of **interlocking fingers** — if you try to pull the two pieces apart along the grain, the wedge-shaped fingers **jam harder** into each other. The angle creates a **mechanical lock** similar to the "puzzle pieces" analogy from Section 1.

### 2.1.4 Mortise & Tenon Joint

**Concept:**
A joint where one piece (the **tenon**) has a **rectangular projection** that fits into a corresponding **rectangular cavity (mortise)** in the other piece.

**How it works:**
- **Tenon** is a rectangular tongue cut on the end of one member.
- **Mortise** is a rectangular hole cut into the other member.
- The tenon is inserted into the mortise, glued, and sometimes pinned with a **wooden dowel (draw-bore pin)** for extra strength.

**Engineering use:**
- Considered the **workhorse of timber framing**.
- Used in **door and window frames, chair legs, table frames, bed frames, and traditional timber-frame buildings**.
- Can be **haunched**, **stubbed**, **through**, or **blind** depending on the application.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:**
> *Mortise = "**M**ortise is **Made** (the hole) — a 'cavity' in wood."*
> *Tenon = "**T**enon **T**hrusts out — a 'projection' from wood."*

## 2.2 KTU Specifications & Formulas Cheat Sheet

Since carpentry is a **practical trade**, the "formulas" relate to **proportions, dimensions, and standard slopes** of joints. The following table is the **must-know reference for KTU viva and lab exams**:

| S.No. | Parameter | Standard Formula/Rule | Typical Value | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Lap Joint Notch Depth** | $D = \dfrac{t}{2}$ | $D = 12.5$ mm for $t = 25$ mm | T-Lap, Cross Lap |
| 2 | **Mortise Width** | $W_m = \dfrac{1}{3} \times t$ | $W_m = 8.3$ mm for $t = 25$ mm | Mortise & Tenon |
| 3 | **Tenon Width** | $W_t = \dfrac{1}{3} \times t$ | Same as mortise width | Mortise & Tenon |
| 4 | **Tenon Length (Shoulder)** | $L_t = 2 \times W_t$ to $3 \times W_t$ | 16–25 mm for $t = 25$ mm | Mortise & Tenon |
| 5 | **Tenon Thickness** | $T_t = \dfrac{t}{3}$ to $\dfrac{t}{2}$ | 8–12.5 mm for $t = 25$ mm | Mortise & Tenon |
| 6 | **Tenon Total Length** | $L_{total} = L_t + $ (chamfer + shoulders) | ~30–40 mm | Mortise & Tenon |
| 7 | **Dovetail Slope** | Slope = $1:6$ or $1:8$ | Angle $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{6}\right) \approx 9.46°$ | Dovetail |
| 8 | **Dovetail Pin Width** | $W_p = 1 \times t$ to $1.5 \times t$ | 25–38 mm | Dovetail |
| 9 | **Dovetail Tail Width** | $W_{tail} = \dfrac{t}{2}$ to $t$ | 12.5–25 mm | Dovetail |
| 10 | **Number of Tails** | $N = \dfrac{L_{board}}{3 \times t}$ to $\dfrac{L_{board}}{4 \times t}$ | E.g., 3–4 tails for 100 mm board | Dovetail |
| 11 | **Mortise Depth** | $D_m = L_t + 3$ mm (glue space) | ~20–28 mm | Mortise & Tenon |
| 12 | **Maximum Haunch Width** | $W_h = \dfrac{W_m}{2}$ | 4–5 mm | Haunched Tenon |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "1/3 Rule" for Tenon Width?**
> The tenon must be **strong in shear and bending**, but the **mortise walls** must also remain strong. If the tenon is too wide, the wood between the mortise and the edge becomes too thin and can **split out**. A width of **1/3 of timber thickness** is the historical rule of thumb used by carpenters for centuries.

### 2.2.1 Mathematical Derivation of Dovetail Slope

The **slope ratio 1:6** means that for every **6 units of length** along the grain, the dovetail rises by **1 unit** of width.

$$\text{Slope Ratio} = \frac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}} = \frac{1}{6}$$

The **angle of the dovetail side** with respect to the board face is:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}}\right)$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.1667)$$

$$\theta \approx 9.46°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Use of Slope Angles:**
> This 9.46° angle is the same principle used in **wedge design** and **tapered keys in machine tool holding**. The shallower the angle, the harder it is to pull apart (mechanical advantage) but the harder it is to assemble. The 1:6 ratio is a centuries-old compromise between **joint strength** and **ease of assembly**.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility of These Joints

| Joint | Real-World Application | Engineering Principle |
|:---|:---|:---|
| T-Lap | Wall stud to plate in building frames | Load transfer in orthogonal direction |
| Cross Lap | Scaffold cross-bracing | In-plane shear resistance |
| Dovetail | Drawer corners, jewellery boxes | Tensile pull-apart resistance |
| Mortise & Tenon | Door frames, chair legs, timber-frame buildings | Moment-resistant rigid frame connection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection to Modern Engineering:**
> The **mortise & tenon joint** is conceptually identical to a **dowel pin joint** in steel fabrication and a **tenon-slot connection** in modular furniture. The **dovetail** is the basis of **taper-lock assemblies** in machine tool spindles and **turbine blade roots**. Understanding these traditional wooden joints gives a strong foundation for understanding **interlocking mechanical assemblies** in modern mechanical engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 Practical Requirement:**
> The KTU lab record should document the **step-by-step construction** of the joint you have performed. This section provides the **standard KTU-approved procedure** for each joint.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Construction of a T-Lap Joint

**Materials Required:**
- 2 pieces of softwood (Pine/Mahogany) — each **~150 mm long × 50 mm wide × 25 mm thick**
- Wood adhesive (Fevicol/PVA)
- Sandpaper

**Tools Required:**
- Try square, marking gauge, marking knife, tenon saw, firmer chisel, wooden mallet, jack plane, steel rule, pencil.

### Step 1 — Marking the Timber Dimensions

- Check that both timber pieces are **square** at the ends (use the try square against each face).
- Plane the surfaces smooth (called **"sizing"** the timber).

### Step 2 — Marking the Center Line of the T

- Take **Piece A** (the horizontal piece in the "T") and measure its **width** with the steel rule.
- On **one face** of Piece A, mark the **center line** lengthwise (a line passing through the center of the width). For a 50 mm wide piece, the center line is at **25 mm** from each edge.

$$\text{Center line position} = \frac{\text{Width}}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Marking the Notch Width

- Determine the **width of Piece B** (the vertical piece in the "T"). Assume it is **50 mm**.
- On Piece A, measure a distance of **50 mm** at the center of the board and mark the two **shoulder lines** (start and end of the notch) using a try square.

### Step 4 — Marking the Notch Depth (Half-Lap)

- Set the **marking gauge** to **half the thickness** of the timber:

$$\text{Notch depth} = \frac{t}{2} = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

- Scribe a line **all the way around** both faces and both edges of Piece A within the shoulder area.

### Step 5 — Cutting the Notch (Waste Removal)

- Place Piece A in the **bench vice** with the waste (notch) section facing up.
- Using a **tenon saw**, make a series of **relief cuts** inside the notch area — cuts about **3 mm apart** and **12.5 mm deep**.
- Hold the saw **vertically** and use light, guided strokes.
- After the relief cuts, use a **firmer chisel** (10 mm or 12 mm wide) and **wooden mallet** to chop out the waste cleanly.
- **Always chisel from the waste side toward the shoulder line** — never from the shoulder line into the waste, or you will split the shoulder.

### Step 6 — Repeating the Process for Piece B

- Repeat Steps 2–5 on Piece B to cut the matching notch.
- The two notches must have **identical dimensions** for a tight fit.

### Step 7 — Test Fitting (Dry Assembly)

- Slide the two pieces together. They should fit **snugly** with no gaps and no forcing.
- If the fit is too tight, **trim** with a sharp chisel. If too loose, re-cut.

### Step 8 — Gluing and Final Assembly

- Apply a **thin, even coat of wood adhesive (PVA)** to all four mating surfaces of the notches.
- Assemble the joint by sliding the two pieces together.
- **Wipe off excess glue** with a damp cloth.
- Clamp the joint using a **sash cramp** or by hand pressure for **~30 minutes** to **2 hours**.
- Allow **24 hours** for full curing.
- Lightly **sand** the joint to remove excess glue and create a flush surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Check — A "Good" T-Lap Joint:**
> 1. Both surfaces **flush and coplanar** (no high spots).
> 2. **No visible gaps** between mating surfaces.
> 3. Joint should **resist twisting** when force is applied.
> 4. Wood grain should be **continuous and aesthetic**.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction of a Cross Lap Joint

The procedure is **identical** to the T-Lap, except that **both pieces are oriented in the same plane**:

### Step 1 — Marking
- Mark the **center line** on **both** pieces (one lengthwise, one crosswise).
- Mark the **shoulder lines** at the center of each piece, equal to the width of the other piece.

### Step 2 — Setting Marking Gauge
- Set the marking gauge to **$t/2$** and scribe around the waste area.

### Step 3 — Cutting the Notches
- Cut and chisel out the notches as in T-Lap Steps 5–6.

### Step 4 — Assembly
- The two pieces slide together to form a **"+"** shape, both lying flat on a single plane.
- Glue, clamp, and finish as in T-Lap Step 8.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Construction of a Dovetail Joint

This is the **most complex** of the four joints. A **Through Dovetail** is constructed as follows:

**Materials:** 2 pieces of wood (~150 × 50 × 25 mm), one for the **"tail board"** and one for the **"pin board"**.

### Step 1 — Marking the Tail Board
- On the **end grain** of the tail board, mark the **dovetail angles** (1:6 slope).
- For a 1:6 slope on a 25 mm thick board:

$$\text{Side offset (rise)} = \frac{\text{Board width}}{6} = \frac{25}{6} \approx 4.17 \text{ mm}$$

- Mark the **dovetail tails** (trapezoids) and **pins** (spaces between tails) on the end grain. Typical proportions:
  - **Tail width (at base) = $t$** (e.g., 25 mm)
  - **Pin width (between tails) = $t/2$ to $t$** (e.g., 15–25 mm)
  - **Number of tails = 2 to 3** for a 50 mm wide board.

### Step 2 — Cutting the Tails
- Use a **tenon saw** to cut along the **angled lines** of each tail. Cut **down to the shoulder line** (the line marking the depth of the joint).
- Be **very careful** to keep the saw vertical.

### Step 3 — Removing the Waste
- Place the tail board in a **bench vice** with the waste between the tails facing up.
- Use a **coping saw** to remove the bulk of the waste between the tails.
- Use a **firmer chisel** to **clean up** the flat shoulders between the tails.

### Step 4 — Transferring the Tail Pattern to the Pin Board
- Place the tail board **on top of** the pin board, aligning the ends perfectly.
- Use a **marking knife** or sharp pencil to **trace the outline of the tails** onto the pin board's end grain.

### Step 5 — Cutting the Pins
- Using the **tenon saw**, cut along the traced lines **vertically** down to the shoulder.
- The pin board now has a series of **rectangular slots** (the spaces between the pins).

### Step 6 — Removing the Waste from the Pin Board
- Use a **coping saw** to remove the waste wood from the slots.
- Chisel clean the **half-pins** at each end (the half-width pins at the extreme ends of the pin board).

### Step 7 — Test Fitting
- Slide the tail board's tails into the pin board's slots. The dovetails should **wedge in tightly**.
- The mechanical lock should hold the two pieces together **without glue** — gravity alone is often enough to keep them engaged.

### Step 8 — Glue & Clamp
- Apply glue, assemble, and clamp. **Use a mallet to gently tap** the pieces fully home.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Dovetail):**
> Students often **revers**e the direction of the slope — i.e., the tails flare **outward** instead of **inward**. This makes the joint **fall apart** instead of locking. Always remember: the **wider end of the tail** is on the **outside** of the joint (the visible face), and the **narrow end** points **into** the joint.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Construction of a Mortise & Tenon Joint

**Materials:** 1 piece for the **"rail"** (the piece with the tenon) and 1 piece for the **"stile"** (the piece with the mortise).

### Step 1 — Marking the Tenon
- On the end of the rail piece, set the marking gauge to **$t/3$** of the timber thickness to mark the **tenon cheeks** (sides).
- Set the marking gauge to **$L_t$** (tenon length) to mark the **tenon shoulder** (around the rail).

$$\text{Tenon width} = \frac{t}{3} = \frac{25}{3} \approx 8.33 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Tenon length (length along grain)} = 2 \times W_t = 16.67 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2 — Cutting the Tenon Shoulders
- Use a **tenon saw** to cut along the **shoulder lines** all the way around the rail.
- The shoulder depth is **$t/3$** (the tenon thickness).

### Step 3 — Cutting the Tenon Cheeks
- Turn the rail on its side and saw down along the **cheek lines** to remove the waste on both sides of the tenon.
- This leaves a **rectangular tongue (tenon)** projecting from the end of the rail.

### Step 4 — Marking the Mortise
- On the **stile** (the other piece), mark the **mortise location** using a try square.
- The mortise dimensions should match the tenon exactly:
  - **Width = $W_t$**
  - **Length = $W_t$ (along the grain)**
  - **Depth = $L_t + 3$ mm** (extra 3 mm for glue space)

### Step 5 — Cutting the Mortise
- Mark the **two long sides** of the mortise with a marking gauge.
- Use a **mortise chisel** (or firmer chisel) and **mallet** to chop out the waste:
  1. Start from one end, chop halfway down the depth.
  2. Work toward the other end, progressively deepening the cuts.
  3. Turn the stile over and chop from the other side to **meet in the middle** — this prevents the wood from **splitting out** at the bottom.
- Keep the **chisel vertical** and the **flat back face of the chisel toward the finished wall** of the mortise.

### Step 6 — Test Fitting
- Insert the tenon into the mortise. The tenon should **slide in snugly**.
- The **shoulder** of the tenon should sit **flush** against the face of the stile.

### Step 7 — Glue & Clamp
- Apply glue to the tenon and the inside of the mortise.
- Assemble, clamp, and let cure for 24 hours.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

This table is the **single most important reference for KTU viva questions**:

| Parameter | T-Lap Joint | Cross Lap Joint | Dovetail Joint | Mortise & Tenon |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Configuration** | T-shape (different planes) | Cross shape (same plane) | Interlocking trapezoids | Rectangular tongue-in-cavity |
| **Notch Depth** | $t/2$ | $t/2$ | Variable (full $t$) | Mortise depth = $L_t + 3$ mm |
| **Strength Mechanism** | Glue + shoulder contact | Glue + shoulder contact | Mechanical interlock (angle) | Glue + shoulder + tenon fit |
| **Resists Pull-Apart** | Poor (relies on glue) | Poor (relies on glue) | **Excellent** (angle lock) | Good (glue + shoulder) |
| **Resists Bending** | Moderate | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Skill Level** | Beginner | Beginner | Advanced | Intermediate |
| **Common Use** | Wall framing | Trestles, scaffolding | Drawers, fine boxes | Door frames, chairs |
| **Visible on Both Sides?** | Yes (one side) | Yes (both) | Yes (through) or no (blind) | No (concealed inside) |
| **Glue Required?** | Yes | Yes | Optional (mechanical lock) | Yes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid diagrams** map the **geometric construction logic** and **functional architecture** of each joint. They are designed for **conceptual clarity**, not as engineering drawings.

## 4.1 Functional Flow — Generic Joint Construction Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Select Timber] --> B[Plane and Size Timber to Dimension]
    B --> C[Mark Reference Lines using Try Square and Marking Gauge]
    C --> D[Set Marking Gauge to Required Dimension]
    D --> E[Scribe Shoulder Lines and Cheek Lines]
    E --> F[Make Relief Cuts with Tenon Saw]
    F --> G[Chisel Out Waste with Mallet and Chisel]
    G --> H[Dry Test Fit both Pieces]
    H --> I{Fit Acceptable?}
    I -- No --> J[Trim High Spots with Chisel or Sandpaper]
    J --> H
    I -- Yes --> K[Apply Wood Adhesive PVA to Mating Surfaces]
    K --> L[Assemble Joint and Clamp for 30 min to 2 hrs]
    L --> M[Allow 24 hr Curing Time]
    M --> N[Sand and Finish the Joint]
    N --> O[End: Joint Complete]
```

## 4.2 T-Lap Joint — Geometric Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Piece A: Horizontal Member] --> A1[Notch of depth t/2]
    B[Piece B: Vertical Member] --> B1[Notch of depth t/2]
    A1 --> C[Interlocked T-Joint]
    B1 --> C
    C --> D[Flush Surface in Vertical Direction]
    C --> E[Shoulder Contact Provides Glue Surface]
    C --> F[Total Joint Height equals thickness of one piece]
```

## 4.3 Cross Lap Joint — Geometric Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Piece 1: Horizontal in Same Plane] --> N1[Notch at center, depth t/2]
    P2[Piece 2: Vertical in Same Plane] --> N2[Notch at center, depth t/2]
    N1 --> J[Cross Joint: Both pieces lie flat in same plane]
    N2 --> J
    J --> S1[Forms plus sign configuration]
    J --> S2[Four shoulder surfaces provide glue area]
    J --> S3[Resists in-plane shear forces]
```

## 4.4 Dovetail Joint — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TB[Tail Board: End grain has trapezoidal tails] --> TT[Tails cut at 1:6 slope]
    TT --> TC[Tails have wide base on outside face]
    TC --> T1[Tail 1: Wide trapezoid]
    TC --> T2[Tail 2: Wide trapezoid]
    PB[Pin Board: End grain has rectangular slots] --> PP[Pins cut as spaces between slots]
    PP --> P1[Pin 1: Rectangular space]
    PP --> P2[Pin 2: Rectangular space]
    T1 --> AS[Assembly: Tails slide into Pins from end]
    T2 --> AS
    P1 --> AS
    P2 --> AS
    AS --> LK[Mechanical Lock: 9.46 deg slope jams tails into pins]
    LK --> RS[Resists tensile pull-apart force along grain]
```

## 4.5 Mortise and Tenon Joint — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Rail Member: Has Tenon Projection] --> T1[Tenon: Rectangular tongue]
    T1 --> T2[Tenon Width equals one third of timber thickness]
    T1 --> T3[Tenon Length equals two to three times tenon width]
    S[Stile Member: Has Mortise Cavity] --> M1[Mortise: Rectangular hole]
    M1 --> M2[Mortise Width matches tenon width]
    M1 --> M3[Mortise Depth equals tenon length plus 3 mm glue space]
    T1 --> ASS[Assembly: Tenon slides into Mortise]
    M1 --> ASS
    ASS --> SH[Shoulder of tenon sits flush against stile face]
    SH --> ST[Joint resists bending and twisting forces]
```

## 4.6 Joint Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Need to Join Two Timber Members?] --> A1{What Angle?}
    A1 -- 90 deg in different planes --> TL[Use T-Lap Joint]
    A1 -- 90 deg in same plane --> CL[Use Cross Lap Joint]
    A1 -- End to end, no angle --> B1{Need high tensile strength?}
    B1 -- Yes --> DV[Use Dovetail Joint]
    B1 -- No, need frame connection --> B2{Need moment resistance?}
    B2 -- Yes --> MT[Use Mortise and Tenon Joint]
    B2 -- No --> SS[Use Simple Butt Joint with Nails or Screws]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a "lap joint" and explain why it is also called a "halving joint." List the two main types of lap joints.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **lap joint** is a type of carpentry joint in which two members overlap each other, with a portion of each member removed so that the two pieces fit flush at the joint. The depth of the removed portion is typically **half the thickness** of the timber, which is why the joint is also called a **"halving joint"** — the half-thickness of one piece "halves" with the half-thickness of the other.

The **two main types** of lap joints are:
1. **T-Lap Joint (Corner Halving Joint)** — used when members meet at right angles in different planes.
2. **Cross Lap Joint (Cross Halving Joint)** — used when members cross each other in the same plane at right angles.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for "halving" explanation, 1 for listing types.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Name any six carpentry tools and state one specific use for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Tool | Specific Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Try Square** | To test the squareness (90°) of timber edges and to mark perpendicular lines. |
| 2 | **Marking Gauge** | To scribe parallel lines along the grain of wood, e.g., for marking tenon shoulders. |
| 3 | **Tenon Saw** | To make precise straight cuts such as tenons, dovetails, and shoulder lines. |
| 4 | **Firmer Chisel** | To pare and chip away waste wood in joints, used with a wooden mallet. |
| 5 | **Wooden Mallet** | To strike chisels during cutting without damaging the chisel handle. |
| 6 | **Jack Plane** | To smooth rough timber surfaces to size and remove high spots. |

*(3 marks: 0.5 mark per correct tool + use.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an **internal choice**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question is divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **T-Lap Joint** (also called Corner Halving Joint). Explain the procedure for its construction, including the marking, cutting, and assembly stages.

**(b) [7 marks]** Differentiate between a **Cross Lap Joint** and a **T-Lap Joint** in terms of (i) plane of members, (ii) area of glue contact, and (iii) typical application. Also state two uses each of both joints.

#### **Model Answer for Q-A:**

**(a) [7 marks] T-Lap Joint Construction:**

The **T-Lap Joint** (Corner Halving Joint) is used to join two timber members at right angles in **different planes**, forming a "T" shape.

**Construction Procedure:**

1. **Selection and Sizing:** Two pieces of timber (each ~150 mm × 50 mm × 25 mm) are selected and planed smooth on all faces.

2. **Marking the Notch Width:** The width of the cross piece is marked on the main piece using a try square. The shoulder lines are drawn at the center of the main piece.

3. **Marking the Notch Depth:** The marking gauge is set to **half the thickness** of the timber:

$$D = \frac{t}{2} = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

4. **Scribing:** The gauge is used to scribe lines on **all four faces** of the timber in the waste area.

5. **Relief Cuts:** Using a tenon saw, a series of cuts ~3 mm apart are made in the waste area, each cut being **12.5 mm deep**.

6. **Waste Removal:** A firmer chisel and mallet are used to chop out the waste, working **from the waste side toward the shoulder line**.

7. **Repeating for the Second Piece:** The procedure is repeated on the cross piece so that both pieces have matching notches.

8. **Test Fitting:** The pieces are dry-fitted. A snug fit without gaps is achieved by trimming with a chisel if necessary.

9. **Gluing and Assembly:** PVA adhesive is applied to all mating surfaces, the joint is assembled, wiped clean, and clamped for 24 hours.

10. **Finishing:** The joint is sanded flush.

**Sketch:**
```
        ┌─────────────┐
        │             │   ←  Piece B (vertical)
        │             │
   ─────┼─────────────┼─────  ←  Piece A (horizontal)
   ┌────┘             └────┐
   │  Notch (12.5 mm)      │
   └───────────────────────┘
```

*(7 marks: 1 for sketch, 6 for procedure steps.)*

**(b) [7 marks] Difference between T-Lap and Cross Lap:**

| Parameter | T-Lap Joint | Cross Lap Joint |
|:---|:---|:---|
| (i) **Plane of Members** | Members lie in **different planes** (one vertical, one horizontal) | Members lie in the **same plane** (both flat) |
| (ii) **Area of Glue Contact** | Two shoulder surfaces only | **Four** shoulder surfaces (provides greater glue area) |
| (iii) **Typical Application** | Wall studs to plates, table rails to legs | Scaffolding cross-bracing, table trestles |

**Two Uses of T-Lap Joint:**
1. Connecting wall studs to horizontal plates in timber-framed buildings.
2. Joining cross-rails in wooden furniture such as tables and chairs.

**Two Uses of Cross Lap Joint:**
1. Cross-bracing in scaffolding and timber staging.
2. Joining the legs and stretchers of trestles and workbenches.

*(7 marks: 3 for table, 2 for T-Lap uses, 2 for Cross Lap uses.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Mortise and Tenon Joint** with a neat sketch. Describe the construction procedure and state the **standard proportions** used for the tenon.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Dovetail Joint** with a sketch. State **three advantages** and **two disadvantages** of the dovetail joint. Why is a 1:6 slope typically used?

#### **Model Answer for Q-B:**

**(a) [7 marks] Mortise and Tenon Joint:**

The **Mortise and Tenon Joint** is one of the **strongest and most common** carpentry joints, used to connect two timber members at right angles (typically a rail and a stile). It consists of a **rectangular projection (tenon)** on one piece that fits into a corresponding **rectangular cavity (mortise)** on the other piece.

**Standard Proportions of the Tenon (for timber of thickness $t$):**

$$\text{Tenon width} = W_t = \frac{t}{3}$$

$$\text{Tenon thickness} = T_t = \frac{t}{3} \text{ to } \frac{t}{2}$$

$$\text{Tenon length (along grain)} = L_t = 2 W_t \text{ to } 3 W_t$$

**Construction Procedure:**

1. Mark the tenon shoulders using a marking gauge set to $L_t$.
2. Mark the tenon cheeks using a marking gauge set to $W_t = t/3$.
3. Cut the shoulders with a tenon saw.
4. Cut the cheeks by sawing down from the shoulder to remove the waste on both sides.
5. On the stile, mark the mortise location and dimensions.
6. Chop out the mortise using a mortise chisel and mallet (working from both faces to prevent splitting).
7. Test-fit the tenon in the mortise; trim if necessary.
8. Apply glue, assemble, and clamp.

**Sketch:**
```
   ┌─────────────┐
   │   Stile     │   ←  Mortise (cavity) inside
   │   ┌─────┐   │
   │   │  M  │   │   ←  M = Mortise
   │   └─────┘   │
   └──────┬──────┘
   ┌──────┴──────┐   ←  Shoulder of tenon
   │   Tenon     │
   │   ┌─────┐   │
   │   │  T  │   │   ←  T = Tenon projection
   │   └─────┘   │
   │   Rail      │
   └─────────────┘
```

*(7 marks: 2 for sketch, 3 for proportions with formulas, 2 for procedure.)*

**(b) [7 marks] Dovetail Joint:**

The **Dovetail Joint** is a carpentry joint formed by interlocking **trapezoidal tails** and **rectangular pins**. The angled sides of the tails (typically with a **1:6 slope**) create a **mechanical interlock** that resists tensile pull-apart forces.

**Sketch:**
```
   Tail Board:                Pin Board:
   ┌─────────────┐           ┌─────────────┐
   │ \   /   \   /│           │             │
   │  \ /     \ / │           │  ┌──┐  ┌──┐ │
   │   V       V  │           │  │  │  │  │ │  ←  Pin slots
   │             │           │  └──┘  └──┘ │
   └─────────────┘           └─────────────┘
       ↓  Slide together  ↑
   ┌─────────────┐
   │  ╲   ╱  ╲   ╱│
   │   ╲ ╱    ╲ ╱ │
   │    V      V  │  ←  Interlocked dovetail
   │             │
   └─────────────┘
```

**Three Advantages:**
1. **Excellent tensile strength** — the angled dovetails mechanically lock the joint, preventing it from being pulled apart.
2. **Self-aligning** — the joint naturally aligns during assembly.
3. **No fasteners required** — the joint is held by geometry alone, even without glue.

**Two Disadvantages:**
1. **Difficult and time-consuming to construct** — requires precise marking, cutting, and fitting.
2. **Requires skilled craftsmanship** — improper angles lead to loose or jammed joints.

**Why 1:6 Slope?**
The **1:6 slope** is the **traditional standard** that provides the optimal balance between:
- **Joint strength:** A steeper angle would allow the joint to pull apart; a shallower angle would jam during assembly.
- **Ease of assembly:** The slope produces an angle of:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{6}\right) \approx 9.46°$$

This angle is steep enough to slide the tails into the pins easily, yet shallow enough that the **mechanical interlock** is so strong that the joint resists pull-apart forces. A 1:8 slope is also used for finer cabinetry where even greater mechanical advantage is desired.

*(7 marks: 2 for sketch, 3 for advantages + disadvantages, 2 for slope explanation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> **1. Confusing T-Lap with Cross Lap (–1 to 2 marks):**
> The most common error. **Remember:** T-Lap = "Tee shape in 3D" (members in different planes). Cross Lap = "Plus sign in 2D" (members in the same plane).
> 
> **2. Forgetting the "Half" in "Halving" (–1 mark):**
> Many students write the notch depth as "$t$" instead of "$t/2$". Always specify **half the thickness** for any lap/halving joint.
> 
> **3. Confusing Mortise with Tenon (–1 to 2 marks):**
> **Mortise = the HOLE (cavity).** **Tenon = the TONGUE (projection).** Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 
> **4. Missing the 1:3 Rule for Tenon Proportions (–1 mark):**
> Always state the tenon width as **$t/3$** of the timber thickness. Stating "small" or "appropriate" is not sufficient — give the **ratio**.
> 
> **5. Skipping Safety Procedures in Lab Records (–1 to 2 marks):**
> Always mention the **safety precautions** in your lab record:
> - Wear **safety goggles** while chiselling.
> - Keep **hands behind the blade** when sawing.
> - Use a **wooden mallet, not a steel hammer**, to strike chisels.
> - Ensure timber is **firmly clamped** in the vice before chiselling.
> - **Sharpen chisels** regularly; a blunt chisel is dangerous.
> 
> **6. Not Including the Sketch in Part B Answers (–2 to 3 marks):**
> A **neat, labelled sketch** is **mandatory** in KTU 2024 scheme answers. A purely textual answer, even if correct, will lose **2–3 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Tools You Must Recognize:**
- ✅ **Measuring/Marking:** Try square, marking gauge, steel rule, carpenter's square, bevel square, spirit level
- ✅ **Cutting:** Hand saw (cross-cut, rip), tenon saw, coping saw, firmer chisel, mortise chisel
- ✅ **Striking:** Wooden mallet, claw hammer
- ✅ **Smoothing:** Jack plane, smoothing plane, file, rasp

### **Joints You Must Know:**
- ✅ **T-Lap Joint** — half-thickness notches, members in different planes, "T" shape
- ✅ **Cross Lap Joint** — half-thickness notches, members in the same plane, "+" shape
- ✅ **Dovetail Joint** — interlocking trapezoidal tails and pins, 1:6 slope (~9.46°), strongest pull-apart resistance
- ✅ **Mortise & Tenon** — tenon (tongue) fits into mortise (cavity), tenon width = $t/3$

### **Key Formulas at a Glance:**
$$\text{Lap notch depth} = \frac{t}{2}$$
$$\text{Tenon width} = \frac{t}{3}$$
$$\text{Tenon length} = 2 \times \text{Tenon width to } 3 \times \text{Tenon width}$$
$$\text{Mortise depth} = \text{Tenon length} + 3 \text{ mm (glue space)}$$
$$\text{Dovetail angle} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{6}\right) \approx 9.46°$$

### **Quick Decision Guide — "Which Joint to Use?"**
- Members in **different planes** at 90°? → **T-Lap**
- Members in **same plane** at 90°? → **Cross Lap**
- Need **maximum pull-apart strength**? → **Dovetail**
- Need **rigid frame connection**? → **Mortise & Tenon**

### **Safety Mantra (Write in Every Lab Record):**
> *"A blunt tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Always clamp the work, always wear goggles, and always keep your hands behind the cutting edge."*

### **Final Tip for KTU Lab Exam:**
> [!TIP]
> In the **lab viva**, the examiner will likely:
> 1. Hand you a tool and ask you to **name it and state its use**.
> 2. Show you a finished joint and ask you to **identify the joint type**.
> 3. Ask **why you chose that notch depth / tenon proportion**.
> 4. Ask about **one safety precaution**.
> 
> Practice the **flashcard method** — write the tool name on one side and the use on the other, and revise before the exam. Memorize the four joint names and their **characteristic shape** (T, +, Trapezoid, Tongue-in-Hole).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foundry: Understanding of foundry tools and knowledge of at least one model: Bench Moulding, Floor Moulding, Core making, Pattern making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCESL106 - ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Foundry: Understanding of foundry tools and knowledge of at least one model: Bench Moulding, Floor Moulding, Core making, Pattern making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Foundry?

A **Foundry** is a manufacturing facility or workshop where metal castings are produced by pouring molten metal into a pre-prepared cavity of desired shape, allowing it to solidify, and then breaking the mould to retrieve the finished casting. Foundry operations represent one of the oldest (over 5000 years old) and most fundamental metal-forming processes in engineering, capable of producing complex shapes ranging from a few grams to several tonnes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (GCESL106):** Foundry is the science and art of casting metals into useful shapes by preparing a mould cavity, melting the metal, pouring it into the mould, and allowing it to solidify. The workshop practice aims at familiarizing the student with the basic foundry tools, pattern making, and moulding techniques (Bench, Floor, and Core).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Sand-Cookie" Model

Imagine making a **sand cookie** (a Jello mould) at home:
1. You take two halves of a shaped plastic cookie-cutter (the **Pattern**).
2. You press half of it into damp beach sand packed in a box (the **Mould**).
3. The cavity left in the sand is the exact negative of your desired shape.
4. You remove the cutter carefully, leaving a clean hollow.
5. You pour molten chocolate (the **Molten Metal**) into the cavity.
6. After it cools, you break the sand away and get your shaped chocolate piece (the **Casting**).

A foundry does the exact same thing — except with **foundry sand** and **molten metal (cast iron, aluminum, brass, etc.)** at temperatures of **1000°C to 1500°C**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology in Foundry Practice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Foundry Vocabulary (MUST remember for KTU viva & exams):**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pattern** | A replica of the final casting, slightly larger to account for shrinkage, used to shape the mould cavity |
| **Mould** | The cavity made of sand (or other refractory material) that receives the molten metal |
| **Cope** | The **upper half** of the mould (the "lid" half) |
| **Drag** | The **lower half** of the mould (the "base" half) |
| **Core** | A separate sand shape placed inside the mould to create internal cavities or hollow features in the casting |
| **Core Print** | A small projection on the pattern that creates a seat (recess) in the mould to hold the core in position |
| **Sprue** | The vertical channel through which molten metal enters the mould cavity |
| **Runner** | The horizontal channel that distributes molten metal from the sprue to the mould cavity |
| **Riser** | An extra reservoir of molten metal that feeds the casting as it shrinks during solidification |
| **Parting Line** | The line that separates the cope and drag (the "seam" of the mould) |
| **Gates** | The actual entry points from the runner into the mould cavity |
| **Pouring** | The act of transferring molten metal into the mould cavity |
| **Fettling** | The cleaning and finishing of the final casting (removing gates, risers, sand) |
| **Bench Moulding** | Small-scale moulding done on a wooden/steel bench (castings up to ~50 kg) |
| **Floor Moulding** | Large-scale moulding done on the foundry floor (castings of several tonnes) |

## 1.4 Scope of Foundry Shop for KTU Practical

In the **Engineering Workshop (GCESL106)** course, the KTU 2024 Scheme requires every student to:
- Identify and demonstrate the use of **all major foundry hand tools**.
- Prepare at least **one sand mould** using one of the techniques: **Bench Moulding, Floor Moulding, or Core Making**.
- Understand the role of **Patterns** in moulding.

> [!TIP]
> The KTU lab exam typically asks students to (a) name a tool shown in the figure, (b) describe the steps of bench moulding, and (c) explain pattern allowances. A clear understanding of the **moulding sand composition** is also a frequent short-answer question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Foundry Hand Tools — The Complete KTU Inventory

Foundry hand tools are categorized based on the operation they perform. The KTU lab exam requires identification of each tool, so each is described below with its function and grip.

### 2.1.1 Moulding Tools (For preparing the sand mould)

**1. Shovel (or Spade)**
- A wide-bladed, slightly curved tool used to **transfer and mix** foundry sand with additives (clay, water, coal dust).
- Also used to fill the moulding box with sand.
- *Analogy:* Like a kitchen spatula used to mix cake batter.

**2. Riddle (or Sand Screen)**
- A **wire-mesh sieve** mounted on a wooden frame.
- Used to **screen the sand** to remove foreign particles, lumps, and metal scraps.
- Mesh size: typically **4 to 8 meshes per cm²**.
- *Analogy:* Like a flour sifter in a bakery.

**3. Rammer (or Peen Rammer)**
- A dual-ended wooden/metal tool.
- **Butt end (flat):** Used to pack/ram the sand firmly in the moulding box.
- **Peen end (wedge-shaped):** Used to pack sand into corners, edges, and around the pattern.
- *Analogy:* Like a potato masher — flat end for the surface, pointy end for the edges.

**4. Strike-off Bar (or Strike Bar / Straight Edge)**
- A flat metal bar with a sharp straight edge.
- Used to **scrape off the excess sand** above the moulding box, making the surface perfectly flat.
- *Analogy:* Like a carpenter's straight edge used to level plaster.

**5. Slick (or Smoother / Spatula)**
- A small, polished, spoon-shaped or flat tool.
- Used to **smooth the inside of the mould cavity** and repair any damage to the mould surface.
- *Analogy:* Like a baker's icing spatula for smooth finishing.

**6. Lifter (or Draw Lifter)**
- A flat, hook-shaped metal tool.
- Used to **lift and remove (draw) the pattern** from the mould without damaging the cavity.
- The pattern is gently tapped with the mallet to loosen it, then the lifter is inserted at the parting line to lift it.
- *Analogy:* Like a cake server used to lift a cake from a pan.

**7. Vent Wire (or Vent Rod)**
- A thin, sharp, pointed steel wire (with a wooden handle).
- Used to **make small vent holes** in the mould to allow gases and steam to escape during pouring.
- *Analogy:* Like poking holes in pastry dough before baking.

**8. Trowel**
- A small flat metal blade with a handle.
- Used to **smooth, patch, and repair** the mould surface and edges.

**9. Sprue Cutter / Sprue Pin**
- A tapered wooden or metal pin used to form the **sprue hole** (downgate) in the cope.

**10. Gate Cutter / Gate Pin**
- A small tapered pin used to cut the **runner and gate channels** in the mould.

**11. Bellows / Hand Blower**
- Used to blow loose sand particles and dust from the mould cavity before assembly.

### 2.1.2 Melting & Pouring Tools

| Tool | Function |
|---|---|
| **Crucible** | A refractory container (graphite/clay) to hold and melt metal |
| **Furnace** (Tilting / Cupola) | Source of heat to melt metal |
| **Tongs / Shank** | Long-handled tongs to lift the crucible safely |
| **Ladle** | A larger pouring vessel for transferring molten metal |
| **Skimmer** | To remove slag and dross from molten metal surface |

### 2.1.3 Moulding Boxes (Flasks)

- A **moulding box** (or flask) is a rigid frame — usually in two halves — that contains and supports the moulding sand.
- Made of **wood, cast iron, or aluminum**.
- The top half is the **cope**; the bottom half is the **drag**.
- Held together with **pins (locating dowels)** and **clamps**.

## 2.2 Composition of Moulding Sand

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Note:** The composition of moulding sand is a guaranteed 3-mark question in almost every KTU workshop viva. Memorize the percentage ranges!

Moulding sand is not just "sand" — it is a carefully engineered mixture:

| Constituent | Composition (%) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Silica Sand (SiO₂)** | 80 – 90% | Refractory base — provides strength and heat resistance |
| **Clay (Bentonite)** | 5 – 15% | Binder — gives green strength and plasticity when moistened |
| **Water** | 2 – 8% | Activates clay and provides moisture for bonding |
| **Coal Dust / Sea Coal** | 2 – 6% | Improves surface finish, prevents burn-on, creates reducing atmosphere |
| **Additives** (dextrin, molasses, silica flour) | 0 – 5% | Special properties — improve hot strength, collapsibility |

## 2.3 Properties of Moulding Sand

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Properties — Examiner's favourite 3-mark topic:**

| Property | Definition | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Refractoriness** | Ability to withstand high temperatures without melting or fusing | Sand must survive molten metal (up to 1500°C) |
| **Permeability** | Ability to allow gases and steam to escape through the mould | Prevents gas holes and blowholes in the casting |
| **Green Strength** | Strength of the moist (un-dried) mould to resist erosion | Allows the mould to be handled and metal poured safely |
| **Dry Strength** | Strength of the oven-dried mould | Important for dry-sand moulds |
| **Cohesiveness / Plasticity** | Ability to be moulded into any shape and retain it | Helps the sand take the pattern's shape |
| **Adhesiveness** | Ability of sand grains to stick to each other | Keeps the mould intact during handling |
| **Collapsibility** | Ability of the mould to collapse after solidification | Prevents hot tears/cracks in the cooling casting |
| **Flowability** | Ability of sand to flow and pack around intricate patterns | Ensures sharp reproduction of details |

## 2.4 Pattern Making — The Heart of Foundry Practice

A **Pattern** is a wooden, metal, or plastic replica of the casting to be made. It is the "master mould" that creates the cavity.

### 2.4.1 Functions of a Pattern

1. Forms the mould cavity of the desired shape.
2. Provides the **parting line** location.
3. Carries the **core prints** for core placement.
4. Incorporates allowances for shrinkage, draft, and machining.

### 2.4.2 Types of Patterns

| Pattern Type | Description | When used |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Piece / Solid Pattern** | One solid piece; mould is split after ramming | Very simple shapes |
| **Split Pattern** | Pattern is split at the parting line, made in two halves | Most common for symmetrical castings |
| **Multi-piece / Follow Board Pattern** | Pattern with a base board (follow board) for support | Complex or asymmetrical shapes |
| **Match Plate Pattern** | Cope and drag halves mounted on a single plate | Mass production |
| **Cope and Drag Pattern** | Two separate patterns, one for each half | Larger castings |
| **Skeleton Pattern** | A frame outlining the shape; sand packed inside | Large, simple castings (floor moulding) |
| **Sweep Pattern** | A wooden template (sweep) revolved/rotated to shape the mould | Circular/rotational shapes |
| **Loose Piece Pattern** | Pattern has a detachable part to allow withdrawal | Patterns with overhangs/recesses |
| **Gated Pattern** | Multiple patterns connected via a common gating system | Small castings in bulk |

### 2.4.3 Pattern Materials

| Material | Use case |
|---|---|
| **Wood** (Pine, Mahogany, Teak) | Most common; cheap, easy to work, single/low production |
| **Metal** (Aluminum, Cast Iron, Brass) | High production, long life, accurate |
| **Plastics / Resins** | Master patterns; limited life |
| **Plaster of Paris** | Prototype patterns only |
| **Wax** | Lost-wax (investment) casting |

### 2.4.4 Pattern Allowances (THE MOST IMPORTANT CONCEPT!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Hot-Topic:** Pattern Allowances — the **shrinkage allowance** is the most frequently asked numerical/diagrammatic question.

Since the pattern must produce a casting of **exact final size**, but the metal **shrinks** on cooling, has to be **machined**, must be **drafted** for easy removal, and is **distorted** by ramming, the pattern is made **larger** than the casting at several stages. These extra dimensions are called **allowances**.

| Allowance | Reason | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Shrinkage (Contraction) Allowance** | Metal shrinks on cooling | Cast Iron: **1%**, Steel: **2%**, Aluminum: **1.25%**, Brass: **1.5%** |
| **2. Draft (Taper) Allowance** | Easy withdrawal of pattern from sand | **1° to 3°** (≈ 2 mm to 5 mm per 100 mm height) |
| **3. Machining (Finish) Allowance** | Extra material for machining surfaces | **2 mm to 5 mm** depending on size |
| **4. Rapping (Shake) Allowance** | Extra material because pattern is rapped to loosen it | **0.5 mm to 1 mm** on each side |
| **5. Camber (Cambering) Allowance** | Curvature given to long horizontal castings to counter sagging | Few mm per meter |
| **6. Distortion Allowance** | Y/I/U-shaped castings distort on cooling | Pre-shaped inversely |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Shrinkage Allowance Calculation

The single most important numerical relationship in pattern making:

$$
L_{pattern} = L_{casting} \times (1 + S)
$$

Where:
- $L_{pattern}$ = required pattern dimension
- $L_{casting}$ = required casting (final) dimension
- $S$ = shrinkage allowance as a decimal (e.g., 0.01 for 1%)

**Mass of Pattern (approximate):** The mass of metal needed for a casting (ignoring allowances) is:

$$
M_{casting} = \rho_{metal} \times V_{casting}
$$

Where $\rho_{metal}$ is the metal density in **g/cm³** (Cast Iron ≈ **7.2**, Steel ≈ **7.85**, Aluminum ≈ **2.7**, Brass ≈ **8.5**).

## 2.6 Core Making

A **Core** is a pre-shaped sand mass placed inside the mould cavity to create **internal hollows, holes, passages, or undercut features** in the casting. Cores are made separately, baked in an oven (200°C – 300°C), and then placed in the mould using **core prints**.

### 2.6.1 Core Sand Composition

- Silica sand + binder (oil — linseed oil, or resin) + catalyst + special additives.
- Core sand must have **high dry strength** (since it is baked) and **good collapsibility** (so it can be removed as dust after casting).
- Binders: **Linseed oil, Core oil, Resins (Phenolic, Furan), Dextrin, Sodium silicate (CO₂ process)**.

### 2.6.2 The CO₂ Core Process (KTU Favorite)

A modern technique:
1. Mix silica sand with **sodium silicate (Na₂SiO₃)** binder (~3–6%).
2. Ram into a **core box**.
3. Pass **CO₂ gas** through the sand for ~30 seconds.
4. The CO₂ reacts with sodium silicate to form **silica gel**, hardening the core instantly.
5. The core is removed from the box and placed in the mould.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage:** Fast setting (seconds), no oven needed, good dimensional accuracy, and the core is strong enough to handle.

### 2.6.3 Steps in Core Making (Conventional)

1. Prepare the **core box** (wooden/metal box with the internal shape of the desired core).
2. Coat the inside of the core box with **release agent** (chalk or parting dust) for easy removal.
3. Fill the core box with **core sand**.
4. **Ram** the sand firmly using a hand rammer or air rammer.
5. Vent the core by pricking with a vent wire.
6. **Open the core box** carefully and remove the green core.
7. **Bake the core** in a core oven at 200°C – 300°C for several hours to harden it.
8. Store in a dry place until use.

### 2.6.4 Properties of a Good Core

- Sufficient **strength** to handle and resist metal pressure.
- **Permeable** to allow gases to escape.
- **Collapsible** after casting so it can be removed.
- **Refractory** to survive the molten metal.
- **Dimensionally accurate**.
