# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six fitting tools and state the specific purpose of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Tool | Purpose |
|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | Try Square | To check and scribe lines at 90° (right angle) |
| 2 | Scriber | To mark fine reference lines on metal surfaces |
| 3 | Hacksaw | To cut metal sections into required sizes |
| 4 | Flat File (Second Cut) | To remove material and size flat surfaces |
| 5 | Vernier Caliper | To measure OD, ID, and depth with 0.02 mm accuracy |
| 6 | Center Punch | To mark hole centers and create starting indentations for drilling |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Bastard file and a Smooth file. When is each used?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Bastard File | Smooth File |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Teeth per cm** | Coarse (4–6) | Fine (10–14) |
| **Material removal rate** | High (rough filing) | Low (finishing) |
| **Surface finish produced** | Rough | Smooth |
| **Application** | Bulk material removal in initial stages | Final sizing and finishing |

**Usage Rule:**
- **Bastard file** → Used when the gap between current size and target size is more than **0.5 mm**.
- **Smooth file** → Used when the gap is between **0.05 mm and 0.1 mm** (final sizing).

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Model Question Paper]**
**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working principle of a **square joint** used in fitting practice. List the tools required. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain step-by-step the procedure for preparing a **square joint model** as per KTU workshop standards. Mention the tolerance values. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of a Square Joint (7 Marks):**

A square joint consists of two mild steel flat workpieces joined at a right angle. The first piece (base plate) has a **rectangular slot** (also called a mortise or groove) of dimensions **25 mm × 12 mm** (length × width) and depth **12 mm**. The second piece (top plate) has a matching **rectangular projection (tongue/tenon)** of the same dimensions.

**Construction Features:**
- **Material**: Mild Steel flat, **100 mm × 25 mm × 12 mm** (two pieces).
- **Joint angle**: **90°** between the mating plates.
- **Fit type**: **Slip fit** with **0.05 mm to 0.1 mm** clearance per side.
- **Slot relief**: Achieved by drilling a **10–11 mm** diameter hole inside the slot to ease chip removal.

**Working Principle:**
The tongue slides into the slot and is held in place by **frictional contact** along the four side walls and the bottom. Once assembled, the joint acts as a rigid **L-shaped bracket** suitable for mounting, framing, or supporting other components.

**Tools Required:**
Try square, scriber, vernier height gauge, vernier caliper, hacksaw (18 TPI), flat file, square file, second-cut file, smooth file, center punch, ball pein hammer, drill machine, surface plate, V-block.

**(b) Step-by-Step Procedure (7 Marks):**

1. **Stock Preparation**: Cut two MS flats to **100 mm × 25 mm × 12 mm** using a hacksaw. File the ends square. Remove mill scale using emery cloth.

2. **Marking the Base Plate (with slot)**:
   - Place on the surface plate.
   - Using the vernier height gauge, scribe the **centerline** along the length.
   - Mark a **25 mm × 12 mm** rectangle at one end, with the long edge along the centerline.
   - Mark a center cross at the midpoint of the slot for drilling.
   - Punch dot marks at the four corners of the rectangle.

3. **Drilling Relief Hole**:
   - Clamp the base plate in a bench vice.
   - Drill a **10 mm** hole at the center of the slot to a depth of **12.5 mm** (slightly deeper than slot).

4. **Rough Cutting the Slot**:
   - Use a hacksaw to cut two parallel slots **2 mm inside** the scribed lines.
   - Use a cold chisel (held at 30°) to remove the central web between the saw cuts.
   - Leave **0.2 mm** for final filing.

5. **Filing the Slot**:
   - Use a **second-cut flat file** to bring the slot close to the scribed lines.
   - Use a **square file** for the side walls.
   - Switch to a **smooth file** for final sizing.
   - **Tolerance**: Slot dimensions **25 mm + 0.1 mm** (length) and **12 mm + 0.05 mm** (width).

6. **Marking the Tongue**:
   - On the second piece, scribe the tongue outline **25 mm × 12 mm**.
   - Mark the depth lines on all four sides.

7. **Cutting and Filing the Tongue**:
   - Hacksaw just outside the scribed lines.
   - File the tongue down to the exact scribed dimensions using a flat file.
   - Apply a slight **draft angle of 0.5°** on the two side faces for easy insertion.

8. **Trial Assembly**:
   - Slide the tongue into the slot by hand.
   - It should enter with **light finger pressure** (slip fit).

9. **Squareness Check**:
   - Use a try square on the assembled joint to confirm **90°** $\pm 0.5°$.

10. **Final Finishing**:
    - Deburr all edges.
    - Polish contact surfaces with **220-grit emery cloth**.
    - Apply a thin coat of **machine oil** to prevent rust.

**Tolerance Summary:**
- Dimensional tolerance: $\pm 0.1$ mm
- Angular tolerance: $\pm 0.5°$
- Surface roughness: $Ra \leq 1.6$ µm

---

### Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the geometry of a **male–female (dovetail) fitting**. Derive the relationship between the top width, bottom width, flank angle, and depth. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A dovetail joint has a bottom width of **20 mm**, depth **15 mm**, and flank angle **12°**. Calculate the top width of the male part. Also, state the fit clearance values for workshop-level acceptance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Geometry of the Male–Female (Dovetail) Fitting (7 Marks):**

The male part has a **trapezoidal cross-section** that is wider at the top (or outer end) and narrower at the bottom (or inner end). The female slot has a matching inverted trapezoid. This geometry creates a **mechanical interlock** – the male part cannot be pulled out in the axial direction without first being slid sideways.

**Key Parameters:**
- $W_1$ = Width at the root (narrow end) of the male part
- $W_2$ = Width at the tip (wide end) of the male part
- $d$ = Depth of the joint
- $\alpha$ = Flank angle (angle of the inclined side with the vertical)
- $c$ = Clearance per side (typically 0.05–0.1 mm)

**Diagrammatic Representation:**

```
              W₂ (top, wider)
             ___________
            /     |     \
           /      | d    \   ← α (flank angle)
          /       |       \
         /________|________\
              W₁ (bottom, narrower)
```

**Derivation of the Formula:**

By considering the right triangle formed by one flank, the depth, and the horizontal projection:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan(\alpha) &= \frac{\text{horizontal projection of one flank}}{\text{depth}} \\[6pt]
\text{Horizontal projection} &= d \cdot \tan(\alpha) \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}$$

The top width equals the bottom width plus twice the horizontal projection:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_2 &= W_1 + 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan(\alpha) \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}$$

For the **female slot**, the dimensions are:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{1,\text{female}} &= W_1 + 2c \\[6pt]
W_{2,\text{female}} &= W_2 + 2c \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}$$

Where $c$ is the clearance per side.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $W_1 = 20$ mm
- $d = 15$ mm
- $\alpha = 12°$
- Clearance $c = 0.1$ mm per side (assumed)

**To Find:** Top width $W_2$

**Step 1: Compute the top width of the male part**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_2 &= W_1 + 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan(\alpha) \\[6pt]
W_2 &= 20 + 2 \cdot 15 \cdot \tan(12°) \\[6pt]
W_2 &= 20 + 30 \cdot 0.2126 \\[6pt]
W_2 &= 20 + 6.378 \\[6pt]
W_2 &= 26.38 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute the female slot dimensions**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{1,\text{female}} &= W_1 + 2c = 20 + 0.2 = 20.2 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
W_{2,\text{female}} &= W_2 + 2c = 26.38 + 0.2 = 26.58 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Workshop Acceptance Criteria**

| Parameter | Tolerance |
|-----------|-----------|
| Dimensional accuracy | $\pm 0.1$ mm |
| Flank angle accuracy | $\pm 0.5°$ |
| Clearance per side | $0.05$ mm to $0.1$ mm |
| Surface finish | $Ra \leq 1.6$ µm |
| Pull-out test | Must resist 50 N axial force |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Errors**: Always use consistent units. If $\alpha$ is in degrees, ensure your calculator is in **degree mode** (not radians). A common mistake is using $\tan(12 \text{ rad})$ instead of $\tan(12°)$, giving wildly incorrect answers.
> 2. **Forgetting the "× 2"**: The formula $W_2 = W_1 + 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ has a factor of **2** because the flank angle is present on **both sides** of the dovetail. Students often write $W_2 = W_1 + d \cdot \tan(\alpha)$, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the female slot dimensions**: The question often asks for both male and female dimensions. If you calculate only $W_2$, you lose 2 marks.
> 4. **No sketch**: Always include a labeled diagram. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for a neat sketch.
> 5. **Ignoring clearance**: Workshop joints always have a **clearance fit**, not an interference fit. Mention the $0.05$–$0.1$ mm clearance explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Fitting (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

### Tools
- ✅ **Marking tools**: Scriber, Try square, Bevel protractor, Vernier height gauge, Surface plate.
- ✅ **Workholding**: Bench vice (jaw width 100/125/150 mm), V-block.
- ✅ **Cutting**: Hacksaw (14/18/24/32 TPI for soft/mild/thin/very-thin stock), Cold chisel (60° edge), Cape chisel (narrow edge).
- ✅ **Filing**: Bastard → Second cut → Smooth → Dead smooth. Use triangular file for V-grooves, square file for slots, round file for holes.
- ✅ **Striking**: Ball pein hammer, Center punch (90°), Dot punch (60°), Prick punch (30°).
- ✅ **Measuring**: Vernier caliper (**0.02 mm**), Micrometer (**0.01 mm**), Feeler gauge (**0.05–1.0 mm**).

### Models
- ✅ **Square Joint**: Tongue-and-slot at **90°**, slip fit, tolerance $\pm 0.1$ mm.
- ✅ **V-Joint**: V-groove (typically **90°** included angle) for self-centering cylindrical parts.
- ✅ **Male–Female (Dovetail)**: Trapezoidal geometry, formula $W_2 = W_1 + 2d\tan(\alpha)$, mechanical interlock.

### Key Numbers to Memorize
- Try square accuracy: **0.02 mm per 100 mm**
- Vernier caliper least count: **0.02 mm**
- Micrometer least count: **0.01 mm**
- Hacksaw blade lengths: **250 mm / 300 mm**
- Standard TPI for MS: **18 TPI**
- Center punch angle: **90°**
- Cold chisel edge angle: **60°**
- Dovetail flank angle range: **5°–15°**
- Workshop tolerance: $\pm 0.1$ mm
- Workshop surface finish: $Ra \leq 1.6$ µm

### Safety Reminders
- ⚠️ **Never use a file without a handle** – the tang can puncture your palm.
- ⚠️ **Always deburr the mushroomed head** of chisels and punches.
- ⚠️ **Wear safety goggles** during chiseling and hacksawing.
- ⚠️ **Secure the workpiece firmly** in the vice before cutting.
- ⚠️ **Keep the work area clean** – metal chips can cause serious cuts.

### Common Exam Traps
- ❌ Confusing **bastard file** (coarse) with **smooth file** (fine).
- ❌ Forgetting the **factor of 2** in the dovetail formula.
- ❌ Using a **rounded-edge chisel** instead of a **sharpened** one.
- ❌ Marking on a **dirty surface** – the scriber line will be inaccurate.
- ❌ Not performing the **trial assembly** before final finishing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plumbing: Understanding plumbing tools and pipe joints, along with practicing one exercise on joining pipes using a minimum of three types of pipe joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCESL106 - ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Plumbing: Understanding plumbing tools and pipe joints, along with practicing one exercise on joining pipes using a minimum of three types of pipe joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Plumbing? (KTU Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plumbing** is the system of pipes, fixtures, fittings, valves, and other apparatus installed in a building for the distribution of water for drinking, heating, washing, and the removal of waterborne wastes. It is derived from the Latin word *plumbum* (meaning **lead**), since the Romans originally used lead pipes for water conveyance.

In the context of the **KTU GCESL106 Engineering Workshop**, plumbing refers to the practical art and science of **cutting, threading, bending, assembling, and joining pipes** (metallic and non-metallic) to form a continuous, leak-proof conduit for fluid transport.

### 1.2 What is a Pipe Joint?

> [!NOTE]
> A **Pipe Joint** is a mechanical or chemical connection point between two pipe sections, or between a pipe and a fitting (such as an elbow, tee, or valve), that ensures structural continuity, fluid-tightness, and mechanical strength under operating pressure and temperature.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train Coupling" Model

Think of pipes as the **cars of a train** and pipe joints as the **couplers between those cars**.

- Just as train cars need **different types of couplers** for passenger trains, freight trains, and high-speed bullet trains, pipes need **different types of joints** depending on:
  - The **material** of the pipe (GI, copper, PVC, CPVC, HDPE).
  - The **fluid** being carried (water, gas, oil, steam).
  - The **pressure** rating required (domestic vs. industrial).
  - Whether the joint needs to be **permanent** or **disassemblable**.

For example, a **threaded joint** is like a bolted coupler (you can undo it with tools), while a **welded joint** is like a permanently fused coupler — extremely strong but you cannot take it apart without cutting.

### 1.4 Classification of Pipe Joints (Master Overview)

| Category | Joint Type | Disassembly | Pipe Material | Typical Use |
|----------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Threaded / Screwed** | BSP / NPT threads | Yes (reusable) | GI, CI, MS | Domestic water lines |
| **Welded** | Arc / Gas welding | No (permanent) | MS, SS, CI | Industrial / High-pressure |
| **Soldered / Brazed** | Capillary fill with filler metal | No (permanent) | Copper, brass | Refrigeration, water heaters |
| **Flanged** | Bolted flanges with gasket | Yes (reusable) | GI, CI, MS, SS | Boiler lines, large diameter |
| **Compression** | Olive/ferrule compression | Yes | Copper, plastic | Household taps, small fittings |
| **Socket / Cement** | Solvent welding | No (permanent) | PVC, CPVC | Drainage, cold water |
| **Union** | Threaded union nut | Yes (easy) | GI, brass | Pump connections, valves |
| **Collar / Pop-Rivet** | Internal sleeve + rivets | No | Aluminium, GI | Sheet metal ducting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Requirement**: The student must practice **at least THREE distinct types of pipe joints** on pipes of appropriate materials. Common combinations chosen are: **(a) Threaded GI joint + (b) Soldered copper joint + (c) PVC solvent-cement joint**, OR **(a) Threaded + (b) Welded + (c) Flanged** for MS pipes.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Tool & Joint Sheet

## 2.1 Plumbing Tools — The Engineering Workshop Arsenal

### 2.1.1 Pipe Wrenches (Stillson Type)
- **Function**: To grip and rotate round objects (pipes, rods, fittings).
- **Working Principle**: The jaw has **angled teeth (typically 45°)** that bite into the pipe as torque is applied. The harder you pull, the tighter it grips.
- **Standard Sizes**: 250 mm, 300 mm, 450 mm, 600 mm (10″, 12″, 18″, 24″).

### 2.1.2 Pipe Vice (Bench Vice for Pipes)
- **Function**: To hold the pipe rigidly while cutting, threading, or fitting.
- **Construction**: Two V-shaped jaws mounted on a heavy cast base, tightened via a screw.

### 2.1.3 Pipe Cutter (Hinged Wheel Cutter)
- **Function**: To make a clean, square cut on a pipe.
- **Components**: A cutting wheel, two guide rollers, and an adjusting screw (handle).
- **Advantage over Hacksaw**: Produces a **square, burr-free cut** with minimal material wastage.

### 2.1.4 Pipe Threading Die Set (Ratchet Type)
- **Function**: To cut **BSP (British Standard Pipe)** or **NPT (National Pipe Thread)** taper threads on the outside of a pipe.
- **Components**: Die head (with cutting teeth), die stock (handle), ratchet mechanism.

### 2.1.5 Hacksaw Frame and Blades
- **Function**: For rough cutting of pipes when a pipe cutter is unavailable.
- **Blade Specification**: 24 TPI (teeth per inch) for thin-wall pipes; 18 TPI for thick-wall MS/GI pipes.

### 2.1.6 Reamer / Deburring Tool
- **Function**: To remove **internal burrs** and **external burrs** produced after cutting.
- **Why critical**: Burrs cause **turbulence**, **pressure loss**, and **flow restriction** inside the pipe.

### 2.1.7 Pipe Bending Machine (Hydraulic / Spring Type)
- **Function**: To bend pipes to a specific angle (typically 45° or 90°) without kinking.
- **Methods**: Cold bending with internal mandrel, or hot bending for thick pipes.

### 2.1.8 Soldering Iron / Blow Torch
- **Function**: To heat copper joints for solder/brazing operations.
- **Fuel**: LPG (liquefied petroleum gas) for portable workshop use.

### 2.1.9 Plumb Bob and Spirit Level
- **Function**: For checking **verticality** and **horizontal alignment** of installed pipe runs.

### 2.1.10 Measuring and Marking Tools
- Steel rule, measuring tape, try square, scriber, chalk, marker.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Tool & Joint Cheat Sheet

| # | Tool / Joint | Primary Use | Pipe Material | KTU Exam Focus |
|---|-------------|-------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Stillson Pipe Wrench** | Gripping & rotating pipes | All metals | Identify parts & jaw angle |
| 2 | **Pipe Vice** | Holding pipes during work | All | Vice vs. Bench Vice difference |
| 3 | **Pipe Cutter** | Clean square cuts | GI, Copper, SS | Cutting wheel vs. hacksaw |
| 4 | **Threading Die Set** | Cutting external taper threads | GI, MS, Brass | BSP vs. NPT distinction |
| 5 | **Reamer** | Removing internal burrs | All | Why reaming is mandatory |
| 6 | **Hacksaw** | Rough pipe cutting | All | TPI selection logic |
| 7 | **Bending Spring / Machine** | Bending without kinking | Copper, GI | Cold vs. hot bending |
| 8 | **Blow Torch** | Heating for soldering/brazing | Copper, Brass | Flux application logic |
| 9 | **Spirit Level / Plumb Bob** | Alignment & alignment check | All | Levelling terminology |
| 10 | **Threaded Joint** | Permanent-reusable pipe connection | GI, MS | Pipe dope / Teflon tape role |
| 11 | **Soldered / Brazed Joint** | Capillary filler-metal joint | Copper, Brass | Solder (≤450 °C) vs. Brazing (>450 °C) |
| 12 | **Welded Joint (Arc / Gas)** | Fusion joint for high pressure | MS, SS, CI | Fillet vs. Butt weld in pipe |
| 13 | **Flanged Joint** | Bolted, gasket-sealed connection | GI, CI, MS | Flange rating: PN 6, PN 10, PN 16 |
| 14 | **Compression Joint** | Olive/ferrule seal under nut | Copper, Plastic | Why it's called a "fitting" joint |
| 15 | **PVC Solvent-Cement Joint** | Chemical fusion of plastic | PVC, CPVC | Primer + Cement = "welding" |
| 16 | **Union Joint** | Three-part detachable coupling | GI, Brass | Where used: pumps, valves |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Constants for Plumbing Practice**:
> - Standard water pressure in domestic lines: **1.5 to 4.0 bar** (≈ 1.5 × 10⁵ to 4.0 × 10⁵ Pa).
> - Soldering temperature: **200 °C to 450 °C** (soft solder: 60/40 Pb-Sn).
> - Brazing temperature: **450 °C to 900 °C** (brass brazing rod).
> - Standard GI pipe thread: **BSP – British Standard Pipe Thread** (ISO 228 / ISO 7).
> - 1 inch nominal pipe = **33.7 mm** outside diameter (medium series GI).

# 3. Step-by-Step Procedures, Specifications & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Three Mandatory Pipe Joints — Step-by-Step Practical Procedures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU workshop experiment mandates that a student **successfully executes and demonstrates at least three different pipe joints** on actual pipes. Below are the three most commonly evaluated joints: **Threaded GI Joint**, **Soldered Copper Joint**, and **PVC Solvent-Cement Joint**.

---

### 3.1.1 JOINT A — Threaded (Screwed) GI Pipe Joint

**Pipe Used**: ½″ (15 mm nominal) **Galvanized Iron (GI) Class-B** pipe, with a **socket/hex nipple or elbow** fitting.

#### Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1: Marking and Measuring**
1. Measure the required pipe length using a steel rule or measuring tape.
2. Mark the cut line with a marker and wrap a piece of paper around the pipe at the mark to get a **square reference line** (perpendicular to the pipe axis).

**Step 2: Securing in Pipe Vice**
1. Place the pipe horizontally in the pipe vice with the cut mark protruding about **100 mm** beyond the jaw.
2. Tighten the vice jaw firmly so the pipe does not slip.

**Step 3: Cutting the Pipe**
1. Open the pipe cutter, place it around the pipe, and tighten the knob lightly so the cutting wheel touches the mark.
2. Rotate the cutter around the pipe **one full turn**, then tighten the knob **¼ turn**.
3. Repeat until the pipe is cut through. (Typically 6–10 rotations for a 15 mm GI pipe.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative**: If a pipe cutter is unavailable, use a **hacksaw with a 24 TPI blade** in a steady horizontal motion. Apply cutting oil for lubrication.

**Step 4: Reaming (Deburring)**
1. Insert the reamer into the pipe end and rotate **clockwise 3–4 times**.
2. This removes the **internal burr** (the raised lip of metal pushed inward by the cutting wheel).
3. Use a flat file to remove the **external burr** on the outside of the pipe.

**Step 5: Threading the Pipe**
1. Mount the correct **BSP die head** (½″ size) in the ratchet die stock.
2. Apply **cutting oil** (e.g., lubrocating oil) liberally on the pipe end.
3. Engage the die head square onto the pipe end, applying firm forward pressure.
4. Rotate the stock **clockwise 1 to 1.5 turns**, then back off **¼ turn** to break the chip. Repeat.
5. Continue until about **12 mm to 15 mm of thread** protrudes from the die head (≈ 8 to 10 threads visible).
6. Unscrew the die, wipe clean, and inspect the thread.

**Step 6: Applying Sealing Compound**
1. Wrap **Teflon (PTFE) tape** **clockwise** around the male threads (2 to 3 wraps).
2. Alternatively, apply **pipe dope / jointing compound** evenly on the male thread.

> [!WARNING]
> Wrapping Teflon **anti-clockwise** will cause it to unravel and shred into the water line when the joint is tightened.

**Step 7: Assembling the Joint**
1. Screw the fitting (elbow / socket) onto the pipe by hand until finger-tight.
2. Place the **pipe wrench** on the pipe end and the **back-up wrench** on the fitting hex.
3. Tighten by rotating the fitting **1 to 2 full turns** beyond hand-tight.
4. Wipe off excess dope. The joint is complete.

**Step 8: Testing for Leaks**
1. Connect the assembly to a **hydrostatic test pump** or workshop water supply at **3 bar** for 5 minutes.
2. Visually inspect all joint interfaces for **sweating, drips, or bubbles** (if submerged in a water trough).

---

### 3.1.2 JOINT B — Soldered (Sweated) Copper Pipe Joint

**Pipe Used**: 15 mm or 22 mm **copper tube (DHP – Drawn High-Purity)** and a **copper socket/capillary fitting**.

#### Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1: Cutting**
1. Cut the copper tube squarely with a **wheel-type pipe cutter** (preferred, as it does not deform the soft copper).
2. **Deburr** the inside and outside edges with a reamer or half-round file.

**Step 2: Cleaning (Critical Step)**
1. Use **emery cloth / sandpaper (120 grit)** to polish the **outside** of the tube end for a length equal to the socket depth of the fitting (≈ 15–20 mm).
2. Polish the **inside** of the fitting socket similarly with a **fitting brush** or emery cloth.
3. The surfaces must appear **bright and shiny** — any oxide or dirt will prevent solder adhesion.

**Step 3: Applying Flux**
1. Apply a thin, even coat of **plumbing flux (zinc-chloride or rosin-based)** to the cleaned tube end AND inside the fitting.
2. Flux prevents re-oxidation during heating and helps solder flow.

**Step 4: Assembly**
1. Push the tube firmly into the fitting socket, giving it a slight twist to distribute the flux.
2. The joint should be **dry-assembled** (no gap between tube end and socket shoulder).

**Step 5: Heating**
1. Light the **blow torch** and adjust to a **neutral flame** (blue inner cone, no yellow tips).
2. Heat the **fitting body** (not the tube) — the fitting has more mass, so the heat must conduct from the fitting to melt the solder at the tube-fillet interface.
3. Keep the flame moving to avoid overheating one spot.

**Step 6: Applying Solder**
1. Touch the **lead-free solder wire** (95/5 tin-antimony or 97/3 tin-copper) to the **fillet** (the 90° corner where the tube meets the socket).
2. If the temperature is correct, the solder will **melt and be drawn into the joint by capillary action**.
3. Continue feeding solder until a **full ring of solder appears** around the entire fillet.
4. Remove heat and hold the joint still for 10 seconds until the solder solidifies (turns dull/matte).

> [!NOTE]
> The **capillary action** pulls molten solder into the tiny gap (0.04–0.20 mm) between the tube and socket. This is why the joint is called a **sweated joint**.

**Step 7: Cleaning and Cooling**
1. Wipe the joint with a **damp rag** while still warm (to remove residual flux — flux is corrosive and will eat the copper if not cleaned).
2. Allow the joint to cool naturally. **Do not quench with water** (causes thermal shock cracks).

**Step 8: Testing**
1. Test the soldered assembly at **1.5 × working pressure** (typically 6 bar for domestic copper water lines) for 10 minutes.

---

### 3.1.3 JOINT C — PVC Solvent-Cement Joint (Socket Weld)

**Pipe Used**: 20 mm or 25 mm **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride) Class-4 pressure pipe** and a **PVC elbow / tee socket fitting**.

#### Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1: Cutting**
1. Cut the PVC pipe squarely with a **hacksaw** (use a miter box for perpendicularity) or a **plastic pipe cutter**.

**Step 2: Deburring and Chamfering**
1. Use a **half-round file or deburring tool** to remove the **internal burr** and to create a **10°–15° external chamfer** on the pipe end.
2. The chamfer allows the pipe to slide into the socket without scraping or pushing cement out.

**Step 3: Dry Fit (Trial Assembly)**
1. Insert the pipe into the fitting socket **without cement**.
2. It should slide in **⅔ of the depth** with hand pressure only. If it bottoms out, the pipe or fitting is defective.

**Step 4: Cleaning**
1. Wipe both the outside of the pipe end and the inside of the socket with a **clean dry rag** to remove dust, grease, and moisture.

**Step 5: Applying Primer**
1. Use the **purple PVC primer** (a mixture of MEK/acetone/Tetrahydrofuran) on a dauber.
2. Apply a generous coat to the **outside of the pipe end** and the **inside of the socket**.
3. The primer **softens and chemically etches** the PVC surface, preparing it for solvent welding.

**Step 6: Applying Solvent Cement**
1. Use **PVC solvent cement** of the correct grade (regular body for 25 mm pipes; medium body for 50 mm; heavy body for 100 mm+).
2. Apply a **heavy, even coat** to the **pipe end** first, then a **lighter coat** to the **inside of the socket**.
3. Work **fast** — the cement begins to set in 30 seconds.

> [!WARNING]
> The **pipe end gets more cement** because it has a larger surface area. The socket gets less to prevent the cement from being scraped off as the pipe enters.

**Step 7: Joining**
1. Immediately push the pipe into the socket with a **¼ turn** (a slight twisting motion) and hold it firmly for **30 seconds** to prevent the pipe from backing out (the tapered socket can create a spring-back effect).
2. A **bead of cement** should be visible all around the fillet — this is your visual proof of a full joint.

**Step 8: Curing**
1. Do **NOT disturb** the joint for **2 minutes** (handling time) for small pipes.
2. Wait **24 hours** before pressure testing (full cure time depends on pipe size and ambient temperature).

**Step 9: Testing**
1. Hydrostatically test at the rated working pressure (typically **10 bar** for Class-4 PVC) for 1 hour.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Joint Selection Logic)

| Parameter | Threaded GI | Soldered Copper | Soldered Copper | PVC Solvent-Cement | Welded MS | Flanged GI |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------------|-----------|------------|
| **Pipe Material** | GI / MS | Copper / Brass | Copper / Brass | PVC / CPVC | MS / SS / CI | GI / CI / MS |
| **Joint Type** | Mechanical (screw) | Chemical (capillary) | Chemical (capillary) | Chemical (solvent) | Metallurgical (fusion) | Mechanical (bolt + gasket) |
| **Disassembly** | ✔ Yes | ✗ No | ✗ No | ✗ No | ✗ No | ✔ Yes |
| **Pressure Rating** | Up to 10 bar | Up to 16 bar | Up to 25 bar | Up to 16 bar | > 100 bar | Any (depends on flange) |
| **Temperature Limit** | 120 °C | 110 °C | 200 °C (brazed) | 60 °C (PVC) / 93 °C (CPVC) | 400 °C+ | 200 °C+ |
| **Tools Required** | Wrench, die, vice | Torch, emery, flux | Torch, emery, flux | Cement, dauber, hacksaw | Arc/Gas welding set | Spanner, gasket, bolts |
| **Skill Level** | Beginner | Intermediate | Intermediate | Beginner | Advanced | Intermediate |
| **Typical Application** | Domestic water | Refrigeration, water heater | Gas lines, industrial | Cold water, drainage | Boiler, process plants | Pump suction, large dia. |
| **KTU Workshop Practice** | ✔ Mandatory | ✔ Mandatory | Optional | ✔ Mandatory | Optional | Optional |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Examinations**: "**T-W-S-P**" — the four most frequently asked joint categories in KTU: **T**hreaded, **W**elded, **S**oldered, **P**VC-cement.
