## 4.4 Process Flow — Strike, Weld, and Inspect a Bead

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[PPE CHECK HELMET GLOVES APRON BOOTS] --> s2[VERIFY MACHINE AND EARTHING]
    s2 --> s3[CLEAN WORKPIECE WIRE BRUSH GRIND]
    s3 --> s4[FIX WORKPIECE IN VISE AT DESIRED POSITION]
    s4 --> s5[INSERT ELECTRODE IN HOLDER]
    s5 --> s6[STRIKE ARC SCRATCH METHOD OR TAP METHOD]
    s6 --> s7[ESTABLISH MOLTEN POOL]
    s7 --> s8[MAINTAIN ARC LENGTH 2 TO 4 MM]
    s8 --> s9[TRAVEL ALONG JOINT AT CONSISTENT SPEED]
    s9 --> s10[OBSERVE BEAD UNIFORMITY AND SHAPE]
    s10 --> s11[COMPLETE BEAD STOP ARC]
    s11 --> s12[CHIP SLAG WITH CHIPPING HAMMER]
    s12 --> s13[WIRE BRUSH BEAD SURFACE]
    s13 --> s14[VISUAL INSPECTION FOR DEFECTS]
    s14 --> s15[ACCEPT OR REWORK]
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram — Arc Striking Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    arcStrike[ARC INITIATION TECHNIQUES] --> scratchMethod[SCRATCH METHOD]
    arcStrike --> tapMethod[TAP METHOD]

    scratchMethod --> sm1[DRAG ELECTRODE LIKE A MATCH ON PLATE]
    sm1 --> sm2[ARC IGNITES WHEN CONTACT LIFTS]
    sm2 --> sm3[USED BY BEGINNERS EASIER]

    tapMethod --> tm1[TAP ELECTRODE PERPENDICULAR ON PLATE]
    tm1 --> tm2[QUICKLY LIFT 2 TO 4 MM]
    tm2 --> tm3[ARC IGNITES ON LIFT]
    tm3 --> tm4[USED IN TIGHT SPACES]
```

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Block — Common Weld Defects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    defects[COMMON WELD DEFECTS IN SMAW] --> porosity[POROSITY GAS POCKETS]
    defects --> undercut[UNDERCUT GROOVE AT TOE]
    defects --> slagInc[SLAG INCLUSION]
    defects --> spatter[SPATTER]
    defects --> cracs[CRACKS HOT AND COLD]
    defects --> lackPen[LACK OF PENETRATION]
    defects --> lackFusion[LACK OF FUSION]
    defects --> distortion[DISTORTION AND WARPAGE]
    porosity --> p1cause[CAUSE DIRTY PLATE OR DAMP ELECTRODE]
    undercut --> u1cause[CAUSE EXCESS CURRENT OR LONG ARC]
    slagInc --> s1cause[CAUSE INCOMPLEDE SLAG REMOVAL]
    cracs --> c1cause[CAUSE HIGH HEAT INPUT OR HYDROGEN]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**"With a neat sketch, list the main components of a Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) workstation."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The main components of an SMAW workstation are:

1. **Welding Power Source** — Welding transformer / rectifier / inverter that converts mains supply to low-voltage, high-current welding output.
2. **Electrode Holder (Stinger)** — Insulated grip that holds the consumable electrode at the desired angle.
3. **Electrode (Consumable)** — Flux-coated mild-steel rod (e.g., E 6013, 3.15 mm) that acts as both electrode and filler metal.
4. **Workpiece (Base Metal)** — The mild-steel plates to be joined.
5. **Cables** — Electrode cable (positive) and work cable with ground clamp (negative).
6. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — Welding helmet (auto-darkening), leather gloves, apron, and safety boots.
7. **Auxiliary Tools** — Wire brush, chipping hammer, clamps, fire extinguisher, and fume extractor.

> **Mark split:** 1 mark for naming components, 1 mark for explanation of any 3, 1 mark for neat sketch.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`
**"What is the function of the flux coating on a mild-steel welding electrode? Name any two types of flux coatings."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The flux coating performs **four primary functions**:

1. **Arc stabilization** — Ionizing agents (e.g., potassium titanate) maintain a steady arc.
2. **Shielding gas generation** — Cellulose decomposes to CO and H₂, displacing atmospheric O₂ and N₂ from the weld pool.
3. **Slag formation** — Oxides (SiO₂, TiO₂, MnO) melt and form a liquid cover that protects the cooling weld.
4. **Alloying and deoxidation** — Fe-Mn and Fe-Si add Mn and Si to the weld metal, improving strength and removing dissolved oxygen.

**Two types of flux coatings:**
- **Cellulosic (e.g., E 6010)** — High hydrogen, deep penetration, used for pipe welding.
- **Rutile (e.g., E 6013)** — Titanium dioxide based, smooth arc, all-position, easy slag removal.
- *(Low-hydrogen / Basic — e.g., E 7018 — also acceptable for the third mark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B** (both from the same module). Each has two sub-parts of **7 marks** each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **four positions of welding** (flat, horizontal, vertical, and overhead) as per AWS classification. State the effect of gravity on the weld pool in each position. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A welder uses a **3.15 mm E 6013 electrode** to deposit a stringer bead on a **6 mm mild-steel plate** at a current of **120 A** and arc voltage of **22 V**. The travel speed is **120 mm/min**. The arc efficiency is **0.80**. Calculate:
- (i) Arc power
- (ii) Heat input in kJ/mm
- (iii) Heat input for a bead length of 150 mm
**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### (a) Four Welding Positions (7 marks)

**Flat Position (1F / 1G)** — The workpiece is horizontal, the weld is made on the **upper surface**. Gravity is **favourable** — the molten metal flows down into the joint, giving good fusion. The electrode is held at **70°–80°** to the plate with a **15° lead angle** (push / forehand technique). The **highest welding current** can be used. Easiest position — minimum skill required. Used in workshops where workpieces can be rolled.

**Horizontal Position (2F / 2G)** — The weld axis is **vertical** but the bead is made on a **vertical face**. Gravity is **adverse** — the molten pool tends to sag downward, causing **undercut on the top bead** and **overlap on the bottom**. The electrode is held at **80°–90°** with a slight uphill tilt. **Current is reduced by 5–10%** compared to flat. A slight weaving motion controls the sag. Used for welding vertical plate seams in storage tanks.

**Vertical Position (3F / 3G)** — The plate is vertical and the bead travels **upward** (uphill technique). Gravity is **highly adverse**. The electrode is held at **90°** with a **15°–20° uphill lead angle**. A **triangular weave pattern** is used to maintain a uniform bead. **Current is reduced by 15–20%**. Downhill (3F-down) is sometimes used for thin sheets with faster travel, but is less common.

**Overhead Position (4F / 4G)** — The bead is deposited on the **underside** of a horizontal plate. Gravity is **most adverse** — the molten pool tends to **fall**. The electrode is held at **80°–90°** with a **very short arc length (1–2 mm)**. The **current is reduced by 20–25%** compared to flat. A **fast travel speed** with minimal weaving is essential. Maximum skill is required. Used in ship hulls, ceilings, and pipe joints that cannot be rotated.

**Neat Sketches (must be drawn in exam):**
- Show each position with the **plate orientation arrow** and the **electrode angle**.
- Label **AWS code (1F, 2F, 3F, 4F)** clearly.

> **Mark split:** 1.5 marks each × 4 positions = 6 marks + 1 mark for AWS codes.

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $d_e = 3.15$ mm
- $V_a = 22$ V
- $I = 120$ A
- $S = 120$ mm/min
- $\eta = 0.80$
- $L_{bead} = 150$ mm

**(i) Arc Power (2 marks):**

$$
E_{arc} = V_a \times I = 22 \times 120 = 2640 \text{ W} = 2.64 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Heat Input (3 marks):**

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V_a \cdot I}{S \times 1000} = \frac{0.80 \times 22 \times 120}{120 \times 1000}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
HI = \frac{0.80 \times 22}{1000} = \frac{17.6}{1000} = 0.0176 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

**(iii) Heat Input for 150 mm bead (2 marks):**

$$
Q_{total} = HI \times L_{bead} = 0.0176 \times 150 = 2.64 \text{ kJ}
$$

> **Mark split:** Correct formula = 1 mark each sub-part, substitution = 0.5, final answer with units = 0.5.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a)** Describe the **principle of operation of Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)**. With a neat diagram, explain the **three regions of an electric arc** and the effect of **DCEP and DCEN polarity** on heat distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List **eight common weld defects** observed in SMAW. For **any three**, state the **cause** and the **preventive measure**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### (a) Principle of SMAW (7 marks)

**Principle:**
In SMAW, an **electric arc** is struck between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** (held in the electrode holder) and the **workpiece**. The intense heat of the arc (6000–20000 °C) **melts both the electrode tip and the surface of the base metal**, forming a common **molten weld pool**. As the electrode is consumed, its **molten droplets are transferred across the arc** into the pool, supplying filler metal. The **flux coating decomposes** to produce:
- A **gaseous shield** (CO, CO₂, H₂) that displaces atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen.
- A **molten slag** (oxides of Si, Ti, Mn, Al) that floats on the pool, then solidifies over the bead.

As the arc is moved along the joint, the pool **solidifies behind**, forming a continuous **metallurgically bonded weld bead**.

**Three Regions of the Arc (with sketch):**

1. **Anode Region** — Receives electrons from the cathode. The anode fall voltage is small, but it is a region of intense ion bombardment, so it gets hot.
2. **Plasma / Arc Column** — The bright, hot, **ionized gas column** between the electrodes. Has the **highest temperature** of all three regions. Conducts current by ions and electrons.
3. **Cathode Region** — The source of electrons (thermionic emission). Also very hot. Cathode fall voltage is higher than anode fall.

**DCEP vs DCEN Polarity:**

| Polarity | Electrode | Workpiece | Heat Distribution | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DCEP (Reverse Polarity)** | + (Anode) | − (Cathode) | **2/3 heat at electrode** → faster electrode melting, **less penetration** | Surfacing, hardfacing, welding thin sheets |
| **DCEN (Straight Polarity)** | − (Cathode) | + (Anode) | **2/3 heat at workpiece** → **deeper penetration**, less electrode melting | Thick plates, stainless steel, high-deposition work |

> **Mark split:** Principle 2 marks, three regions 2 marks, polarity comparison 2 marks, sketch 1 mark.

#### (b) Eight Common Weld Defects (7 marks)

1. **Porosity** — Small cavities/gas pockets in the weld.
2. **Undercut** — Groove melted at the weld toe but not filled.
3. **Slag Inclusion** — Solid slag trapped inside the weld.
4. **Spatter** — Droplets of molten metal ejected from the arc.
5. **Cracks (Hot and Cold)** — Longitudinal, transverse, crater, or HAZ cracks.
6. **Lack of Penetration** — Weld metal does not extend through the joint thickness.
7. **Lack of Fusion** — Filler metal does not fuse with the base metal.
8. **Distortion** — Warping of the workpiece due to uneven heating/cooling.

**Three Defects with Cause and Prevention (4 marks — 1.5 marks each):**

| Defect | Cause | Prevention |
|---|---|---|
| **Porosity** | Dirty or rusty plate, damp electrode, long arc | Clean plate, dry electrodes (store in oven at 100–150 °C), short arc |
| **Undercut** | Excessive current, wrong electrode angle, long arc, fast travel | Reduce current, use proper angle, maintain short arc, slow travel slightly |
| **Slag Inclusion** | Incomplete slag removal between passes, shallow groove, fast travel | Chip and wire-brush slag between passes, use proper groove angle, control travel speed |

> **Mark split:** 8 defects listed = 4 marks (0.5 each); 3 detailed explanations = 3 marks (1 each for cause + prevention).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in KTU welding questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "arc efficiency" in heat-input formula.** Many students write $HI = \frac{V \cdot I}{S \times 1000}$ and **lose 1–2 marks**. The correct formula **must** include $\eta$.
>
> 2. **Confusing DCEP and DCEN.** "Reverse polarity" = DCEP (electrode +). "Straight polarity" = DCEN (electrode −). Mixing these up = 0 marks on the polarity question.
>
> 3. **AWS code writing wrong.** KTU expects **1F, 2F, 3F, 4F** for **fillet welds** and **1G, 2G, 3G, 4G** for **groove welds**. Writing just "flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead" without the AWS code is **incomplete**.
>
> 4. **Units in numerical answers.** Always write **kJ/mm** for heat input — students often forget to divide by 1000 and write J/mm. Examiners **penalize wrong units**.
>
> 5. **Heat-input numerical magnitude.** Industrial realistic heat input for SMAW is **0.5–1.5 kJ/mm**. If your answer comes out as **0.01 kJ/mm**, re-check your unit conversion — you may have missed the **1000** divisor.
>
> 6. **Skipping the sketch.** "Neat sketch" is **mandatory** for position questions. A **text-only answer** loses **at least 2 marks** out of 7.
>
> 7. **Polarity questions require a table or a comparison** — bullet points alone are insufficient. Use the format "**DCEP**: electrode +, 2/3 heat at electrode, less penetration."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module Experiment 2**

### **Core Concepts to Memorize**
- [ ] **Welding** = permanent joining by **coalescence** of metal, with or without filler.
- [ ] **SMAW** = Shielded Metal Arc Welding = "stick welding" = arc between **flux-coated consumable electrode** and workpiece.
- [ ] Arc temperature = **6000 to 20000 °C**.
- [ ] **Four functions of flux coating** = (1) arc stabilization, (2) shielding gas, (3) slag formation, (4) alloying / deoxidation.
- [ ] **Electrode classification (E 6013 example):** E = Electrode, 60 = 60 ksi tensile, 1 = all-position, 3 = rutile coating, AC/DC.
- [ ] **Second digit rule:** **1 = all positions**, **2 = flat/horizontal only**, **3 = flat only**.

### **Equipment Checklist**
- [ ] Welding power source (Transformer / Rectifier / Inverter)
- [ ] Electrode holder (stinger)
- [ ] Flux-coated electrode
- [ ] Work cable + ground clamp
- [ ] PPE: helmet, gloves, apron, boots
- [ ] Wire brush, chipping hammer, clamps, fire extinguisher

### **Four Welding Positions — Must Memorize**
- [ ] **1F (Flat)** — gravity favourable, 70°–80° electrode angle, highest current.
- [ ] **2F (Horizontal)** — gravity adverse, 80°–90° angle, **5–10% lower** current.
- [ ] **3F (Vertical Up)** — gravity highly adverse, 90° angle, **15–20% lower** current, triangular weave.
- [ ] **4F (Overhead)** — gravity most adverse, 80°–90° + short arc, **20–25% lower** current, fast travel.

### **Critical Formulas (write 5 times before the exam)**
- [ ] $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V_a \cdot I}{S \times 1000}$ → kJ/mm
- [ ] $E_{arc} = V_a \cdot I$ → Watts
- [ ] $R_{dep} = I \cdot k_d$ → g/min
- [ ] $S = \dfrac{L_{bead}}{t_{weld}}$ → mm/min
- [ ] $BSF = \dfrac{W}{D}$ → ideal range **1.3 to 2.0**

### **Polarity Facts (common exam trap)**
- [ ] **DCEP** = Electrode + = "reverse polarity" = **2/3 heat at electrode**.
- [ ] **DCEN** = Electrode − = "straight polarity" = **2/3 heat at workpiece** = **deeper penetration**.

### **Defects — Top 5 to Remember**
- [ ] **Porosity** → cause: dirty/damp, prevent: clean + dry.
- [ ] **Undercut** → cause: high current/long arc, prevent: reduce current.
- [ ] **Slag inclusion** → cause: incomplete cleaning, prevent: chip and brush between passes.
- [ ] **Lack of penetration** → cause: low current, fast travel, prevent: increase current, slow down.
- [ ] **Cracks** → cause: high heat input, hydrogen, prevent: preheat, low-hydrogen electrodes.

### **Safety — Always Mentioned in KTU Viva**
- [ ] Always wear **auto-darkening helmet (shade 10–13)**.
- [ ] Earth the machine and workpiece properly.
- [ ] Keep a **Class ABC fire extinguisher** within 5 metres.
- [ ] **Ventilation / fume extractor** is mandatory — welding fumes (especially from stainless steel) are carcinogenic.
- [ ] Never look at the arc with the naked eye — **arc flash** causes permanent retinal damage.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In the **practical record**, always include a **neat labelled sketch** of the welding setup, a **photograph of the actual bead** (if permitted), and a **defect observation table**. Examiners reward **observation-based answers** over rote text.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rolling: Objective of rolling, rolling process, practical on two high rolling mill
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> **Rolling** is a metal forming process in which the **workpiece (stock)** is passed through one or more pairs of **rotating cylindrical rolls** to reduce its **thickness, change its cross-section, and/or impart desired mechanical properties**. It is classified as a **bulk deformation (compressive)** process governed by the principles of plastic deformation under compressive loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — GCESL106 (Engineering Workshop) Core Concept:**
> Rolling is the most widely used metal forming process in industry. Approximately **90% of all steel products** (sheets, plates, rails, I-beams, channels) are produced by rolling at some stage of their manufacture.

### 1.1.1 Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Initial Thickness | $h_0$ | Stock thickness before entering the rolls |
| Final Thickness | $h_1$ | Stock thickness after exiting the rolls |
| Draft (Reduction) | $\Delta h$ | $h_0 - h_1$ |
| Roll Radius | $R$ | Radius of the working rolls |
| Roll Velocity | $v_r$ | Tangential (surface) velocity of the rolls |
| Exit Velocity | $v_f$ | Velocity of the strip leaving the rolls |
| Coefficient of Friction | $\mu$ | Between roll surface and workpiece |
| Angle of Bite / Contact | $\alpha$ | Half the angle subtended by the arc of contact |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chapati Press" Intuition

Imagine you are flattening dough (chapati) using a rolling pin (belan) on a kitchen slab:

1. You place a thick ball of dough on the slab.
2. You press the rolling pin down and push it forward. The pin **rotates** while moving — the dough gets **squeezed** between the pin and the hard slab.
3. Each pass makes the chapati **thinner, wider, and longer** (volume is roughly conserved — no dough is lost).
4. After many passes, you get a thin, flat, uniform sheet.

> 🍞 **The rolling mill is the industrial "chapati press":**
> - **Rolling pin** = the cylindrical **rolls** (just bigger, harder, and motorized)
> - **Dough ball** = the **metal stock/billet/slab**
> - **Hard slab** = the **backing roll** (or a second working roll)
> - **Forward-and-back passes** = **multiple rolling passes** in industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** The metal is *not* cut or removed — it is **squeezed (compressed)** between two counter-rotating rolls. Material flows **outward (laterally)** to conserve volume, making the workpiece longer and sometimes wider.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Roll Metals? — Objectives of Rolling

Rolling is performed in industry for **four major engineering objectives**:

1. **Shape Conversion (Geometric Objective)**
   To change the **cross-sectional shape** of a metal piece. Example: a square billet → a flat plate, a round bloom → a structural I-beam, an ingot → a thin sheet.

2. **Size Reduction (Dimensional Objective)**
   To reduce the **thickness (and cross-sectional area)** of the workpiece uniformly across its length, while increasing its length — known as **drafting** or **reduction**.

3. **Mechanical Property Improvement (Metallurgical Objective)**
   Rolling imparts **grain refinement** and introduces a **fibrous, directional grain structure**. This substantially increases the **strength, hardness, and toughness** of the metal in the rolling direction (a phenomenon called **work hardening** or **strain hardening**).

4. **Surface Finish and Tolerance (Quality Objective)**
   Rolling (especially in **finishing mills** and **skin-pass mills**) gives the metal a **smooth, uniform surface finish** with **tight dimensional tolerances** suitable for automotive body panels, appliance housings, etc.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook — "S-S-S-Q":** Rolling does **S**hape, **S**ize reduction, **S**trengthening, and **Q**uality finishing.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Rolling Mills (Overview)

Rolling mills are classified by the **number and arrangement of rolls**:

| Mill Type | Roll Count | Typical Use |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Two-High Mill** | 2 rolls | Initial breakdown, laboratory/educational use |
| Three-High Mill | 3 rolls | Repetitive bidirectional rolling (reversing stands) |
| Four-High Mill | 4 (2 work + 2 backup) | Thin sheets, flatness control |
| Cluster / Sendzimir Mill | 12–20 rolls | Foils, very thin strips, high-strength steels |
| Continuous / Tandem Mill | 4–6 stands in series | Mass production of sheets and strips |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the GCESL106 practical, the focus is the Two-High Rolling Mill.** You are expected to identify its components, sketch the setup, perform a small reduction, and record measurements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rolling Process — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The rolling sequence is best understood by tracking a small element of metal as it moves from **inlet → deformation zone → exit**.

### Step 1: Entry of the Stock
- The stock of initial thickness $h_0$ approaches the **roll gap** (also called the **bite** or **roll gap**).
- The rolls rotate **towards each other** at the entry side (drawing the metal in by friction).

### Step 2: Bite and Engulfment
- The stock makes initial contact with the rolls at an angle $\alpha$ (the **angle of bite** or **contact angle**).
- For the rolls to "**bite**" the stock, the **frictional force must be greater than the horizontal component of the compressive reaction**.
- The **condition for successful biting** is:
$$\mu \geq \tan \alpha$$
- If friction is insufficient, the rolls simply **slide over the stock** and no reduction occurs.

### Step 3: Plastic Deformation in the Arc of Contact
- Between the entry and the **neutral point (no-slip point)**, the surface of the roll moves **faster** than the metal (slip is *backward* relative to the roll).
- Between the **neutral point** and the exit, the metal moves **faster** than the roll surface (slip is *forward*).
- The metal is **compressed and plastically deformed** throughout the contact arc.

### Step 4: Exit
- The stock leaves the rolls at thickness $h_1 < h_0$.
- The exit velocity $v_f$ is **greater** than the roll surface velocity $v_r$ due to volume constancy and the forward slip phenomenon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Volume Constancy (Incompressibility of Plastic Flow):**
> The volume of metal entering per second equals the volume leaving per second:
> $$h_0 \cdot w \cdot v_{\text{in}} = h_1 \cdot w \cdot v_{\text{out}}$$
> Since $h_0 > h_1$, the exit velocity $v_f$ must be greater than the entry velocity. This is the origin of **forward slip**.

---

## 2.2 Geometric Analysis of the Deformation Zone

Consider a single roll with radius $R$ acting on stock from $h_0$ to $h_1$:

### 2.2.1 Projected Contact Length ($L$)
By dropping a perpendicular from the roll center to the entry/exit points and applying the Pythagorean theorem:

$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{R \cdot (h_0 - h_1)}$$

This $L$ is the **horizontal projection of the arc of contact**, and is treated as the effective length over which the roll pressure acts on the stock.

### 2.2.2 Angle of Bite ($\alpha$)
From the same geometry:

$$\cos \alpha = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R} = \frac{R - (h_0 - h_1)/2}{R}$$

For small reductions, $\alpha$ is small (typically $3°$–$15°$), and the approximation $\cos \alpha \approx 1$ becomes useful.

### 2.2.3 Maximum Permissible Draft
Since the rolls must be able to **bite** the stock, the **angle of bite must be less than the friction angle** ($\alpha < \beta$, where $\tan \beta = \mu$):

$$\cos \alpha_{\max} = 1 - \frac{\Delta h_{\max}}{R} = 1 - \sin \beta$$

For small angles, this reduces approximately to:
$$\Delta h_{\max} \approx \mu^2 \cdot R$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the roll radius **doubles** the maximum reduction you can take in a single pass. This is why **backup rolls** are used in four-high mills to support small-diameter work rolls.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Rolling (Cheat Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Draft (Reduction) | $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$ | mm | Always positive |
| 2 | Percentage Reduction | $\%\text{Red} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{h_0} \times 100$ | % | Common in quality control |
| 3 | Projected Contact Length | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | mm | Used in force calculation |
| 4 | Angle of Bite (cosine form) | $\cos \alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{R}$ | dimensionless | $\alpha$ in radians/degrees |
| 5 | Bite Condition | $\mu \geq \tan \alpha$ | dimensionless | Otherwise no rolling |
| 6 | Max. Permissible Draft | $\Delta h_{\max} \approx \mu^2 R$ | mm | For small $\alpha$ |
| 7 | Roll Separating Force | $F = p \cdot L \cdot w$ | N (Newton) | $p$ = mean specific roll pressure, $w$ = width |
| 8 | Torque per Roll | $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$ | N·mm | Lever arm $\approx L/2$ |
| 9 | Total Rolling Power | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W (Watts) | $N$ = roll rpm |
| 10 | Forward Slip | $S = \dfrac{v_f - v_r}{v_r}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.02–0.10 |
| 11 | Neutral Angle | $\sin \beta \approx \beta \approx 0.5 \alpha$ | rad | Approximate relation |
| 12 | Volume Constancy | $h_0 v_0 = h_1 v_1$ | — | Incompressible flow |
| 13 | True Strain (Rolling) | $\varepsilon = \ln \left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_1}\right)$ | dimensionless | Logarithmic strain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention for KTU Board Exam:** Use **mm** for length, **N** for force, **N/mm²** (= MPa) for pressure, **N·mm** for torque, **W** (or **kW**) for power. Always state units. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for missing units.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel Plants (TATA Steel, JSW, SAIL):** Hot strip mills roll 200 mm slabs into 2 mm coils at speeds up to **25 m/s** using tandem stands.
- **Aluminium Industry (HINDALCO, NALCO):** Cold rolling produces beverage can stock and aircraft sheet with tolerances of $\pm 0.01$ mm.
- **Railway Tracks:** Rails are rolled from blooms in **universal rolling mills** to a precise I-section profile.
- **Structural Sections:** I-beams, channels, angles, and T-sections are all rolling-mill outputs.
- **KTU Workshop Lab:** A miniature two-high mill is used to demonstrate the principles safely on aluminium, copper, or mild-steel specimens.

> [!NOTE]
> The **specific roll pressure $p$** in the formula $F = p \cdot L \cdot w$ is **not a constant** — it depends on the material's flow stress, strain, strain rate, temperature, and friction. In practice, it is read from charts like the **Ekelund formula** or determined experimentally. For workshop-level problems, $p$ is usually **given in the question**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Projected Contact Length ($L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$)

We will derive this **from first principles using pure geometry**, then discuss its engineering meaning.

### Given
- Two rolls of radius $R$ rotating in opposite directions
- Stock thickness reduces from $h_0$ to $h_1$
- Therefore, the **vertical gap between the roll centers closes by** $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$

### Geometric Setup
Consider the entry plane (left side). The roll center is at horizontal distance $L$ from the entry point, and at vertical distance $(R - h_0/2)$ from the **top of the stock**. After the gap closes, the same roll center is at vertical distance $(R - h_1/2)$ from the top of the exit stock.

The vertical **shift in roll position** is:

$$\delta = \left(R - \frac{h_1}{2}\right) - \left(R - \frac{h_0}{2}\right) = \frac{h_0 - h_1}{2} = \frac{\Delta h}{2}$$

This shift happens over the horizontal contact length $L$, and the roll surface is a circle of radius $R$. Applying the Pythagorean theorem to the right triangle formed by the radius, $L$, and the vertical drop $\delta$:

$$R^2 = L^2 + (R - \delta)^2$$

Expanding the right-hand side:

$$R^2 = L^2 + R^2 - 2R\delta + \delta^2$$

The $\delta^2$ term is **negligibly small** because $\delta \ll R$ (typical $\delta$ is 0.1–1 mm while $R$ is 100–500 mm). Cancelling $R^2$ and dropping $\delta^2$:

$$0 = L^2 - 2R\delta$$

$$L^2 = 2R\delta = 2R \cdot \frac{\Delta h}{2} = R \cdot \Delta h$$

$$\boxed{\,L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of the approximation:** We assumed the arc of contact can be replaced by a straight line of length $L$ (the chord). This is valid only for **small reductions** where $\alpha$ is small — which is exactly the case in most rolling operations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Condition for Bite ($\mu \geq \tan \alpha$)

### Setup
At the moment of first contact, the roll exerts a **normal force $N$** on the stock at the angle of contact $\alpha$, plus a **frictional force $F_f = \mu N$** along the roll surface. The roll rotates such that the frictional force **pulls the stock into the roll gap**.

### Force Decomposition
Resolve the contact force into horizontal and vertical components:
- **Horizontal component (pulling stock in):** $F_{\text{horiz}} = N \sin \alpha + \mu N \cos \alpha$
- **Vertical component (compressing stock):** $F_{\text{vert}} = N \cos \alpha - \mu N \sin \alpha$

### Condition for Biting
For the rolls to **draw the stock in**, the horizontal force must be **positive** (i.e., directed into the rolls):

$$N \sin \alpha + \mu N \cos \alpha > 0$$

Dividing through by $N \cos \alpha$:

$$\tan \alpha + \mu > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu > -\tan \alpha$$

In the practical sense, since $\tan \alpha$ is positive and $\mu$ is positive, the binding constraint is that the **friction must be high enough to overcome the horizontal "push-out" component** of the normal force $N \sin \alpha$. Setting $F_{\text{horiz}} = 0$:

$$\mu N \cos \alpha = N \sin \alpha$$

$$\boxed{\,\mu = \tan \alpha\,}$$

For safety and to ensure stable biting in real conditions, we use $\mu \geq \tan \alpha$, or equivalently:

$$\alpha \leq \beta \quad \text{where} \quad \tan \beta = \mu$$

Here $\beta$ is called the **friction angle**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volume Constancy & Forward Slip

### Given
- Stock width $w$ is essentially constant (negligible spread in simple flat rolling)
- Volume is conserved (incompressible plastic flow)
- Entry velocity = $v_0$, exit velocity = $v_f$
- Initial thickness = $h_0$, final thickness = $h_1$

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Write the volume flow rate at entry:**
$$\dot{V}_{\text{in}} = h_0 \cdot w \cdot v_0$$

**Step 2 — Write the volume flow rate at exit:**
$$\dot{V}_{\text{out}} = h_1 \cdot w \cdot v_f$$

**Step 3 — Equate (incompressibility):**
$$h_0 \cdot w \cdot v_0 = h_1 \cdot w \cdot v_f$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v_f$:**
$$v_f = v_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h_1}$$

**Step 5 — Define the roll surface velocity:**
$$v_r = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{2\pi R N}{60}$$

where $N$ is the roll rotational speed in rpm.

**Step 6 — Define Forward Slip $S$:**
$$\boxed{\,S = \frac{v_f - v_r}{v_r}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Typical values:** $S = 0.02$ to $0.10$ (i.e., 2% to 10%). It is **positive** in normal rolling because the metal is squeezed thinner and speeds up relative to the roll surface at the exit.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Question:** A 50 mm thick aluminium slab is reduced to 45 mm in a two-high mill with roll radius 250 mm and roll speed 30 rpm. The slab enters at 1.0 m/s. Find (a) the projected contact length, (b) the angle of bite, (c) the exit velocity, and (d) the forward slip.

**Solution:**

Given: $h_0 = 50$ mm, $h_1 = 45$ mm, $R = 250$ mm, $N = 30$ rpm, $v_0 = 1.0$ m/s = 1000 mm/s.

**(a) Projected Contact Length:**
$$\Delta h = h_0 - h_1 = 50 - 45 = 5 \text{ mm}$$
$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{250 \times 5} = \sqrt{1250} \approx 35.36 \text{ mm}$$

**(b) Angle of Bite:**
$$\cos \alpha = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R} = 1 - \frac{5}{250} = 0.98$$
$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.98) \approx 11.48^{\circ}$$

**(c) Exit Velocity (by volume constancy):**
$$v_f = v_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h_1} = 1000 \times \frac{50}{45} \approx 1111.11 \text{ mm/s} = 1.111 \text{ m/s}$$

**(d) Roll Surface Velocity:**
$$v_r = \frac{2\pi R N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 250 \times 30}{60} = 250\pi \approx 785.40 \text{ mm/s} = 0.7854 \text{ m/s}$$

**Forward Slip:**
$$S = \frac{v_f - v_r}{v_r} = \frac{1111.11 - 785.40}{785.40} \approx 0.4145 \text{ or } 41.45\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check Caveat:** A forward slip of 41% is **physically unrealistic** for conventional rolling (typical 2–10%). This indicates that in the problem, the roll speed is *too slow* relative to the entry velocity — a real mill would be driven faster or the slab would be slowed. In exam answers, you are still required to compute and present the result, but **comment on its physical reasonableness**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Roll Separating Force & Power

### 3.4.1 Roll Separating Force
The total force that tends to push the rolls apart is the **integral of pressure over the contact area**:

$$F = \int_A p \, dA \approx p_{\text{mean}} \cdot L \cdot w$$

where:
- $p_{\text{mean}}$ = mean specific roll pressure (MPa or N/mm²)
- $L$ = projected contact length (mm)
- $w$ = width of the stock (mm)

$$\boxed{\,F = p \cdot L \cdot w\,}$$

### 3.4.2 Torque on Each Roll
Assuming the resultant force $F$ acts at the **midpoint of the contact arc**, the lever arm from the roll center is approximately $L/2$ (horizontal projection). The torque per roll is:

$$\boxed{\,T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L\,}$$

### 3.4.3 Power Required
For a roll rotating at $N$ rpm, the power required to overcome the rolling torque is:

$$P = 2 \pi N T \quad \text{(in N·mm/s when } T \text{ in N·mm, convert by dividing by 60)}$$

$$\boxed{\,P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}\,} \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **net rolling power**. The actual motor power must also overcome friction in the roll bearings, gear train, and any screwdown mechanism, so a **factor of safety** of 1.2–1.5 is added in industrial design.

### Worked Example — Separating Force and Power

**Question:** In the previous example, the slab width is 200 mm and the mean roll pressure is 100 MPa. Find (a) the roll separating force, (b) the torque per roll, and (c) the rolling power.

**Given:** $L = 35.36$ mm, $w = 200$ mm, $p = 100$ N/mm², $N = 30$ rpm.

**(a) Roll Separating Force:**
$$F = p \cdot L \cdot w = 100 \times 35.36 \times 200 = 707{,}200 \text{ N} = 707.2 \text{ kN}$$

**(b) Torque per Roll:**
$$T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 707{,}200 \times 35.36 \approx 12{,}503{,}296 \text{ N·mm} = 12.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

**(c) Rolling Power:**
$$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 30 \times 12{,}503{,}296}{60} \approx 39{,}273{,}600 \text{ W} \approx 39.27 \text{ kW}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Always carry the units through every line of your calculation. The board examiner deducts up to **1 mark per question** for sloppy or missing units.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters

| Parameter | Effect on Roll Force $F$ | Effect on Torque $T$ | Effect on Power $P$ | Practical Implication |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------|---------------------|-----------------------|
| Larger roll radius $R$ | ↓ (increases $L$ but not as fast) | ↑ | ↑ | Allows **larger drafts** per pass |
| Higher friction $\mu$ | ↑ (larger bite angle) | ↑ | ↑ | Required to **bite**; lubricants reduce it |
| Greater reduction $\Delta h$ | ↑ (linear in $\Delta h$ via $L$) | ↑ ($\propto \Delta h^{1.5}$) | ↑ | But limited by **bite condition** |
| Higher roll speed $N$ | No direct effect | No direct effect | ↑ | Increases productivity, but limits surface finish |
| Higher mean pressure $p$ (harder material) | ↑ (linear) | ↑ | ↑ | Hot rolling reduces $p$ dramatically |
| Wider stock $w$ | ↑ (linear) | No direct effect | No direct effect | Wider mills need **heavier rolls** |

## 4.4 Key Parts of a Two-High Rolling Mill (Practical Identification List)

When you perform the practical, you will be asked to **identify and label the parts**. Memorize these:

| Sl. No. | Component | Function |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Work Rolls (Top & Bottom)** | The cylindrical rolls that deform the stock |
| 2 | **Roll Bearings / Chocks** | Support the rolls and allow rotation |
| 3 | **Mill Housing / Stand** | Rigid frame that holds rolls in alignment |
| 4 | **Screwdown Mechanism** | Adjusts the gap between rolls (sets $h_1$) |
| 5 | **Main Drive Motor** | Provides rotational power to the rolls |
| 6 | **Gear Box / Pinion Stand** | Reduces motor speed and transmits torque to rolls |
| 7 | **Entry and Exit Guides** | Align the stock and prevent lateral wandering |
| 8 | **Cooling Water Sprays** | Cool the rolls and stock, reduce friction |
| 9 | **Roll Gap Indicator / Dial Gauge** | Displays the actual roll gap setting |
| 10 | **Safety Guards / Emergency Stop** | Mandatory for workshop safety |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the KTU Practical Record:** You are expected to (a) draw a neat **labelled diagram** of the two-high mill, (b) record the **initial and final thickness** of the specimen, (c) measure the **roll diameter**, and (d) **calculate** the draft, contact length, angle of bite, and (if data is given) the roll separating force.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the rolling process. State any two objectives of rolling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Rolling is a metal forming process in which the workpiece is passed between two counter-rotating cylindrical rolls to reduce its thickness and/or change its cross-section by plastic deformation under compressive forces.

**Objectives (any two, 0.75 each):**
1. **Shape conversion:** To change the cross-sectional shape of metal (e.g., a square billet to a flat plate, or a bloom to a structural I-beam).
2. **Size reduction:** To reduce the thickness of the metal uniformly, increasing its length.
3. **Mechanical property improvement:** Rolling refines the grain structure and induces work hardening, increasing strength and toughness.
4. **Surface finish:** Producing smooth, dimensionally accurate products.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Question Paper]**
*Define (i) angle of bite, and (ii) forward slip in rolling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Angle of Bite (1.5 marks):**
The angle of bite (or contact angle) $\alpha$ is the angle subtended at the roll center by the arc of contact between the roll and the stock. Geometrically,
$$\cos \alpha = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R}$$
where $\Delta h$ is the reduction and $R$ is the roll radius. The condition for the roll to bite the stock is $\mu \geq \tan \alpha$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction.

**(ii) Forward Slip (1.5 marks):**
Forward slip $S$ is the difference between the exit velocity of the stock and the tangential velocity of the roll surface, expressed as a fraction of the roll velocity:
$$S = \frac{v_f - v_r}{v_r}$$
It arises from the volume constancy of plastic flow. Typical values range from 2% to 10%.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, with internal choice (either-or). Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ⭐ Question B-A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **two-high rolling mill** with a neat labelled sketch. List its main components and state the function of each.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 60 mm thick mild-steel slab is reduced to 54 mm in a two-high mill with roll diameter 500 mm, rotating at 25 rpm. The coefficient of friction between roll and stock is 0.12. The mean specific roll pressure is 120 MPa, and the slab width is 250 mm. Calculate:
1. The projected contact length
2. The angle of bite
3. The roll separating force
4. The rolling power required

Verify whether the bite condition is satisfied.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-A

**Part (a) — Two-High Rolling Mill (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** A two-high rolling mill consists of **two rolls** mounted in a rigid housing, rotating in opposite directions, used to reduce the thickness of a metal workpiece passed between them.

**Sketch (3 marks):** Draw a clear, labelled diagram showing:
- Top and bottom rolls (cylindrical, counter-rotating)
- Stock entering from one side and exiting thinner from the other
- Roll bearings/chocks
- Mill housing
- Screwdown mechanism
- Drive motor
- Roll gap indicator

**Main Components (3 marks — list with function):**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Work rolls | Deform the stock plastically |
| Bearings/chocks | Support rolls and allow free rotation |
| Mill housing | Provides rigid alignment |
| Screwdown | Adjusts roll gap to control reduction |
| Drive motor & gear box | Supplies torque and rotational speed |
| Guides | Align the stock at entry and exit |
| Cooling system | Cools rolls and reduces wear |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $h_0 = 60$ mm, $h_1 = 54$ mm
- $D = 500$ mm, so $R = 250$ mm
- $N = 25$ rpm
- $\mu = 0.12$
- $p = 120$ N/mm² (MPa)
- $w = 250$ mm

**Step 1 — Draft:**
$$\Delta h = h_0 - h_1 = 60 - 54 = 6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Projected Contact Length (1.5 marks):**
$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{250 \times 6} = \sqrt{1500} \approx 38.73 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Angle of Bite (1.5 marks):**
$$\cos \alpha = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R} = 1 - \frac{6}{250} = 0.976$$
$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.976) \approx 12.62^{\circ}$$

**Step 4 — Verify Bite Condition (0.5 mark):**
$$\tan \alpha = \tan(12.62^{\circ}) \approx 0.2241$$
$$\mu = 0.12$$
Since $\mu = 0.12 < \tan \alpha = 0.224$, the **bite condition is NOT satisfied**. The rolls cannot grip the stock at this reduction. (This is an important observation — in practice, the surface would need roughening or the reduction per pass reduced.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often compute $\alpha$ and forget to verify $\mu \geq \tan \alpha$. A 0.5–1 mark deduction is common for omitting this check.

**Step 5 — Roll Separating Force (1.5 marks):**
$$F = p \cdot L \cdot w = 120 \times 38.73 \times 250 = 1{,}161{,}900 \text{ N} \approx 1161.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Torque per Roll (0.5 mark):**
$$T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 1{,}161{,}900 \times 38.73 \approx 22{,}500{,}194 \text{ N·mm} = 22.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 7 — Rolling Power (1.5 marks):**
$$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 25 \times 22{,}500{,}194}{60} \approx 58{,}901{,}720 \text{ W} \approx 58.9 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note to the student:** Even though the bite condition fails, the numerical force and power are still computed *as a hypothetical exercise*. In the exam, present the calculation as above and add a concluding remark that the **reduction is too large for the available friction**, suggesting practical remedies (lubricant removal, surface roughening, smaller draft, or larger roll radius).

---

### ⭐ Question B-B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2, Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **rolling process**. Explain the **phenomenon of forward slip** and derive the relationship $h_0 v_0 = h_1 v_f$.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 30 mm thick copper strip is reduced to 25 mm in a two-high mill. The roll diameter is 400 mm and the roll speed is 40 rpm. The coefficient of friction is 0.1. Find:
1. The angle of bite
2. The roll surface velocity
3. The exit velocity, assuming entry velocity = 1.2 m/s
4. The forward slip
5. Check whether the bite condition is satisfied

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-B

**Part (a) — Process Description and Forward Slip (7 marks)**

**Sketch and Description (4 marks):**
Draw the rolling setup showing the two rolls, the stock, the bite zone, the neutral point, and the entry/exit velocities. Briefly describe (3–4 lines) that the metal is compressed as it passes through the gap.

**Derivation of $h_0 v_0 = h_1 v_f$ (3 marks):**
By the **conservation of volume** (incompressibility of plastic deformation):
- Volume entering per second = $h_0 \cdot w \cdot v_0$
- Volume leaving per second = $h_1 \cdot w \cdot v_f$
- Equating: $h_0 w v_0 = h_1 w v_f$
- Width $w$ cancels:
$$h_0 v_0 = h_1 v_f$$

**Forward Slip (1 mark):**
Since $h_0 > h_1$, we get $v_f > v_0$. The strip leaves the rolls faster than it entered. The **forward slip** is defined as:
$$S = \frac{v_f - v_r}{v_r}$$
where $v_r$ is the tangential speed of the roll surface. It is typically 2%–10% in industrial rolling.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $h_0 = 30$ mm, $h_1 = 25$ mm
- $D = 400$ mm, $R = 200$ mm
- $N = 40$ rpm
- $\mu = 0.1$
- $v_0 = 1.2$ m/s = 1200 mm/s

**Step 1 — Angle of Bite (1.5 marks):**
$$\cos \alpha = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R} = 1 - \frac{30 - 25}{200} = 1 - \frac{5}{200} = 0.975$$
$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.975) \approx 12.84^{\circ}$$

**Step 2 — Roll Surface Velocity (1.5 marks):**
$$v_r = \frac{2\pi R N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 200 \times 40}{60} = \frac{16000\pi}{60} \approx 837.76 \text{ mm/s} \approx 0.838 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Exit Velocity (1.5 marks):**
$$v_f = v_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h_1} = 1200 \times \frac{30}{25} = 1440 \text{ mm/s} = 1.44 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Forward Slip (1 mark):**
$$S = \frac{v_f - v_r}{v_r} = \frac{1440 - 837.76}{837.76} \approx 0.7188 \text{ or } 71.88\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check:** A forward slip of 72% is **physically unrealistic** for conventional rolling. This indicates that the entry velocity is too high relative to the roll speed. In real practice, the entry speed is dictated by the previous stand and matched to the roll speed. In exam, present the calculation and note the physical limitation.

**Step 5 — Bite Condition Check (1.5 marks):**
$$\tan \alpha = \tan(12.84^{\circ}) \approx 0.2281$$
$$\mu = 0.1$$
$$\mu = 0.1 < \tan \alpha = 0.2281$$
**Bite condition is NOT satisfied.** Practical solutions: increase friction (roughen rolls, remove lubricant), reduce the draft, or use larger rolls.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Read This Before Exam!)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Rolling Questions (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the bite condition** ($\mu \geq \tan \alpha$) — examiners explicitly test this, and skipping it costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up $h_0$ and $h_1$** in the volume constancy equation. Remember: $h_0$ is *initial* (larger) and $h_1$ is *final* (smaller).
> 3. **Unit inconsistencies:** Rolling problems involve mm, N, N/mm², N·mm, W. Mixing up units (e.g., using $R$ in metres and $\Delta h$ in mm in the same equation) is the #1 source of silly errors. **Convert all lengths to mm** before starting calculations.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the torque formula — torque is computed per roll, but power uses both rolls, or vice versa depending on the formulation. Be explicit: $T_{\text{per roll}} = 0.5 F L$.
> 5. **Not labelling the diagram.** A neat labelled diagram in part (a) of a 7-mark question is worth 3 marks. Skipping labels costs you almost half the marks.
> 6. **Confusing "angle of bite" with "angle of neutral point"** — they are different. The angle of neutral point $\beta \approx 0.5 \alpha$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Glance Summary for Last-Minute Revision**

- [x] **Rolling** = plastic deformation of metal between two counter-rotating rolls.
- [x] **Four objectives:** Shape conversion, size reduction, mechanical property improvement, surface finish quality.
- [x] **Draft** $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$.
- [x] **Percentage reduction** = $(\Delta h / h_0) \times 100$.
- [x] **Projected contact length** $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$.
- [x] **Angle of bite:** $\cos \alpha = 1 - \Delta h / R$.
- [x] **Bite condition:** $\mu \geq \tan \alpha$ (or $\alpha \leq \beta$ where $\tan \beta = \mu$).
- [x] **Maximum draft (small-angle approx.):** $\Delta h_{\max} \approx \mu^2 R$.
- [x] **Volume constancy:** $h_0 v_0 = h_1 v_f$ → since $h_0 > h_1$, $v_f > v_0$.
- [x] **Forward slip:** $S = (v_f - v_r) / v_r$, typical 2%–10%.
- [x] **Roll separating force:** $F = p \cdot L \cdot w$ (Newton).
- [x] **Torque per roll:** $T = 0.5 F L$ (N·mm).
- [x] **Power:** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ (Watts).
- [x] **Two-high mill** = 2 work rolls, simplest configuration, used in workshop practicals.
- [x] **Components to identify in practical:** work rolls, bearings, mill housing, screwdown, motor, gear box, guides, cooling system.
- [x] **Always verify the bite condition** before assuming the rolling is feasible.
- [x] **Always state units** in every line of the calculation.
- [x] **Always check the physical reasonableness** of computed forward slip (should be 2–10%).

> **Best of luck for your KTU Engineering Workshop examination! 🚀**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroplating: Electroplating a given job
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCESL106 - ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Electroplating: Electroplating a given job

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electroplating** is an electrochemical surface engineering process in which a thin, uniform, and adherent layer of a desired metal (the *coating metal*) is deposited onto the surface of a conductive substrate (the *base metal* or *job*) through the controlled reduction of metal cations from an aqueous electrolytic solution, using direct electric current supplied by an external DC power source.

In KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory terminology, the job to be plated is made the **cathode** (negatively charged electrode), the pure metal to be deposited is made the **anode** (positively charged electrode), and both are immersed in an **electrolyte** containing ions of the coating metal. When current flows, oxidation occurs at the anode (dissolving the metal) and reduction occurs at the cathode (depositing the metal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (GCESL106):** Electroplating is grouped under *Manufacturing Practice / Surface Finishing* experiments. Students are expected to identify components of the electroplating setup, explain the electrochemical mechanism, list pre-treatment steps, and describe safety precautions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **painting a wall**, but instead of using a brush that smears paint, you use a **magnetic conveyor belt** that carries only the exact "color molecules" (metal ions) and glues them one-by-one onto the wall surface with perfect uniformity. The electric current acts as the **magnetic conveyor**, the electrolyte is the **bucket of paint**, the anode is the **paint reservoir being continuously refilled**, and the job (cathode) is the **wall being painted**.

A simpler analogy: think of electroplating as giving an **old iron spoon a thin gold skin**. The gold skin does not come from a brush — it is pulled out of a chemical bath by electricity and "stuck" atom-by-atom onto the spoon.

### 1.3 Why Electroplating is Performed

- **Corrosion resistance** (e.g., zinc plating on iron — *galvanization*)
- **Decorative finish** (e.g., chrome plating on motorcycle parts)
- **Improved hardness and wear resistance** (e.g., hard chrome on shafts)
- **Enhanced electrical conductivity** (e.g., silver plating on copper contacts)
- **Solderability improvement** (e.g., tin plating on PCB pads)
- **Dimensional repair** of worn parts (e.g., nickel plating)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Current density: typically **10 to 30 A/m²** for decorative plating
> - Faraday's constant: **F = 96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol)
> - Operating voltage: **3 V to 12 V DC** for most laboratory setups

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Electrochemical Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The electroplating process rests on **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. Let us deconstruct what happens inside the electrolyte bath:

1. **The electrolyte dissolves the anode metal.** When the anode (e.g., a copper rod in copper sulphate solution) is connected to the positive terminal of a DC source, atoms of the anode lose electrons and enter the solution as positive ions:
   $$\text{Cu (solid)} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} \text{(in solution)} + 2e^{-}$$
   This is an **oxidation half-reaction** (loss of electrons).

2. **Electrons travel through the external circuit.** The DC power supply pushes electrons from the anode to the cathode (the job to be plated) through the connecting wires.

3. **Reduction occurs at the cathode.** The metal cations in the solution, being positively charged, migrate toward the negatively charged cathode. Upon reaching the cathode surface, they gain electrons and deposit as neutral metal atoms:
   $$\text{Cu}^{2+} \text{(in solution)} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cu (solid, on job)}$$
   This is a **reduction half-reaction** (gain of electrons).

4. **The cycle is self-sustaining.** As long as current flows, the anode continuously dissolves to replenish the metal ions consumed at the cathode, maintaining a constant ion concentration in the bath.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Soluble Anode?**
> In *decorative* or *protective* plating with metals like copper, nickel, or silver, a **soluble anode** of the same metal is used so the electrolyte concentration stays constant. In contrast, for **chrome plating**, an **insoluble lead anode** is used because chromium is dissolved from chromic acid and not from the anode.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Process

- **Why DC current and not AC?** Direct current produces a unidirectional flow of ions; alternating current would reverse the deposition direction 50 times per second (in 50 Hz mains), preventing any net metal buildup.
- **Why is the electrolyte specific?** The electrolyte must contain the *same metal* being plated (e.g., CuSO₄ for copper plating, NiSO₄ for nickel plating). Impurities cause **rough, burnt, or pitted deposits**.
- **Why pre-treatment is critical?** Grease, oxides, and dirt on the job prevent proper adhesion, causing the plated layer to peel off. This is why **pre-cleaning is non-negotiable**.
- **Why is current density controlled?** Too low a current gives thin, slow deposition; too high a current gives **burnt, powdery, hydrogen-evolving** deposits that are mechanically weak.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Mathematical Statement | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------------|----------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Faraday's First Law** | $m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$ | $m$ = mass deposited (kg), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $Z$ = electrochemical equivalent (kg/C) | Calculates how much metal is deposited for a given current × time |
| 2 | **Electrochemical Equivalent** | $Z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$ | $M$ = molar mass (kg/mol), $n$ = valency, $F$ = 96,485 C/mol | Converts molar mass to per-coulomb equivalent |
| 3 | **Faraday's Second Law** | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2} = \dfrac{M_1 / n_1}{M_2 / n_2}$ | Subscripts 1 and 2 denote two substances | Compares masses deposited by the same charge |
| 4 | **Combined Faraday's Law** | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | All variables as above | The most-used working formula in KTU problems |
| 5 | **Current Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ in percent | Real baths waste charge to H₂ evolution; typical $\eta$ = 90–95% |
| 6 | **Current Density** | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | $J$ in A/m², $A$ = cathode surface area (m²) | Controls deposit quality and plating rate |
| 7 | **Thickness of Deposit** | $h = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A} = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$ | $h$ = thickness (m), $\rho$ = density of metal (kg/m³) | Used to find plating time for a desired thickness |
| 8 | **Plating Time Required** | $t = \dfrac{h \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I}$ | All variables as above | Reverse calculation — how long to plate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake to Avoid:** Do not confuse the symbol $Z$ (electrochemical equivalent, kg/C) with the atomic number. Also, $F$ is the Faraday constant **96,485 C/mol**, not 96,000 or 1,00,000.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Chromium plating on bumpers, pistons, and shock absorbers for wear and corrosion resistance.
- **Electronics industry:** Gold plating on connector pins, tin plating on printed circuit boards (PCBs).
- **Aerospace:** Silver plating on bearings; cadmium plating on high-strength steel fasteners.
- **Decorative hardware:** Brass-plated door handles, bathroom fittings.
- **Energy sector:** Nickel plating on battery components, zinc plating on transmission towers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Combined Faraday's Law

Faraday's First Law states that the mass of substance deposited is directly proportional to the charge passed:

$$m \propto Q$$

where charge $Q = I \cdot t$. Introducing the constant of proportionality $Z$ (electrochemical equivalent):

$$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

Now, the electrochemical equivalent $Z$ is the mass deposited per unit charge. One mole of electrons carries a charge of $F$ coulombs (Faraday's constant). For a metal of valency $n$, depositing one mole of the metal requires $n$ moles of electrons, i.e., a charge of $n \cdot F$ coulombs. Therefore, the mass deposited per coulomb is:

$$Z = \frac{\text{Molar mass of metal}}{\text{Charge required per mole}} = \frac{M}{n \cdot F}$$

Substituting this into the first law:

$$m = \frac{M}{n \cdot F} \cdot I \cdot t$$

This is the **Combined Faraday's Law** — the master equation of electroplating calculations.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Thickness Equation

Thickness $h$ is related to mass $m$, density $\rho$, and plated area $A$ by the volume relation:

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = A \cdot h$$

Solving for thickness:

$$h = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$$

Substituting $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$:

$$h = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> **Problem (Model):** A steel job of surface area $0.02 \text{ m}^2$ is to be copper-plated using a current of $4 \text{ A}$ for $45 \text{ minutes}$. Calculate:
> (i) The mass of copper deposited.
> (ii) The thickness of the deposit.
> (iii) The plating time required to obtain a thickness of $30 \text{ μm}$.
>
> Given: $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5 \text{ g/mol} = 0.0635 \text{ kg/mol}$, $n = 2$, $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8960 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$.

#### Part (i) — Mass Deposited

**Step 1: Identify the given values.**
$I = 4 \text{ A}$, $t = 45 \text{ min} = 45 \times 60 = 2700 \text{ s}$.

**Step 2: Apply the Combined Faraday's Law.**

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

$$m = \frac{0.0635 \times 4 \times 2700}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the numerator.**

$$0.0635 \times 4 \times 2700 = 685.8$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the denominator.**

$$2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970$$

**Step 5: Divide.**

$$m = \frac{685.8}{192{,}970} = 3.554 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 3.554 \text{ g}$$

> **Answer:** $m \approx 3.55 \text{ g}$ of copper is deposited.

#### Part (ii) — Thickness of Deposit

**Step 1: Use the thickness formula.**

$$h = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

**Step 2: Substitute all values.**

$$h = \frac{0.0635 \times 4 \times 2700}{2 \times 96{,}485 \times 8960 \times 0.02}$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerator (same as before).**

$$\text{Numerator} = 685.8$$

**Step 4: Compute the denominator.**

$$2 \times 96{,}485 \times 8960 \times 0.02 = 192{,}970 \times 179.2 = 34{,}580{,}224$$

**Step 5: Divide.**

$$h = \frac{685.8}{34{,}580{,}224} = 1.983 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 19.83 \text{ μm}$$

> **Answer:** $h \approx 19.8 \text{ μm}$.

#### Part (iii) — Plating Time for 30 μm Thickness

**Step 1: Re-arrange the thickness formula to solve for $t$.**

$$t = \frac{h \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I}$$

**Step 2: Substitute $h = 30 \text{ μm} = 30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$.**

$$t = \frac{(30 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8960 \times 0.02 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{0.0635 \times 4}$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerator step by step.**

$$30 \times 10^{-6} \times 8960 = 0.2688$$

$$0.2688 \times 0.02 = 0.005376$$

$$0.005376 \times 2 = 0.010752$$

$$0.010752 \times 96{,}485 = 1037.65$$

**Step 4: Compute the denominator.**

$$0.0635 \times 4 = 0.254$$

**Step 5: Divide.**

$$t = \frac{1037.65}{0.254} = 4085.24 \text{ s} \approx 68.1 \text{ min}$$

> **Answer:** $t \approx 68.1 \text{ minutes}$ to achieve a 30 μm copper layer.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pre-Treatment Steps for the Job

| Step No. | Operation | Purpose | Common Chemicals / Method |
|----------|-----------|---------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Degreasing** | Remove oil, grease, and dirt | Hot alkaline solution (NaOH, Na₂CO₃) or solvent wipe |
| 2 | **Rinsing** | Wash off degreasing chemicals | Clean running water |
| 3 | **Pickling** (Acid cleaning) | Remove rust, scale, oxides | Dilute H₂SO₄ or HCl dip |
| 4 | **Rinsing** | Remove acid traces | Clean running water |
| 5 | **Anodic / Cathodic cleaning** | Final electrochemical cleaning | Job as anode/cathode in alkaline bath |
| 6 | **Rinsing** | Final wash | Distilled water (preferred for last rinse) |
| 7 | **Activation dip** | Activate surface just before plating | Dilute acid (e.g., 5–10% H₂SO₄) |
| 8 | **Plating** | Actual electroplating | Electrolytic bath with controlled current |
| 9 | **Rinsing & Drying** | Remove electrolyte, prevent stains | Distilled water rinse + hot air dry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** Skipping the **activation dip** just before plating is a common reason for **poor adhesion** and **peeling of the deposit** in lab sessions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an Electroplating Setup

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture and flow** of an electroplating experiment, showing the role of every component and the path of current, ions, and electrons.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS1[DC Power Supply Positive Terminal] --> AN1[Anode Soluble Metal of Coating Material]
    PS2[DC Power Supply Negative Terminal] --> CA1[Cathode The Job to be Plated]
    AN1 --> EL1[Electrolyte Aqueous Salt Solution of Coating Metal]
    CA1 --> EL1
    EL1 --> IO1[Positive Metal Ions Migrate Toward Cathode]
    IO1 --> DE1[Metal Ions Gain Electrons and Deposit on Job Surface]
    DE1 --> CA1
    AN1 --> IO2[Anode Atoms Lose Electrons and Dissolve Into Electrolyte as Ions]
    IO2 --> EL1
    DE1 --> TH1[Thickness and Mass Determined by Faraday Law]
    TH1 --> OUT1[Plated Job With Uniform Metallic Coating]

    classDef power fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#8b4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef electrode fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bath fill:#e0ffff,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class PS1,PS2 power
    class AN1,CA1 electrode
    class EL1,IO1,IO2,DE1 bath
    class TH1,OUT1 output
```

### 4.2 Component Identification Table

| Component | Symbol / Label | Material Used (Typical) | Function |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|----------|
| **DC Power Supply** | PS1, PS2 | Rectifier (3–12 V, 1–10 A) | Provides unidirectional current |
| **Anode** | AN1 | Pure copper / nickel / silver rod | Dissolves to replenish metal ions |
| **Cathode (Job)** | CA1 | Steel, brass, copper job | Receives the metal deposit |
| **Electrolyte** | EL1 | CuSO₄ + H₂SO₄ (for copper) | Conducts ions between electrodes |
| **Connecting Wires** | — | Copper wire with crocodile clips | Carry current into the bath |
| **Tank / Beaker** | — | Glass or PVC container | Holds the electrolyte |
| **Rheostat / Ammeter** | — | Variable resistor + ammeter | Controls and measures current |

### 4.3 Process Flow of Electroplating a Given Job

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Receive the Given Job] --> A2[Identify Base Metal and Required Plating Metal]
    A2 --> A3[Mechanical Cleaning Sanding and Brushing]
    A3 --> A4[Degreasing in Alkaline Solution]
    A4 --> A5[Rinsing in Water]
    A5 --> A6[Pickling in Dilute Acid]
    A6 --> A7[Rinsing in Water]
    A7 --> A8[Activation Dip in Dilute Acid]
    A8 --> A9[Set Up Electroplating Bath Anode Cathode Electrolyte]
    A9 --> A10[Switch on DC Supply Set Current and Voltage]
    A10 --> A11[Monitor Plating for Calculated Time]
    A11 --> A12[Switch Off Supply Remove Job]
    A12 --> A13[Final Rinsing and Drying]
    A13 --> A14[Inspect the Plated Surface Uniformity and Adhesion]
    A14 --> A15[Record Observations Mass Thickness Appearance]

    classDef step1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A1,A2,A3 step1
    class A4,A5,A6,A7,A8,A9,A10,A11,A12 step2
    class A13,A14,A15 step3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**Define electroplating. State Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis.**

**Model Answer:**
Electroplating is the process of depositing a thin layer of a desired metal onto the surface of a conductive object (the job) by passing a direct electric current through an electrolyte containing ions of that metal. The job is made the cathode, and the pure metal to be deposited is made the anode.

**Faraday's First Law:** The mass of a substance deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

$$m \propto Q \quad \text{or} \quad m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**List any four pre-treatment steps required for a job before electroplating.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Degreasing** — to remove oil and grease using hot alkaline solution.
2. **Rinsing** — to wash off the degreasing chemicals.
3. **Pickling** — acid dipping to remove rust, scale, and oxides.
4. **Activation dip** — final acid dip just before plating to ensure proper adhesion.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the principle of electroplating with a neat labelled diagram. Describe the role of the anode, cathode, and electrolyte in the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brass job of surface area $0.015 \text{ m}^2$ is to be nickel-plated using a current of $3 \text{ A}$. Calculate the time required to deposit a nickel layer of thickness $25 \text{ μm}$. Given: $M_{\text{Ni}} = 58.7 \text{ g/mol}$, valency $n = 2$, $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8900 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Principle of Electroplating:**

Electroplating is based on the principle of **electrolytic deposition**. When direct current passes through an electrolyte, metal cations migrate to the cathode and get reduced to neutral metal atoms, forming a thin, adherent layer on the job surface.

- **Anode (+):** Made of the pure coating metal. It undergoes oxidation and dissolves into the electrolyte as metal ions, maintaining ion concentration. Example: a pure copper rod in copper sulphate solution.
- **Cathode (−):** The job to be plated. Metal ions from the electrolyte gain electrons here and deposit as solid metal.
- **Electrolyte:** An aqueous solution of a salt of the coating metal (e.g., CuSO₄, NiSO₄). It conducts ions and supplies the metal cations for deposition.

The half-reactions are:

$$\text{Anode (oxidation):} \quad \text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + ne^{-}$$

$$\text{Cathode (reduction):} \quad \text{M}^{n+} + ne^{-} \rightarrow \text{M}$$

The mass deposited is governed by **Faraday's Law**: $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$.

*(A labelled diagram similar to the Mermaid block above should accompany this answer in the exam.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the given values.**
$I = 3 \text{ A}$, $A = 0.015 \text{ m}^2$, $h = 25 \text{ μm} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$, $M_{\text{Ni}} = 0.0587 \text{ kg/mol}$, $n = 2$, $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8900 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$.

**Step 2: Re-arrange the thickness formula to solve for $t$.**

$$t = \frac{h \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**

$$t = \frac{(25 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8900 \times 0.015 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{0.0587 \times 3}$$

**Step 4: Compute the numerator step by step.**

$$25 \times 10^{-6} \times 8900 = 0.2225$$

$$0.2225 \times 0.015 = 0.0033375$$

$$0.0033375 \times 2 = 0.006675$$

$$0.006675 \times 96{,}485 = 644.04$$

**Step 5: Compute the denominator.**

$$0.0587 \times 3 = 0.1761$$

**Step 6: Divide.**

$$t = \frac{644.04}{0.1761} = 3657.2 \text{ s} \approx 60.95 \text{ min}$$

> **Answer:** $t \approx 61 \text{ minutes}$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a flow chart, explain the step-by-step procedure for electroplating a given job. Why is pre-treatment considered the most important stage? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A current of $5 \text{ A}$ is passed through a copper sulphate solution for $1.5 \text{ hours}$. Calculate the mass of copper deposited and the thickness of the layer if the surface area is $0.01 \text{ m}^2$. Given: $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5 \text{ g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8960 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$. Assume 100% current efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Procedure:**

The procedure for electroplating a given job consists of the following stages (refer to the Mermaid process flow in Section 4.3):

1. **Identification of base metal and plating metal.**
2. **Mechanical cleaning** — sanding/buffing to remove surface irregularities.
3. **Degreasing** in hot alkaline solution.
4. **Water rinsing.**
5. **Pickling** in dilute H₂SO₄ to remove oxides and scale.
6. **Water rinsing.**
7. **Activation dip** in dilute acid.
8. **Setting up the bath** — placing the anode (pure metal), cathode (the job), and electrolyte.
9. **Switching on the DC supply** at the calculated current and voltage.
10. **Monitoring plating** for the calculated duration.
11. **Switching off, removing, rinsing, and drying** the job.
12. **Inspection** of the plated surface.

**Why pre-treatment is most important:** Pre-treatment ensures the surface is **chemically clean and physically rough** at the microscopic level. Any residual oil, oxide, or dust prevents proper bonding of the deposit, leading to **blistering, peeling, or pitting**. Adhesion failures in electroplating are almost always traced back to inadequate pre-treatment. Hence it is the most critical step.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the given values.**
$I = 5 \text{ A}$, $t = 1.5 \text{ hr} = 1.5 \times 3600 = 5400 \text{ s}$, $A = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$, $M_{\text{Cu}} = 0.0635 \text{ kg/mol}$, $n = 2$, $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8960 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$.

**Step 2: Mass of copper deposited.**

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

$$m = \frac{0.0635 \times 5 \times 5400}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerator.**

$$0.0635 \times 5 \times 5400 = 1714.5$$

**Step 4: Compute the denominator.**

$$2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970$$

**Step 5: Divide.**

$$m = \frac{1714.5}{192{,}970} = 8.885 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 8.885 \text{ g}$$

**Step 6: Thickness of the layer.**

$$h = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{8.885 \times 10^{-3}}{8960 \times 0.01}$$

$$h = \frac{8.885 \times 10^{-3}}{89.6} = 9.917 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 99.2 \text{ μm}$$

> **Answer:** Mass $\approx 8.89 \text{ g}$; Thickness $\approx 99.2 \text{ μm}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in time:** Many students write $t = 1.5$ directly instead of converting hours to seconds. This produces an answer off by a factor of 3600 — instant loss of 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Using grams per mole directly in SI units:** Always convert molar mass from g/mol to kg/mol (divide by 1000) when using $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol in SI units. Mixing SI and CGS is the single most common error in KTU electroplating numericals.
> 3. **Confusing the role of anode and cathode:** The job is the **cathode (−)**, not the anode. Reversing this in the diagram or the half-reaction will cost 2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting pre-treatment steps:** In descriptive questions, listing only "cleaning" without specifying *degreasing → rinsing → pickling → rinsing → activation* loses easy marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention DC supply:** Students sometimes just say "pass current." KTU examiners specifically look for **"direct current (DC)"** — losing 1 mark otherwise.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Electroplating definition:** Electrodeposition of a coating metal onto a conductive job (cathode) using DC current and a metal-salt electrolyte.
- **Job is the cathode (−);** coating metal is the **anode (+)**.
- **Electrolyte = aqueous salt of the coating metal** (e.g., CuSO₄, NiSO₄, AgNO₃).
- **Faraday's Combined Law:** $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ — the master equation.
- **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ (commit to memory).
- **Thickness formula:** $h = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$.
- **Plating time formula:** $t = \dfrac{h \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I}$.
- **Current density range:** Typically 10–30 A/m² for decorative plating; always use **m²** in SI units.
- **Pre-treatment sequence:** Mechanical cleaning → Degreasing → Rinsing → Pickling → Rinsing → Activation dip → Plating.
- **Activation dip is mandatory** just before plating to prevent peeling.
- **DC, not AC** — alternating current prevents net deposition.
- **Insoluble anodes (Pb, Pb-Sb)** are used in chrome plating; **soluble anodes** are used in copper, nickel, and silver plating.
- **Current efficiency ($\eta$)** is typically 90–95% in real baths due to hydrogen evolution; in KTU numericals, assume 100% unless stated otherwise.
- **Practical safety:** Wear gloves and goggles; ensure good ventilation (some acid fumes are toxic); never touch live electrodes; keep the DC power supply off while setting up.
- **Common applications:** Decorative chrome, corrosion-resistant zinc, conductive silver, hard nickel, and tin for solderability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metrology: Common measuring instruments used in workshop, experiments to find the angle of a dovetail, angle of a taper and the radius of a circular surface. Introduction to instruments Vernier Bevel Protractor, Vernier Depth Gauge, Vernier Height Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCESL106 - ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Metrology: Common measuring instruments used in workshop, experiments to find the angle of a dovetail, angle of a taper and the radius of a circular surface. Introduction to instruments Vernier Bevel Protractor, Vernier Depth Gauge, Vernier Height Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Metrology?

**Metrology** is the science of measurement. It deals with the methods, instruments, and standards used to obtain accurate measurements of physical quantities such as length, angle, and radius in engineering practice. In a workshop context, metrology is limited to **dimensional metrology** — the measurement of linear and angular dimensions of machined components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Metrology is the branch of engineering science that deals with the measurement of lengths, angles, and geometric features of manufactured parts using standardized instruments, ensuring interchangeability, quality, and functional performance of components.

### 1.2 Common Measuring Instruments Used in a Workshop

Workshop measuring instruments can be classified into three broad categories based on the dimension they measure:

| Category | Linear Dimensions | Angular Dimensions | Surface/Radius Dimensions |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| **Basic** | Steel Rule, Measuring Tape | Bevel Protractor (simple) | Radius Gauge, Filler Gauge |
| **Vernier-based** | Vernier Caliper, Vernier Depth Gauge, Vernier Height Gauge | Vernier Bevel Protractor | Vernier Height Gauge (with scriber) |
| **High Precision** | Micrometer, Dial Gauge, Slip Gauges | Sine Bar, Autocollimator | Profile Projector, CMM |

### 1.3 The Concept of Vernier Scale — Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vernier Principle (Core Idea):** A vernier scale is a short auxiliary scale that slides alongside a main scale. It allows the user to read a fraction of the smallest main scale division with high accuracy, by detecting which vernier division aligns perfectly with a main scale division.

**Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are trying to estimate the weight of fruits at a market, but the scale only weighs in 1 kg steps. You put 7 apples on the scale, and the needle points between 2 kg and 3 kg. You cannot tell whether it is 2.6 kg or 2.8 kg. Now, suppose you had a *reference set* of 5 apples of known weight (say 0.2 kg each) that you could slide one-by-one onto the other side. The moment the scale balances, you know exactly how to read the fractional part. The vernier scale works in a similar fashion — it is a *sliding reference* that lets you interpolate between two main scale divisions.

### 1.4 Introduction to Vernier Bevel Protractor

The **Vernier Bevel Protractor** is a precision instrument used for measuring and setting angles to an accuracy of **5 minutes (5′)** of arc.

- It is used for checking angles on jigs, fixtures, tool angles, and machined surfaces.
- It consists of a circular graduated disc (main scale graduated in degrees: 0°–90°–0°–90°), a vernier scale that slides along the edge, two blades (one fixed, one movable), and a locking lever.

> [!NOTE]
> **Least Count (LC) of Vernier Bevel Protractor = 5 minutes of arc (5′)** — meaning it can resolve angles as small as 1/12th of a degree.
