### 1.5 Introduction to Vernier Depth Gauge

The **Vernier Depth Gauge** is used for measuring the depth of slots, holes, recesses, and steps that cannot be measured with a vernier caliper.

- It consists of a graduated main scale (a thin steel rule) and a sliding head with a vernier scale.
- A flat reference base slides on the surface of the workpiece, and the main scale drops vertically into the slot to be measured.
- Typical Least Count: **0.02 mm** (for 50 vernier divisions) or **0.05 mm** (for 20 vernier divisions).

### 1.6 Introduction to Vernier Height Gauge

The **Vernier Height Gauge** is a precision instrument used to measure the vertical height of a point above a reference surface (typically a surface plate) and to scribe parallel lines on a workpiece.

- It is mounted on a heavy stable base and slides along a vertical column.
- The scriber/jaw is used as the measuring point.
- A common application in this experiment is **finding the radius of a circular surface** by using the height gauge in combination with a surface plate and slip gauges (or by the rise-and-fall method).
- Typical Least Count: **0.02 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vernier Scale Theory — Universal Working Principle

Let the value of one main scale division (MSD) be $s$ units.
Let the value of one vernier scale division (VSD) be $v$ units.
Let $n$ vernier scale divisions coincide with $(n-1)$ main scale divisions.

By the definition of the vernier scale:

$$v \times n = s \times (n - 1)$$

Therefore:

$$v = s \times \frac{(n-1)}{n}$$

The **least count (LC)** is the smallest measurable value, given by:

$$LC = s - v = s - s \times \frac{(n-1)}{n} = \frac{s}{n}$$

Or in its more general form:

$$LC = \frac{\text{Value of one MSD}}{\text{Number of VSD divisions}}$$

The **vernier reading formula** for any instrument is:

$$\text{Measurement} = \text{Main Scale Reading (MSR)} + (\text{Vernier Coincidence} \times LC)$$

### 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Instrument

#### A. Vernier Bevel Protractor

- The main disc is graduated in **degrees** from 0° to 90° on each side of the zero line.
- 12 vernier divisions correspond to 23 main scale divisions (23 degrees).
- Thus, 12 VSD = 23 MSD (each MSD = 1° = 60′).
- Value of 1 VSD = 23°/12 = 1° 55′.
- Therefore: $LC = 60' - 55' = 5' = \frac{1}{12} \text{ degree}$.

**Angular Measurement Formula:**

$$\theta = \text{MSR (in degrees)} + (V \times 5') \quad \text{[where } V \text{ is the coinciding vernier division]}$$

#### B. Vernier Depth Gauge

- Main scale: 1 MSD = 1 mm (typical).
- 50 VSD = 49 MSD.
- $LC = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{50} = 0.02 \text{ mm}$.

**Depth Measurement Formula:**

$$D = \text{MSR (in mm)} + (V \times 0.02) \text{ mm}$$

#### C. Vernier Height Gauge

- Same vernier principle as the depth gauge.
- Used on a **surface plate** (precision flat reference base).
- For **radius of a circular surface** experiment, the height gauge is used in conjunction with slip gauges or by direct measurement over the arc.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in almost every KTU numerical on this topic.**

| # | Instrument | Value of 1 MSD | Vernier Relation | Least Count (LC) | Measurement Formula |
|---|------------|----------------|------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Steel Rule | 1 mm | — | 1 mm | Direct reading |
| 2 | Vernier Caliper (metric) | 1 mm | 50 VSD = 49 MSD | 0.02 mm | MSR + (V × 0.02) mm |
| 3 | Vernier Caliper (English) | 0.025 in (1/40″) | 40 VSD = 39 MSD | 0.025 mm (0.001″) | MSR + (V × 0.025) mm |
| 4 | Vernier Bevel Protractor | 1° (60′) | 12 VSD = 23 MSD | 5′ (five minutes) | MSR + (V × 5′) |
| 5 | Vernier Depth Gauge | 1 mm | 50 VSD = 49 MSD | 0.02 mm | MSR + (V × 0.02) mm |
| 6 | Vernier Height Gauge | 1 mm | 50 VSD = 49 MSD | 0.02 mm | MSR + (V × 0.02) mm |
| 7 | Micrometer (metric) | 0.5 mm | 50 div on thimble | 0.01 mm | MSR + (Thimble × 0.01) mm |
| 8 | Dial Gauge | 1 mm/rev | 100 div on dial | 0.01 mm | Dial reading × 0.01 mm |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Instruments

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> - **Vernier Bevel Protractor** — Inspecting tool angles (e.g., drill point angle 118°), angle of dovetail joints, taper on lathe, milling cutter geometry.
> - **Vernier Depth Gauge** — Depth of T-slots, blind holes, keyways, recessed cavities, and step heights in machine tools.
> - **Vernier Height Gauge** — Scribing parallel lines, layout work, finding centre of round jobs, and determining radii of arcs/curves using slip gauges.

### 2.5 Conceptual Logic Flow for the Three Experiments

1. **Angle of a Dovetail** — A dovetail is a flared, trapezoidal joint/slide. The included angle (typically 60° or 55°) is measured by seating the blades of the Vernier Bevel Protractor against the two sloped faces of the dovetail.
2. **Angle of a Taper** — A taper is a gradual reduction in diameter/length. The included angle between the tapered surface and the axis is measured by laying the bevel protractor on the tapered portion and rotating until full contact is achieved.
3. **Radius of a Circular Surface** — A vernier height gauge is used in conjunction with a surface plate and a known reference (slip gauge of fixed height). The reading difference, along with the chord length, gives the radius using the geometric relation $R = \frac{h}{2} + \frac{c^2}{8h}$, where $h$ is the sagitta (rise) and $c$ is the chord length.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Least Count of Vernier Bevel Protractor

**Step 1 — Identify the main scale division value.**
The main disc of the bevel protractor is graduated in degrees. So:

$$1 \text{ MSD} = 1° = 60 \text{ minutes of arc } (60')$$

**Step 2 — Identify the vernier scale relation.**
The vernier scale has **12 divisions** that span **23 main scale divisions**.

$$12 \text{ VSD} = 23°$$

**Step 3 — Compute the value of one VSD.**

$$\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ VSD} &= \frac{23°}{12} \\
&= 1.9166...° \\
&= 1° + 0.9166° \times 60' \\
&= 1° 55' \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the least count.**

$$\begin{aligned}
LC &= 1 \text{ MSD} - 1 \text{ VSD} \\
&= 60' - 55' \\
&= 5' \text{ (five minutes of arc)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Or equivalently:

$$LC = \frac{60'}{12} = 5'$$

This confirms the universal formula $LC = \frac{\text{1 MSD}}{n}$, where $n = 12$ for the bevel protractor.

### 3.2 Derivation of Least Count of Vernier Depth Gauge

**Step 1 — Identify the main scale division value.**

$$1 \text{ MSD} = 1 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the vernier scale relation.**

$$50 \text{ VSD} = 49 \text{ MSD} = 49 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the value of one VSD.**

$$1 \text{ VSD} = \frac{49 \text{ mm}}{50} = 0.98 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the least count.**

$$\begin{aligned}
LC &= 1 \text{ MSD} - 1 \text{ VSD} \\
&= 1.00 \text{ mm} - 0.98 \text{ mm} \\
&= 0.02 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Least Count of Vernier Height Gauge

The vernier height gauge uses an **identical vernier mechanism** to the depth gauge, so the derivation is exactly the same:

$$LC = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{50} = 0.02 \text{ mm}$$

### 3.4 Detailed Procedure — Experiment 1: Finding the Angle of a Dovetail

**Apparatus Required:** Vernier Bevel Protractor, workpiece with a dovetail, surface plate, cleaning cloth.

**Working Principle:**
The blades of the bevel protractor are seated against the two sloping surfaces of the dovetail. The angle enclosed between the two blades (read on the main + vernier scale) is the **included angle of the dovetail**.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. Clean the dovetail surfaces and the blades of the bevel protractor thoroughly.
2. Place the workpiece on a clean surface plate.
3. Slide the movable blade of the bevel protractor outward, so the angle between the two blades can be set to a value close to the expected dovetail angle.
4. Insert the blades into the dovetail slot, ensuring both blades make firm, even contact with the two sloping faces.
5. Apply gentle pressure and tap the side of the head gently with a finger to ensure no gap exists between the blades and the dovetail faces (use a feeler gauge to confirm zero gap if needed).
6. Tighten the locking clamp to freeze the reading.
7. Read the **Main Scale Reading (MSR)** — note the degree just before the vernier zero (e.g., 54° or 55°).
8. Read the **Vernier Coincidence (V)** — find the vernier line that aligns perfectly with any main scale line (e.g., 9th line).
9. Apply the formula: $\theta = \text{MSR} + (V \times 5')$.

**Worked Example:**
- Main Scale Reading: $55°$ (zero of vernier has crossed 55°)
- Vernier Coincidence: $V = 9$ (i.e., 9 × 5′ = 45′)
- Angle of dovetail:

$$\theta = 55° + 45' = 55° 45'$$

Or in decimal degrees:

$$\theta = 55 + \frac{45}{60} = 55.75°$$

### 3.5 Detailed Procedure — Experiment 2: Finding the Angle of a Taper

**Apparatus Required:** Vernier Bevel Protractor, tapered workpiece, surface plate, slip gauges (optional).

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. Clean the tapered surface and the blades.
2. Place the workpiece on a clean, flat surface plate.
3. Lay the body of the bevel protractor on the tapered surface such that the measuring edges of the blades lie along the tapered surface.
4. Adjust the angle by sliding the movable blade until both blades are in full contact with the taper.
5. Alternatively, stand the bevel protractor on a flat reference and bring the tapered surface against one blade.
6. Lock the clamp and read the main and vernier scales.
7. Apply the formula: $\theta = \text{MSR} + (V \times 5')$.

**Special Note on Taper Measurement:**
For a **morse taper** or **self-holding taper** (such as on a lathe tailstock), the standard measured angle is typically:
- **Morse Taper:** ~1° 25′ 56″ per side (or 1.49°), included angle ≈ 2° 51′ 52″.
- **Brown & Sharpe Taper:** ~1° 6′ per side.

### 3.6 Detailed Procedure — Experiment 3: Finding the Radius of a Circular Surface

**Apparatus Required:** Vernier Height Gauge, surface plate, slip gauge set (or a known-height reference block), V-block (optional), the circular workpiece.

**Method 1 — Sagitta Method (using slip gauges):**

**Geometric Basis:**
Consider a circle of radius $R$. A chord of length $c$ is drawn, and the **sagitta** (rise) is $h$. The relation is:

$$R = \frac{h}{2} + \frac{c^{2}}{8h}$$

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. Clean the surface plate and place it on a sturdy, vibration-free table.
2. Mount the Vernier Height Gauge on the surface plate and check for zero error.
3. Place a **slip gauge of known height** $H$ (e.g., $H = 10$ mm) on the surface plate, near the circular workpiece.
4. Bring the scriber tip of the height gauge to touch the top of the slip gauge. Note the reading: $R_1 = H + \text{zero offset}$.
5. Now bring the scriber tip to touch the **highest point of the circular (curved) surface**. Note the reading: $R_2$.
6. The **sagitta** (rise of the arc above the chord):

$$h = R_2 - R_1$$

7. Measure the **chord length** $c$ using a vernier caliper — the chord is the distance across the circle at the level corresponding to the slip gauge height.
8. Apply the formula:

$$R = \frac{h}{2} + \frac{c^{2}}{8h}$$

**Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical):**

Given:
- Slip gauge height $H = 10.00$ mm
- Height gauge reading at top of slip gauge: $R_1 = 10.00$ mm
- Height gauge reading at top of arc: $R_2 = 18.00$ mm
- Chord length measured: $c = 40.00$ mm

**Step 1 — Sagitta calculation:**

$$h = R_2 - R_1 = 18.00 - 10.00 = 8.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{h}{2} + \frac{c^{2}}{8h} \\
&= \frac{8.00}{2} + \frac{(40.00)^{2}}{8 \times 8.00} \\
&= 4.00 + \frac{1600}{64} \\
&= 4.00 + 25.00 \\
&= 29.00 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Hence, the radius of the circular surface is 29.00 mm.**

**Method 2 — Direct Method (when the height of the highest point is known):**

If the height $H$ of the curved surface above the base and the **base diameter** $D$ are known:

$$R = \frac{H}{2} + \frac{D^{2}}{8H}$$

This is identical in form, with $h$ replaced by $H$ and chord $c$ replaced by base diameter $D$.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Procedure Summary

| Experiment | Instrument Used | Formula | Key Step |
|------------|-----------------|---------|----------|
| Angle of dovetail | Vernier Bevel Protractor | $\theta = \text{MSR} + (V \times 5')$ | Seat blades on both slopes |
| Angle of taper | Vernier Bevel Protractor | $\theta = \text{MSR} + (V \times 5')$ | Ensure full blade contact |
| Radius of arc | Vernier Height Gauge + Slip Gauges | $R = \frac{h}{2} + \frac{c^{2}}{8h}$ | Measure sagitta $h$ and chord $c$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Vernier Bevel Protractor

<!-- 
Mermaid safeguards followed:
- All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters
- No markdown formatting (bold, italics) inside quoted node labels
- Reserved words avoided as standalone node IDs
-->

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Main Circular Disc with 0 to 90 to 0 to 90 degree scale] --> B1[Fixed Blade attached to disc centre]
    A1 --> C1[Movable Blade pivoting on disc]
    C1 --> D1[Sliding Vernier Scale graduated 0 to 60 minutes]
    D1 --> E1[Locking Clamp for fixing vernier position]
    A1 --> F1[Base Plate with flat seating face]
    E1 --> G1[Output Angular Reading in degrees and minutes]
    F1 --> G1
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Vernier Depth Gauge Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A2[Main Scale thin steel rule graduated in mm] --> B2[Sliding Head with vernier carriage]
    B2 --> C2[Flat Reference Base plate at top]
    C2 --> D2[Workpiece Slot or Hole whose depth is to be measured]
    B2 --> E2[Locking Screw for sliding head]
    A2 --> F2[Output Depth Reading MSR plus V times LC]
    E2 --> F2
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Vernier Height Gauge with Scribing Facility

```mermaid
graph TD
    A3[Heavy Stable Base with flat bottom] --> B3[Vertical Main Column graduated in mm]
    B3 --> C3[Sliding Carriage with vernier scale LC equals 0 point 02 mm]
    C3 --> D3[Measuring Jaws for height measurement]
    C3 --> E3[Scribing Point with clamp]
    E3 --> F3[Surface Plate reference base]
    D3 --> G3[Workpiece circular surface or flat face]
    F3 --> G3
    G3 --> H3[Output Height Reading used to compute radius R]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow — Three Experiments Combined

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Start: Identify Dimension to Measure] --> P1{Angle or Radius}
    P1 --> P2[Angle of Dovetail] 
    P1 --> P3[Angle of Taper]
    P1 --> P4[Radius of Circular Surface]
    P2 --> Q1[Seat Bevel Protractor blades on dovetail faces]
    Q1 --> Q2[Read MSR plus V times 5 minutes]
    P3 --> R1[Align Bevel Protractor blades on taper surface]
    R1 --> R2[Read MSR plus V times 5 minutes]
    P4 --> S1[Place slip gauge on surface plate]
    S1 --> S2[Measure height of slip gauge and arc top using height gauge]
    S2 --> S3[Compute sagitta h and measure chord c with vernier caliper]
    S3 --> S4[Apply R equals h by 2 plus c squared by 8h]
    Q2 --> Z[Final Report with average of 3 readings]
    R2 --> Z
    S4 --> Z
```

### 4.5 Visual Concept of Sagitta Geometry (Described in Text)

Imagine a circle resting on a flat surface plate. The **chord** is the imaginary line connecting the two points where the circle touches the chord level. The **sagitta** $h$ is the vertical distance from the chord (the slip-gauge height) up to the **highest point of the arc** (the crown). The height of the crown above the chord determines how curved the surface is — a small sagitta means a large radius (gentle curve), while a large sagitta means a small radius (sharp curve). The formula $R = \frac{h}{2} + \frac{c^{2}}{8h}$ mathematically captures this geometric relationship.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define metrology. List any four common measuring instruments used in an engineering workshop. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Metrology is the science of measurement applied to manufacturing and engineering processes, dealing with methods, instruments, and standards used to determine physical dimensions such as length, angle, and geometric form of a workpiece.

Four common workshop measuring instruments are:
1. **Steel Rule** — for direct linear measurement (LC = 1 mm).
2. **Vernier Caliper** — for internal, external, and depth measurements (LC = 0.02 mm).
3. **Vernier Bevel Protractor** — for angular measurements (LC = 5 minutes of arc).
4. **Micrometer** — for high-precision linear measurements (LC = 0.01 mm).

*(Other valid answers: Vernier Depth Gauge, Vernier Height Gauge, Dial Gauge, Feeler Gauge, Radius Gauge, Slip Gauges, Sine Bar.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State the least count of a Vernier Bevel Protractor and derive it. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The least count of a Vernier Bevel Protractor is **5 minutes of arc (5′)**.

**Derivation:**
- Value of 1 main scale division (MSD) on the main disc = $1° = 60$ minutes of arc.
- The vernier scale has 12 divisions that span 23 main scale divisions.
- Therefore: $12 \text{ VSD} = 23°$, which gives $1 \text{ VSD} = \frac{23°}{12} = 1° 55'$.

$$LC = 1 \text{ MSD} - 1 \text{ VSD} = 60' - 55' = 5'$$

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern, Module Experiment 2]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Vernier Bevel Protractor**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the procedure to find the **angle of a dovetail** using a Vernier Bevel Protractor. A dovetail has main scale reading 54° and vernier coincidence at 9. Calculate the included angle in degrees and minutes. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of a Vernier Bevel Protractor (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
- It consists of a circular **main disc** graduated from 0° to 90° on either side of the centre zero, giving a total range of 0° to 360°.
- A **fixed blade** is rigidly attached to the centre of the disc.
- A **movable blade** rotates about the same centre and carries the **vernier scale** (12 divisions: 0–60 minutes).
- A **locking clamp** is provided to fix the movable blade in position.
- The whole assembly is mounted on a flat **base plate** that rests on the workpiece or surface plate.
- The vernier scale divides each main scale degree into 12 equal parts of 5 minutes each.

**Working:**
- The blades are placed in contact with the two surfaces whose included angle is to be measured.
- The movable blade is rotated until both blades are in full contact.
- The locking clamp is tightened.
- The **main scale reading (MSR)** is noted in degrees.
- The vernier division coinciding with any main scale line gives the **vernier coincidence $V$**.
- The angle is computed as:

$$\theta = \text{MSR} + (V \times 5')$$

**(b) Finding Angle of a Dovetail (7 marks):**

**Procedure:**
1. Clean the dovetail surfaces and the blades of the bevel protractor.
2. Place the workpiece on a clean surface plate.
3. Insert the two blades into the dovetail slot, seating one blade on each sloping face.
4. Apply light pressure to ensure no gap (verify with a feeler gauge).
5. Tighten the locking clamp.
6. Read MSR and vernier coincidence.

**Calculation:**
- Main Scale Reading (MSR) = $54°$
- Vernier Coincidence $V = 9$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Angle} \theta &= \text{MSR} + (V \times 5') \\
&= 54° + (9 \times 5') \\
&= 54° + 45' \\
&= 54° 45' \\
\end{aligned}$$

**In decimal degrees:**

$$\theta = 54 + \frac{45}{60} = 54.75°$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Module Internal Choice — Alternative)*

**(a)** Explain the construction and least count derivation of a **Vernier Depth Gauge**. List two applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the procedure to find the **radius of a circular (curved) surface** using a Vernier Height Gauge and slip gauges. In an experiment, the height of a slip gauge is 15.00 mm, the height gauge reading on the arc top is 24.50 mm, and the chord length measured is 38.00 mm. Calculate the radius. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Vernier Depth Gauge (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
- A thin **main scale (steel rule)** graduated in mm, typically 0–200 mm or 0–300 mm long.
- A **sliding head** with a flat reference base carries the **vernier scale** (50 VSD = 49 MSD).
- A **locking screw** is provided to fix the sliding head position.
- The instrument resembles a vernier caliper but with the jaws replaced by a flat base (top) and a measuring rule (bottom).

**Least Count Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ MSD} &= 1 \text{ mm} \\
50 \text{ VSD} &= 49 \text{ MSD} = 49 \text{ mm} \\
1 \text{ VSD} &= \frac{49}{50} = 0.98 \text{ mm} \\
LC &= 1 \text{ MSD} - 1 \text{ VSD} = 1.00 - 0.98 = 0.02 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Two Applications:**
1. Measuring the depth of blind holes, slots, and recesses in a workpiece.
2. Measuring the height of shoulders, steps, and the depth of keyways in machine components.

**(b) Radius of a Circular Surface (7 marks):**

**Procedure:**
1. Place the surface plate on a steady table and clean it.
2. Mount the Vernier Height Gauge on the surface plate and check for zero error.
3. Place a slip gauge of known height ($H = 15.00$ mm) on the surface plate next to the circular workpiece.
4. Bring the height gauge scriber to touch the top of the slip gauge; note the reading.
5. Bring the scriber to touch the **highest point (crown)** of the curved surface; note the reading.
6. The sagitta $h$ is the difference between the two readings.
7. Measure the **chord length** $c$ across the curved surface at the level of the slip gauge using a vernier caliper.
8. Apply the formula:

$$R = \frac{h}{2} + \frac{c^{2}}{8h}$$

**Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= R_2 - R_1 = 24.50 - 15.00 = 9.50 \text{ mm} \\
c &= 38.00 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{9.50}{2} + \frac{(38.00)^{2}}{8 \times 9.50} \\
&= 4.75 + \frac{1444}{76} \\
&= 4.75 + 19.00 \\
&= 23.75 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Hence, the radius of the circular surface = 23.75 mm.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units in the angle calculation** — Always write the answer in the form `degrees° minutes'`, e.g., `54° 45'`, not just `54.75`. KTU examiners specifically look for the `°` and `'` notation.
> 2. **Using the wrong chord in the radius formula** — The chord $c$ must be measured at the level corresponding to the slip-gauge height, NOT at the base of the circular surface. Many students incorrectly use the base diameter, which gives a wrong answer.
> 3. **Not averaging multiple readings** — KTU values require a minimum of **three readings** taken at different positions on the dovetail/taper/arc and the **mean value** reported. Reporting only one reading costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing "least count" with "accuracy"** — Least count is the smallest *measurable* division, not the accuracy (which depends on instrument quality, parallax, and zero error). Examiners may deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 5. **Skipping the zero-error correction** — Always check and apply the zero error before recording the final reading. A common 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **In the dovetail/taper experiment, not verifying blade contact** — A loose blade contact gives a reading error. The correct procedure is to verify with a feeler gauge and re-take the reading.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points for KTU Viva & Exams:**

- **Metrology** = science of measurement; in workshops, it focuses on linear, angular, and geometric dimensions.
- **Vernier Bevel Protractor:** LC = **5 minutes of arc**; 12 VSD = 23 MSD; range 0°–360°.
- **Vernier Depth Gauge:** LC = **0.02 mm**; 50 VSD = 49 MSD; measures depth of slots/holes.
- **Vernier Height Gauge:** LC = **0.02 mm**; used on a **surface plate**; measures heights and scribes lines; can determine radius of arcs.
- **Universal LC formula:** $LC = \frac{1 \text{ MSD}}{n}$, where $n$ = number of vernier divisions.
- **Universal measurement formula:** Measurement = MSR + (Vernier coincidence × LC).
- **Dovetail angle** is the included angle between the two sloping surfaces of a dovetail joint.
- **Taper angle** is the included angle of a tapered (gradually reducing) surface.
- **Radius of circular surface:** $R = \frac{h}{2} + \frac{c^{2}}{8h}$ — sagitta formula.
- **Sagitta $h$** = difference between the height-gauge reading at the crown of the arc and the reading at the slip-gauge height.
- **Chord $c$** = linear distance measured at the level of the slip gauge, across the circular surface.
- Always take **at least 3 readings** and report the **mean value**.
- Always **clean the instrument and workpiece** before measurement to avoid errors.
- Always **check for zero error** before using a vernier instrument.
- **Use a feeler gauge** to verify zero gap between the blade and the workpiece surface for angular measurements.
- For the **dovetail experiment**, the typical standard angles are **60°** (most common) and **55°**.
- For the **taper experiment**, the bevel protractor reading gives the **included angle**; half of it is the **taper per side**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly (Demonstration only): Dissembling and assembling of Cylinder and piston assembly, Tail stock assembly, Bicycle, Pump or any other machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCESL106 - ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly (Demonstration only): Dissembling and assembling of Cylinder and piston assembly, Tail stock assembly, Bicycle, Pump or any other machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assembly (in Engineering Workshop context):** Assembly is the systematic process of joining individual manufactured components and sub-assemblies together in a predefined sequence using appropriate tools, fasteners, and techniques to form a complete, functional machine or mechanism that meets the required design specifications and operational tolerances.

**Disassembly (Dissembling)** is the reverse process — the controlled, sequential separation of components from an assembled machine, typically performed for:
- Maintenance and servicing
- Inspection and fault diagnosis
- Replacement of worn-out parts
- Cleaning and lubrication
- Reassembly after refurbishment

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (GCESL106 – Engineering Workshop), Module Experiment 2 is a **demonstration-only module**, meaning students observe the complete teardown and build-up of four classic engineering assemblies:
1. **Cylinder and Piston Assembly** (IC Engine / Compressor)
2. **Tailstock Assembly** (Lathe machine)
3. **Bicycle** (Domestic mechanical assembly)
4. **Water Pump** (Centrifugal / Reciprocating)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego + Doctor" Intuition

Imagine a **Lego castle** (Assembly) versus **a doctor performing surgery** (Disassembly):

- **Assembly = Building the Lego castle:** You follow the instruction manual, snap bricks together in the correct order (crankshaft → connecting rod → piston → cylinder head), use the right tool (torque wrench, not a hammer!), and check at each step that pieces fit snugly.
- **Disassembly = The doctor opening up a patient:** You do NOT just rip things apart. You diagnose first, document the order, label every part, and place them in a clean tray. If you yank a piston out without marking its orientation, you'll never get the rings back correctly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The objective of this experiment is **not** to memorize procedures. It is to develop:
> - **Visual literacy** in recognizing engineering components
> - **Tool selection skills** for mechanical work
> - **Sequential thinking** (every assembly has a *mandatory* order)
> - **Safety awareness** in handling pressurized, rotating, or hot systems

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Bolded for Exam Reference)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Fit** | The degree of looseness or tightness between two mating parts (clearance, transition, interference). |
| **Tolerance** | The permissible limit of variation in a physical dimension. |
| **Gasket** | A mechanical seal (paper, rubber, copper, or composite) that fills the gap between mating surfaces. |
| **Fastener** | A hardware device (bolt, nut, screw, stud) that mechanically joins two or more parts. |
| **Piston Ring** | An expandable split ring fitted into the piston groove to seal combustion gases. |
| **Bearing** | A machine element that constrains relative motion and reduces friction between moving parts. |
| **Dead Centre** | The fixed centre of a lathe (headstock) or movable centre (tailstock) used to support the workpiece. |
| **Wear Pattern** | The visible trace of friction on a contact surface, used to diagnose alignment and lubrication issues. |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for remembering the 4 assemblies — "C-T-B-P" → "Cricket-Tail-Bat-Pump"**
> (Cylinder, Tailstock, Bicycle, Pump)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal 5-Phase Workflow of Any Assembly/Disassembly

Every professional mechanical procedure — whether it's a car engine, a lathe tailstock, or a bicycle — follows the same five logical phases. Master this framework once, apply it to all four KTU assemblies.

### Phase 1 — Preparation & Workspace Setup
- Clean the workbench; remove unrelated tools
- Lay out a **soft rubber mat or clean cloth** to prevent scratching parts
- Arrange **labeled trays** (A, B, C...) in sequence
- Keep the **service manual / exploded view diagram** open at hand

### Phase 2 — Tool & Safety Check
- Inspect tools for damage (worn screwdriver tips, cracked hammer handles)
- Wear **PPE**: safety glasses, gloves (for sharp parts), closed-toe shoes
- **De-energize** any machine (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic)
- For engines: ensure the system is **cold and depressurized**

### Phase 3 — Disassembly (Teardown)
- Follow the **reverse of the assembly order**
- Photograph or sketch the layout **before** each step
- **Mark mating parts** (e.g., punch marks on bearing caps) to ensure correct orientation during reassembly
- Keep fasteners (bolts, nuts, washers) with their parent component

### Phase 4 — Cleaning & Inspection
- Clean all parts with **kerosene / diesel / approved solvent**
- Inspect for: **cracks, wear, corrosion, deformation, broken teeth**
- Measure critical dimensions (bore diameter, piston OD, journal diameter) with vernier/micrometer
- Replace any part failing inspection — **never reuse a questionable gasket or safety-critical fastener**

### Phase 5 — Reassembly & Testing
- Lubricate moving parts with the **correct grade of oil/grease** before fitting
- Torque bolts in the **correct sequence** (usually a criss-cross/star pattern) to the **specified torque value**
- Reinstall the **new gasket** (never reuse old gaskets on critical joints)
- Test-run the machine under no-load first, then under progressive load

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Tables

### 2.2.1 Standard Tools Required Across All Four Assemblies

| Tool | Primary Use | KTU Assemblies Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Spanner Set (Ring + Open-end)** | Tightening/loosening hex nuts and bolts | All four |
| **Screwdriver Set (Flat + Phillips)** | Slotted and cross-head screws | Bicycle, Pump |
| **Allen Key (Hex Key) Set** | Socket-head (Allen) bolts | Tailstock, Pump |
| **Hammer (Ball-pein / Soft)** | Tapping, aligning | All four |
| **Pliers (Combination / Circlip)** | Gripping, removing circlips/retaining rings | Cylinder-Piston, Pump |
| **Piston Ring Expander / Pliers** | Safely expanding piston rings for installation | Cylinder-Piston |
| **Torque Wrench** | Applying precise tightening force | Cylinder-Piston, Tailstock, Pump |
| **Vernier Caliper / Micrometer** | Measuring diameters and clearances | Cylinder-Piston, Tailstock |
| **Feeler Gauge** | Measuring small gaps (e.g., valve clearance) | Cylinder-Piston, Tailstock |
| **Puller (Bearing / Gear Puller)** | Removing bearings and gears without damage | Cylinder-Piston, Tailstock, Pump |
| **Scraper / Wire Brush** | Cleaning carbon deposits, rust | Cylinder-Piston, Pump |

> [!WARNING]
> **No vertical pipes in tables — KTU formatting rule.** All notation is text-based (e.g., "diameter" not "diameter").

### 2.2.2 Critical Torque & Clearance Specifications (Typical Engineering Values)

| Assembly | Critical Specification | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cylinder Head Bolts | Tightening torque | 60 to 90 | N·m |
| Piston-to-Bore Clearance | Diameter difference (piston smaller than bore) | 0.02 to 0.05 | mm |
| Piston Ring End Gap | Gap when ring placed in bore | 0.15 to 0.40 | mm |
| Connecting Rod Big-end Bearing | Clearance (diameter) | 0.02 to 0.07 | mm |
| Tailstock Spindle Quill Fit | Quill sliding clearance | 0.01 to 0.03 | mm |
| Bicycle Crank Bolt | Tightening torque | 30 to 40 | N·m |
| Pump Impeller Clearance | Axial clearance | 0.5 to 2.0 | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> These values are **typical industry values** for medium-sized components. Always refer to the **manufacturer's service manual** for exact values. KTU examiners may test your awareness of the *concept* of tolerance rather than exact numbers.

### 2.2.3 Common Failure Modes — What to Look For

| Component | Symptom | Probable Cause |
|---|---|---|
| Piston | Scoring marks, oval wear | Lubrication failure, dirt ingestion |
| Cylinder Liner | Vertical scratches, taper at top | Ring breakage, overheating |
| Piston Rings | Sticking in grooves, broken ends | Carbon buildup, poor oil |
| Tailstock Quill | Rough sliding, scored surface | Lack of lubrication, dirt |
| Bicycle Chain | Stretched links, skipping | Wear, poor lubrication |
| Pump Impeller | Cavitation pitting, broken vanes | Dry running, suction blockage |

---

## 2.3 Why This Topic Matters in Real Engineering

The skill set developed in this experiment is the **direct foundation of mechanical maintenance engineering** — the discipline that keeps:
- Aircraft flying (engine borescope inspections)
- Power plants running (turbine disassembly)
- Cars on the road (engine overhauls)
- Factory production lines alive (machine tool maintenance)

According to ISO 9001 and standard industrial practice, **any technician who cannot systematically disassemble and reassemble a machine is not certified to maintain it**. The KTU workshop module plants this seed early in your engineering career.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is **exhaustive by design**. Every step of the four KTU-mandated assembly demonstrations is detailed in full, including pre-conditions, actions, tool selections, and post-condition checks.

---

## 3.1 ASSEMBLY 1 — CYLINDER AND PISTON ASSEMBLY (IC Engine)

### 3.1.1 Components Identification

Before any procedure, identify and list:
1. **Cylinder block / liner** — the stationary bore
2. **Piston** — the cylindrical slider with ring grooves
3. **Piston rings** — typically 3 rings (top compression, second compression, oil control)
4. **Wrist pin (Gudgeon pin)** — connects piston to connecting rod
5. **Connecting rod** — links piston to crankshaft
6. **Circlips (Snap rings)** — retain the wrist pin
7. **Cylinder head** — closes the top of the bore
8. **Head gasket** — seals the head-to-block interface
9. **Spark plug / Injector** — for ignition/fuel
10. **Inlet and exhaust valves** — for gas exchange

### 3.1.2 Disassembly Procedure (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Safety and Setup**
- Ensure engine is **cold** and **depressurized**
- Disconnect battery (electrical safety)
- Place engine on a clean, flat workbench with a drip tray beneath

**Step 2 — External Component Removal**
- Remove the **valve cover / rocker arm assembly** using a spanner
- Unbolt and remove the **cylinder head** in a **star/criss-cross sequence** (center-out) to prevent warping
- Lift the head **straight up**; do not pry sideways (gasket damage risk)

**Step 3 — Piston-Cylinder Separation**
- Rotate the crankshaft (manually) until the piston is at **Top Dead Centre (TDC)**
- Cover the exposed cylinder bore with a **clean rag** to prevent debris falling in
- Push the connecting rod cap bolts out using a spanner
- Remove the **big-end cap** (note the cap-number match-mark; it must return to the same rod)

**Step 4 — Piston Extraction**
- Push the connecting rod and piston **upward and out** of the cylinder bore gently
- If stuck, **do not hammer**; use a wooden dowel and a soft mallet from below
- Once out, place the piston-rod assembly on a padded surface

**Step 5 — Piston Ring and Wrist Pin Removal**
- Use the **piston ring expander tool** to carefully slip each ring off the piston
- **Note the orientation and order** — compression rings are not interchangeable
- Remove the **circlips** from the piston using circlip pliers
- Push the **wrist pin** out using a drift and hammer (or a wrist pin puller)
- Support the piston internally to avoid cracking the pin boss

**Step 6 — Cleaning, Inspection, and Marking**
- Scrape carbon from the piston crown with a **wooden scraper** (never metal — it scratches the soft aluminum)
- Clean the cylinder bore with a **honing brush or soft cloth**
- Measure piston OD and bore ID with a micrometer and bore gauge
- Check ring end-gap by placing each ring in the bore and measuring with feeler gauge
- Mark all parts for orientation

### 3.1.3 Reassembly Procedure (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Wrist Pin Installation**
- Heat the piston to **~80 to 100 degrees C** in hot water (aluminum expands; this allows interference fit)
- Apply assembly grease to the wrist pin
- Insert the wrist pin through the piston boss and connecting rod small-end
- Install **new circlips** (the old ones lose tension); ensure they seat fully in their grooves

**Step 2 — Piston Ring Installation**
- Stagger the ring end-gaps at **120 degree intervals** around the piston circumference
  * Top compression ring gap at 12 o'clock
  * Second compression ring gap at 4 o'clock
  * Oil control ring gap at 8 o'clock
- Use the **ring expander** to slip each ring into its groove — never over-stretch
- Verify rings rotate freely in their grooves

**Step 3 — Piston-Cylinder Insertion**
- Coat the cylinder bore and piston with **clean engine oil**
- Use a **piston ring compressor tool** to squeeze the rings flush with the piston OD
- Insert the piston-rod assembly into the bore, **arrow or "FRONT" mark on piston crown facing the front of the engine**
- Tap the piston **gently** with the wooden handle of a hammer until it slides past the rings
- Remove the ring compressor

**Step 4 — Connecting Rod to Crankshaft**
- Lubricate the crankshaft journal and bearing shell
- Lower the big-end onto the journal
- Install the **big-end cap** with new bolts (or re-used, but torqued correctly)
- Torque to manufacturer spec in **two stages** (e.g., 30 N·m, then 60 N·m)

**Step 5 — Cylinder Head Installation**
- Fit a **new head gasket** (dry, no sealant on modern multi-layer steel gaskets)
- Lower the head onto the block; align with dowel pins
- Hand-tighten all head bolts first
- Torque in the **prescribed sequence** (usually center-outward) in **three stages** (e.g., 30, 60, 90 N·m)
- Reinstall valve train components

**Step 6 — Final Checks**
- Rotate crankshaft by hand 2 full revolutions — should turn smoothly
- Check for free piston travel with no binding
- Reconnect auxiliary systems (intake, exhaust, electrical)

---

## 3.2 ASSEMBLY 2 — TAILSTOCK ASSEMBLY (Centre Lathe)

### 3.2.1 Function of the Tailstock

The tailstock is the **movable, opposing support** of a lathe, mounted on the bedways opposite the headstock. It supports the free end of long workpieces (via a dead centre) and holds drilling/milling/boring tools via its **quill** (a sliding spindle).

### 3.2.2 Key Components

1. **Base** — slides on the lathe bed
2. **Body / Saddle** — houses the quill mechanism
3. **Quill (Spindle)** — the hollow sliding shaft (holds MT — Morse Taper — tooling)
4. **Dead Centre (MT3 / MT4)** — hardened point that supports the workpiece
5. **Handwheel and Leadscrew** — for quill advancement
6. **Locking Levers** — clamp the quill and the body to the bed
7. **Graduated Dial** — for quill feed indication
8. **Bearing Surfaces** — bronze or needle bearings for smooth quill motion

### 3.2.3 Disassembly Procedure

**Step 1 — Lockout and Removal**
- Lock the tailstock to the bed (loosen bed clamp, slide off the lathe for the workbench)
- Place on a clean flat surface

**Step 2 — Quill Withdrawal**
- Loosen the **quill locking lever**
- Crank the handwheel to push the quill out fully
- Remove the **dead centre** (twist out from the Morse taper)

**Step 3 — Quill Extraction from the Body**
- Unscrew the **rear cap / quill nut** that retains the quill
- Slide the quill out the front of the body
- Note any shims or adjustment washers

**Step 4 — Internal Disassembly**
- Remove the **handwheel** (usually held by a nut and washer)
- Unscrew the **leadscrew** from the body
- Extract the **bearing shells or needle bearings**
- Note the order: bottom bearing, spacer, thrust bearing, top bearing

**Step 5 — Cleaning**
- Clean the quill bore with kerosene and a lint-free cloth
- Clean all bearings and threaded parts
- Inspect the quill for scoring, the bore for ovality, the Morse taper for wear or chips

### 3.2.4 Reassembly Procedure

**Step 1 — Bearing and Quill Installation**
- Lubricate the quill OD and the internal bore
- Install bottom bearing, spacer, thrust bearing, top bearing
- Slide the quill into the body from the front
- Install the rear cap with shims (shims control quill drag and preload)
- Tighten the cap until the quill slides with slight drag (too loose = chatter, too tight = binding)

**Step 2 — Leadscrew and Handwheel**
- Reinstall the leadscrew; ensure threads engage smoothly with the quill nut
- Reinstall the handwheel and tighten the retaining nut
- Fit the graduated dial (zero it against a reference mark)

**Step 3 — Dead Centre Installation**
- Clean the Morse taper on both the quill and the dead centre
- Press the dead centre in firmly (a sharp tap with a soft mallet on the centre's shank)
- The taper should seat with a dull ring, not a dull thud

**Step 4 — Functional Test**
- Slide the tailstock back onto the lathe bed; lock to bed
- Verify quill advances and retracts smoothly
- Check the dead centre alignment with the headstock centre (use a dial indicator for precision)
- Test quill locking lever: must hold firmly under drilling load

---

## 3.3 ASSEMBLY 3 — BICYCLE

### 3.3.1 Major Subsystems

1. **Frame** — the structural skeleton
2. **Drivetrain** — chain, chainring, freewheel/cassette, derailleurs
3. **Wheels** — hub, spokes, rim, tyre, tube
4. **Braking System** — rim brakes (V-brake/caliper) or disc brakes
5. **Steering** — handlebar, stem, fork, headset bearings
6. **Saddle and Seatpost**
7. **Pedals and Crank**

### 3.3.2 Disassembly Procedure

**Step 1 — Wheels**
- Open the **quick-release skewers** or loosen the axle nuts
- For rim brakes: release the brake cable noodle to widen the brake pads
- For disc brakes: be careful not to squeeze the brake lever after wheel removal (pistons will clamp)
- Slide wheels out of the dropouts

**Step 2 — Drivetrain**
- Loosen the **rear derailleur cable** at the pinch bolt
- Shift the chain onto the smallest cog
- Use a **chain breaker tool** to push the chain pin and separate the chain
- Remove the **chainring bolts** (often an Allen key + wrench combination) to remove the chainring
- Remove the **crank arms** using a crank puller tool
- Unscrew the **bottom bracket** (left and right cups)

**Step 3 — Brakes and Cables**
- Unthread the brake cables
- Remove brake calipers by unscrewing the mounting bolts
- Note the **pad orientation** (front pads ≠ rear pads on some models)

**Step 4 — Steering**
- Loosen the **stem bolts** and remove the handlebar
- Loosen the **headset top cap** and stem
- Slide the fork down out of the head tube
- Extract the **headset bearing races and balls**; note orientation

**Step 5 — Seatpost and Saddle**
- Loosen the **seatpost clamp** and slide the post out (apply penetrating oil if seized)
- Unscrew the **saddle rail clamps** to remove the saddle

### 3.3.3 Reassembly Procedure

**Step 1 — Headset and Fork**
- Install the **lower bearing race** onto the fork crown
- Slide the fork up into the head tube
- Install the **upper bearings, top race, and top cap**
- Adjust the headset: tighten the top cap until no play, then align the stem and tighten the stem bolts

**Step 2 — Bottom Bracket and Cranks**
- Apply grease to the bottom bracket threads
- Install the **bottom bracket cups** (right cup tighter, left cup reverse-thread on some bikes)
- Apply grease to the **crank spindle square taper or splines**
- Install the drive-side crank; torque to spec
- Install the **crank bolt** with washer
- Repeat for the non-drive-side

**Step 3 — Chainring and Chain**
- Mount the **chainring(s)** onto the crank spider; align with the bolt holes
- Torque the chainring bolts in a **star pattern**
- Thread the chain around the chainring and the smallest cog
- Use the chain breaker to install and lock the master link
- Adjust rear derailleur **limit screws (H and L)** and the **B-tension screw**
- Set cable tension via the barrel adjuster

**Step 4 — Brakes**
- Mount the brake calipers; do not fully tighten yet
- Install the **brake pads** with the toe-in aligned (front of pad just touches the rim slightly before rear)
- Squeeze the brake lever to centre the caliper
- Tighten the mounting bolts
- Set cable tension; verify full pad-to-rim contact with firm lever pressure

**Step 5 — Wheels and Final Checks**
- Install wheels; close the quick-release firmly
- For rim brakes, re-engage the cable noodle
- Inflate tyres to the **sidewall-specified pressure** (printed on the tyre)
- Spin wheels to check for **true** (no lateral wobble) and **dish**
- Test all functions: shifting, braking, steering, pedalling

---

## 3.4 ASSEMBLY 4 — WATER PUMP (Centrifugal)

### 3.4.1 Components

1. **Casing (Volute)** — the spiral housing that converts velocity to pressure
2. **Impeller** — the rotating vaned disc that imparts energy to the water
3. **Shaft** — transmits torque from the motor to the impeller
4. **Mechanical Seal** — prevents water leaking along the shaft
5. **Bearings** — support the shaft at both ends
6. **Suction and Discharge Ports**
7. **Motor Coupling or Mounting Frame**
8. **Gaskets and O-rings**

### 3.4.2 Disassembly Procedure

**Step 1 — Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)**
- Isolate the electrical supply; lock the breaker open
- Close the **suction and discharge valves**
- Drain the pump casing via the **drain plug**
- Allow the pump to cool if it was running

**Step 2 — Casing Removal**
- Unbolt the **casing cover** bolts in a criss-cross pattern
- Lift the casing cover off carefully (it may be heavy)
- Withdraw the **impeller** by gripping the shaft behind it (or use a puller if corroded)

**Step 3 — Shaft and Seal Disassembly**
- Loosen the **mechanical seal** set screws (if present)
- Slide the **seal seat** off the shaft
- Loosen the **bear-end frame / bearing housing** bolts
- Pull the **shaft assembly** (with bearings and seal) out of the frame

**Step 4 — Bearing Removal**
- Use a **bearing puller** to extract the bearings from the shaft
- Note the order: bearing → spacer → bearing → circlip
- Inspect the shaft for wear, the impeller for pitting, the seal faces for scoring

### 3.4.3 Reassembly Procedure

**Step 1 — New Mechanical Seal**
- The mechanical seal is **consumable** — always install a new one
- Clean the seal housing with a lint-free cloth (any debris destroys the lapped faces)
- Lubricate the seal's elastomer (O-ring) with soapy water (not oil, which attacks some rubbers)
- Press the **stationary face** into the housing squarely
- Slide the **rotating face** onto the shaft with light grease on the shaft

**Step 2 — Shaft and Bearings**
- Press the bearings onto the shaft using a bearing press or a suitable sleeve (never hammer directly)
- Install the **circlips** to locate the bearings axially
- Lower the shaft assembly into the bearing housing
- Torque the bearing housing bolts evenly

**Step 3 — Impeller Installation**
- Slide the impeller onto the shaft
- Set the **axial impeller clearance** using feeler gauge (typical 0.5 to 2.0 mm)
- Lock the impeller nut (often with a tab washer to prevent loosening)

**Step 4 — Casing Reassembly**
- Place a **new casing gasket** on the volute
- Lower the casing cover; hand-tighten all bolts
- Torque the casing bolts in a **criss-cross pattern** to avoid leaks

**Step 5 — Priming and Testing**
- **Prime the pump** (fill the casing with water) — dry running destroys the mechanical seal in seconds
- Open the suction valve; close the discharge valve momentarily
- Start the motor; check rotation direction (impeller is usually marked with an arrow)
- Slowly open the discharge valve; listen for abnormal noise or vibration
- Check for leaks at the seal, the casing joint, and the pipe flanges
- Monitor bearing temperature for the first hour of operation

---

## 3.5 Cross-Assembly Comparison Matrix (KTU-Favourite Table)

| Aspect | Cylinder-Piston | Tailstock | Bicycle | Water Pump |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Key Rotating/Sliding Part** | Piston in bore | Quill in body | Crank-pedal-wheel | Impeller-shaft |
| **Critical Clearance** | 0.02 to 0.05 mm | 0.01 to 0.03 mm | Bearings preload | 0.5 to 2.0 mm |
| **Primary Sealing** | Piston rings + head gasket | None (mechanical fit) | Grease in bearings | Mechanical seal + gasket |
| **Lubricant** | Engine oil (splash) | Grease / way oil | Grease | Grease (bearings) + water-lubricated seal |
| **Torque-Sequence Critical** | Yes (head bolts) | No (mostly friction-fit) | Yes (crank, stem) | Yes (casing bolts) |
| **Most Common Failure** | Ring wear / scoring | Quill scoring | Chain stretch | Seal leakage / cavitation |
| **Safety Hazard** | Hot, pressurized, flammable fuel | Heavy, sharp centre | Spring-loaded brakes | Electrical + pressurized water |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Assembly/Disassembly Flowchart (Applies to All Four Demonstrations)
