<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any six safety precautions to be observed while disassembling an IC engine cylinder-piston assembly.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. Ensure the engine is **completely cold** and the ignition / electrical system is **disconnected**.
2. **Depressurize** all systems (fuel, cooling, lubrication) before opening any joint.
3. Wear appropriate **PPE**: safety glasses, cut-resistant gloves, closed shoes, and an apron.
4. Place the engine on a **stable, clean workbench** with a drip tray for oil and fuel.
5. **Label and mark** every component (e.g., bearing caps, big-end orientation) before removal.
6. Use the **correct tool for the job**; never improvise (e.g., do not use a screwdriver as a chisel).
7. Keep **flammable solvents** (kerosene, petrol) away from open flames or sparks.
8. Dispose of used oil, filters, and contaminated rags in **designated waste containers**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is a piston ring? State its functions. Why must the ring end-gaps be staggered during assembly?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A piston ring is an expandable, split metal ring fitted into a groove on the piston OD. It presses radially outward against the cylinder bore to form a sliding seal.
- **Functions (1 mark):** (i) Seal combustion gases above the piston (compression rings); (ii) Control the thickness of the oil film on the cylinder wall (oil control ring); (iii) Transfer heat from the piston crown to the cylinder wall.
- **Reason for staggering (1 mark):** If all three ring end-gaps are aligned, combustion gases bypass the rings in a single vertical channel (called **blow-by**). Staggering the gaps at **120-degree intervals** forces gases to take a tortuous path, dramatically reducing leakage and improving sealing efficiency.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With the help of a neat procedure, explain the **step-by-step disassembly of a single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine** for inspection and overhaul. Mention the tools used at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **mechanical seal** in a centrifugal pump? Why is a new mechanical seal always fitted during pump reassembly? Describe the **procedure for installing a new mechanical seal** correctly. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Preparation (1 mark)**
- Park engine in a well-ventilated area; allow it to cool.
- Disconnect the spark plug lead and the battery negative terminal.
- Drain engine oil and coolant into labelled containers.

**Step 2 — External Accessories Removal (1 mark)**
- Remove the air-filter assembly, carburettor / injector rail, exhaust manifold, and fuel lines using the appropriate spanner set.
- Tag each hose with masking tape to ensure correct reconnection.

**Step 3 — Cylinder Head Removal (2 marks)**
- Using a **socket wrench and torque wrench**, loosen the head bolts in the **reverse of the tightening sequence** (start from the outer bolts and work inward).
- Tools: socket set, torque wrench, wooden mallet.
- Lift the head straight up; do not pry.

**Step 4 — Piston and Connecting Rod Withdrawal (2 marks)**
- Rotate the crankshaft to bring the piston to **Top Dead Centre (TDC)**.
- Remove the **big-end cap** (note the cap-match marks) using a spanner.
- Push the piston-rod assembly **upward and out** of the bore.
- Tools: spanner, wooden dowel (for stubborn pistons).

**Step 5 — Piston Ring and Wrist Pin Removal (1 mark)**
- Use a **piston-ring expander** to remove each ring carefully.
- Use **circlip pliers** to remove the snap rings; push the wrist pin out with a drift and soft hammer.
- Tools: ring expander, circlip pliers, drift, soft hammer.

**Inspection Note (extra 0.5 mark, if mentioned):** Measure piston OD, bore ID, and ring end-gap with micrometer and feeler gauge. Replace any part outside service limits.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Definition (1 mark):**
A mechanical seal is a precision-engineered sealing device consisting of two **lapped (optically flat) faces** — one rotating with the shaft (carbon or silicon carbide) and one stationary in the housing (ceramic or tungsten carbide) — pressed together by a spring and sealed at the shaft by a rubber O-ring or bellows. It prevents the pumped fluid from leaking along the shaft.

**Why Always New (2 marks):**
- The seal faces wear microscopically during operation; the **lapped flatness is lost**.
- Reusing a worn seal causes **immediate leakage**, which can damage bearings and the motor.
- The cost of a new seal is negligible compared to the downtime of a leaking pump.
- Reassembly usually involves disturbing the seal area, contaminating the faces with oil or dirt.

**Installation Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Clean the seal housing** with a lint-free cloth; even a single hair will cause a leak.
2. **Lubricate the elastomer (O-ring)** of the stationary face with soapy water — not grease, which attacks some rubber compounds.
3. **Press the stationary face into the housing by hand**, applying even pressure straight down. Do not tilt or tap with a hammer.
4. **Slide the rotating face onto the shaft** with a thin film of light oil on the shaft to ease assembly; the carbon face must not be touched by bare fingers (skin oils etch the lapped surface).
5. **Install the spring** to apply axial preload — typically 5 to 15 N·m equivalent closure force.
6. **Reassemble the impeller**, which acts as a secondary retainer for the seal.
7. **Prime the pump completely** with water before the first start — dry running destroys the new seal in seconds due to friction heat.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **construction and function of a lathe tailstock**. List the steps to **disassemble it for cleaning and inspection** of its quill mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure to assemble a bicycle** after a complete strip-down. Highlight the **key torque specifications and adjustments** that must be made. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction and Function (2 marks):**
- The tailstock is mounted on the lathe bed and provides the **opposing support** for the workpiece.
- Main parts: base (slides on bed), body (saddle), quill (sliding spindle with a Morse Taper socket), handwheel-driven leadscrew, locking lever for the quill, and dead centre or drill chuck fitted in the quill.

**Disassembly Steps (5 marks):**
1. **Lockout** the lathe; slide the tailstock off the bed and place on a clean bench.
2. **Loosen the quill locking lever** and fully advance the quill using the handwheel.
3. **Remove the dead centre** by rotating it out of the Morse taper.
4. **Unscrew the rear cap** of the quill housing (with shims set aside carefully).
5. **Withdraw the quill** forward from the body.
6. **Remove the handwheel** (retain the nut and washer) and **unscrew the leadscrew**.
7. **Extract the bearings** (note the order: bottom bearing, spacer, thrust bearing, top bearing).
8. **Clean all parts** in kerosene; inspect the quill for scoring, the Morse taper for chips, the bearings for pitting.
9. **Measure the quill OD and bore ID** with a micrometer; check sliding clearance against spec (typical 0.01 to 0.03 mm).

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Frame Preparation (1 mark)**
- Mount the frame in a workstand; clean the head tube and bottom bracket shell.

**Step 2 — Headset and Fork (1 mark)**
- Press the lower bearing race onto the fork crown.
- Slide the fork into the head tube; install upper bearings, top race, and top cap.
- **Adjust preload**: tighten the top cap until the bearings are smooth with zero play; then align the stem and torque the stem bolts (typically 5 to 6 N·m).

**Step 3 — Bottom Bracket and Cranks (2 marks)**
- Grease the BB threads; install the cups (note: left cup may be reverse-threaded on some bikes).
- Apply grease to the crank spindle; install the drive-side crank, torque the bolt to **30 to 40 N·m**.
- Install the non-drive-side crank.

**Step 4 — Chainring and Chain (1 mark)**
- Mount the chainring(s) on the spider; torque the bolts in a **star pattern** to **10 N·m** (typical).
- Thread the chain; install the master link; adjust rear derailleur limit screws (H, L) and B-tension.

**Step 5 — Brakes (1 mark)**
- Mount the brake calipers; install pads with slight toe-in.
- Set cable tension; verify lever feel and full pad-to-rim contact.

**Step 6 — Wheels and Final Adjustments (1 mark)**
- Install wheels; close quick-release levers firmly.
- Inflate tyres to sidewall pressure; check for true and dish.
- Test ride to confirm shifting, braking, and steering functions.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Writing steps in the wrong order.** Disassembly is *not* random; examiners award marks for the **logical sequence** (external first, internal last). Writing "remove the piston" before "remove the cylinder head" loses 1 to 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention safety precautions.** Every disassembly procedure in KTU papers must begin with a safety step. Skipping LOTO (Lockout-Tagout) or PPE loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing "assembly" with "disassembly" sequences.** Reassembly is the **reverse** of teardown — students often describe the same order, which is technically wrong.
> 4. **Omitting the cleaning and inspection phase.** A common trap: students jump from "remove part" to "fit part" without mentioning cleaning, measurement, and inspection. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Not specifying torque values or units.** Whenever you mention "tighten the bolt", KTU examiners expect the **torque value in N·m** and the **sequence** (criss-cross, star).
> 6. **Reusing gaskets and seals.** A classic KTU trap. The model answer always says "**fit a new gasket / seal**". Writing "refit the old gasket" will lose 1 mark in the maintenance section.
> 7. **Skipping the priming step for pumps.** Dry-running a centrifugal pump destroys the mechanical seal. The examiner expects "**prime the pump before starting**" as a mandatory final step.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Five-Phase Workflow:** Preparation → Tool/Safety Check → Disassembly → Cleaning/Inspection → Reassembly/Testing. Memorize this framework; it applies to **every** assembly.
- **Disassembly is the *reverse* of assembly.** Always mark parts for orientation; the same bolt that goes in last comes out first.
- **Cleanliness is non-negotiable.** A single dust particle can score a cylinder bore, destroy a mechanical seal, or jam a quill. Use kerosene, lint-free cloths, and clean trays.
- **Piston Ring End-Gaps** are staggered **120° apart** (12, 4, 8 o'clock) to prevent blow-by.
- **Head bolts** are tightened in a **criss-cross (star) pattern** in **multiple stages** (e.g., 30 → 60 → 90° angle) to ensure even gasket compression and prevent warping.
- **Torque wrench** is used for any safety-critical fastener (head bolts, crank bolts, big-end bolts, casing bolts). A regular spanner is not acceptable.
- **Mechanical seals are always replaced** during pump reassembly; the pump must be **primed** with water before the first start to prevent dry-run destruction.
- **Piston-Cylinder Clearance** is typically 0.02 to 0.05 mm; measured with a micrometer and bore gauge.
- **Tailstock Quill** has a sliding fit (0.01 to 0.03 mm) supported by bearings with shim-adjusted preload.
- **Bicycle headset adjustment**: top cap tightens the bearings; stem bolts clamp the stem — *do not* use the stem bolts to adjust preload.
- **Bicycle crank bolt torque** is approximately **30 to 40 N·m**; **chainring bolts** are tightened in a star pattern to ~**10 N·m**.
- **Always depressurize and de-energize** any machine before disassembly — this is the single most cited safety step in KTU marking schemes.
- **Document as you go:** take photos, label parts, and use trays in order. KTU examiners reward systematic students.
- **Final Test:** After reassembly, always run a **no-load test** first, then a **progressive-load test**, listening for abnormal noise and checking for leaks, binding, or overheating.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line exam mnemonic:** "**PDCIT**" — **P**repare, **D**isassemble (mark), **C**lean, **I**nspect, **T**est. Use this in the introduction of any 14-mark disassembly answer to earn quick marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machines (Demonstration): Shaping and slotting machine, Milling machine, Grinding Machine, Lathe, Drilling Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCESL106 - ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Machines (Demonstration): Shaping and slotting machine, Milling machine, Grinding Machine, Lathe, Drilling Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Workshop Machines (Demonstration)

A **workshop machine** is a powered mechanical device used in manufacturing industries to shape, cut, drill, grind, or finish raw material (typically metal) into a finished product with the desired geometry, dimensions, and surface finish. In the KTU 2024 Scheme Engineering Workshop, the demonstration of these machines is designed to give first-year B.Tech students hands-on familiarization with the basic principles of material removal processes (machining).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module Experiment 2):** This module is purely a **demonstration-based** experiment. Students are expected to identify machine parts, understand the working principle, observe safe operating procedures, and relate the machine tool to its industrial application. **No actual machining operation is graded**, but viva questions are asked on functional components and cutting parameters.

## 1.2 Classification of Machine Tools Used in the Demonstration

The five machines demonstrated in this module can be classified based on the type of motion between the cutting tool and the workpiece:

| Category | Machines in this module | Primary Motion |
|---|---|---|
| Reciprocating Type | Shaping, Slotting | Tool reciprocates |
| Rotating Tool Type | Drilling, Milling, Grinding | Tool rotates |
| Rotating Workpiece Type | Lathe | Workpiece rotates |
| Abrasive Type | Grinding | Abrasive wheel rotates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine sculpting a clay block. A lathe is like turning the clay on a potter's wheel (workpiece rotates). A milling machine is like using a spinning chisel (tool rotates). A shaper is like using a saw that moves back and forth (tool reciprocates). A grinding machine is like using sandpaper but rotating at very high speed.

## 1.3 The Shaping Machine

A **shaping machine** (or shaper) is a reciprocating type machine tool that removes metal from a workpiece during the forward (cutting) stroke of a single-point cutting tool, with the workpiece being held stationary. The tool returns to its starting position during the return (idle) stroke, with no metal removal occurring.

- **Primary Motion:** Linear reciprocation of the ram carrying the tool.
- **Feed Motion:** Intermittent slow lateral movement of the workpiece.
- **Workpiece:** Stationary; flat surfaces, keyways, slots, and contours are produced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of a hand saw cutting wood. The saw moves forward to cut (cutting stroke) and comes back without cutting (return stroke). A shaper does the same thing, but mechanically and far more precisely.

## 1.4 The Slotting Machine (Vertical Shaper)

A **slotting machine** (also called a slotter) is essentially a vertical shaper. The reciprocating motion of the ram is **vertical** (up and down) instead of horizontal, as in a shaping machine. It is used for machining internal surfaces, slots, keyways, and dies that are difficult to reach with a horizontal shaper.

- **Primary Motion:** Vertical reciprocation of the ram (downward cutting stroke, upward return stroke).
- **Feed Motion:** Lateral cross-feed of the worktable.
- **Application:** Internal keyways, circular contours, splines on shafts that have already been mounted.

## 1.5 The Milling Machine

A **milling machine** is a versatile machine tool in which metal is removed by a **multi-tooth rotating cutter** (called a milling cutter) held on the spindle. The workpiece is held stationary on the worktable, which is fed in controlled motions. It can produce flat surfaces, slots, gears, helical surfaces, and complex contours.

- **Primary Motion:** Rotation of the milling cutter.
- **Feed Motion:** Linear movement of the workpiece against the rotating cutter.
- **Types:** Column-and-knee type (vertical/horizontal milling attachment), bed type, CNC type.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A milling machine is like a rotating pizza cutter that moves over a slab of dough. Multiple teeth engage the workpiece simultaneously, making it faster and more productive than a single-point tool.

## 1.6 The Grinding Machine

A **grinding machine** is an abrasive machining process that uses a **rotating abrasive wheel** as the cutting tool. Each abrasive grain on the wheel acts as a tiny single-point cutting edge, removing very small chips of material to achieve very fine surface finish and high dimensional accuracy.

- **Primary Motion:** Rotation of the abrasive grinding wheel.
- **Feed Motion:** Linear movement of the workpiece or wheel.
- **Types:** Surface grinder, cylindrical grinder, centerless grinder, tool and cutter grinder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Surface finish achieved in grinding is typically in the range of **0.2 µm to 1.6 µm Ra** (roughness average), which is far superior to any other conventional machining process.

## 1.7 The Lathe

A **lathe** is a machine tool that rotates the workpiece about a fixed axis to perform various cutting operations such as turning, facing, knurling, threading, drilling, and boring. It is one of the oldest and most widely used machine tools in the world.

- **Primary Motion:** Rotation of the workpiece.
- **Feed Motion:** Movement of the cutting tool along the workpiece axis or radially.
- **Operations Performed:** Turning, facing, parting, threading, knurling, drilling, boring, reaming.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A lathe is exactly like a potter's wheel, where the clay (workpiece) spins and the potter's hands (cutting tool) shape it. Without a lathe, manufacturing of cylindrical parts like shafts, bolts, and screws would be almost impossible.

## 1.8 The Drilling Machine

A **drilling machine** is used to produce **circular holes** in a workpiece using a rotating multi-point cutting tool called a **drill bit**. The drill rotates about its own axis while being fed into the workpiece to cut material in the form of chips.

- **Primary Motion:** Rotation of the drill bit.
- **Feed Motion:** Axial downward movement of the drill into the workpiece.
- **Types:** Bench drilling machine, pillar drilling machine, radial drilling machine, sensitive drill, gang drill, and CNC drilling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024):** The demonstration module mandates that the student should be able to **identify the part name, describe its function, and state the primary and secondary motions** of each of the five machine tools. This forms the basis of viva voce questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Shaping Machine – Theoretical Analysis

The shaping machine works on the **Quick-Return Mechanism** (also called Whitworth or crank-and-slot mechanism). This mechanism ensures that the time taken for the forward (cutting) stroke is longer than the return (idle) stroke, thereby increasing productivity.

### Key Components and Their Functions

- **Base:** Heavy cast-iron bed that supports the entire machine and absorbs vibrations.
- **Column:** Vertical cast-iron body that supports the ram, cross-slide, and drive mechanism.
- **Cross-rail:** Horizontal slide that carries the worktable and can be moved up or down.
- **Worktable:** Holds the workpiece using a vice or clamping devices. It can be swivelled to any angle for angular cutting.
- **Ram:** The reciprocating member that carries the tool head. Moves in a horizontal line.
- **Tool head (Tool slide):** Holds the single-point cutting tool. It can be swivelled for vertical or angular cuts.
- **Apron / Feed mechanism:** Provides the intermittent cross-feed to the worktable at the end of each return stroke.
- **Quick-return mechanism:** Reduces return stroke time, increasing machine efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Quick-Return?** The return stroke produces no chips but takes time. By making the return stroke faster, more cutting strokes per minute are achieved, increasing productivity.

### Cutting Parameters (Formula Sheet)

**Cutting Velocity in Shaping:**

$$V_c = \frac{L \times (1 + r) \times N_s}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

Where:
- $V_c$ = Cutting speed (m/min)
- $L$ = Length of stroke (mm)
- $r$ = Return stroke ratio (typically 2:3 or 3:5 depending on mechanism)
- $N_s$ = Number of strokes per minute (double strokes/min)

**Time for Shaping Operation:**

$$T_{shaping} = \frac{L}{f \times N_s} \quad \text{(min)}$$

Where:
- $f$ = Feed per stroke (mm/stroke)
- $N_s$ = Number of strokes per minute

**Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$\text{MRR} = L \times f \times d \times N_s \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

Where $d$ = depth of cut (mm).

## 2.2 Slotting Machine – Theoretical Analysis

A slotting machine is essentially a **vertical shaper**. The mechanical design of the ram, drive mechanism, and quick-return mechanism is similar, but the ram reciprocates vertically.

### Key Components

- **Base, column, and table:** Similar to a shaper but more robust.
- **Ram:** Vertical reciprocating member carrying the tool holder at its lower end.
- **Tool head:** Holds the single-point cutting tool. It can be adjusted for angle of cut.
- **Rotary table (circular table):** The worktable can be rotated about a vertical axis for machining circular or curved internal surfaces.
- **Cross-slide:** Allows the worktable to move radially for feed motion.

### Applications of Slotting Machine

- Internal keyways in gears, pulleys, and hubs.
- Internal splines on shafts.
- Square and hexagonal holes.
- Dies and punches.
- Curved and contour surfaces (using rotary table).

## 2.3 Milling Machine – Theoretical Analysis

Milling is the most versatile machining operation. The cutter is a multi-tooth rotary cutting tool. Each tooth of the cutter removes a small chip per revolution, making it much faster than single-point tools.

### Key Components

- **Base:** Provides rigidity and supports column.
- **Column:** Houses the spindle and motor assembly.
- **Knee:** Vertical adjustable member carrying the saddle and worktable.
- **Saddle:** Cross-feed member, moves horizontally on the knee.
- **Worktable:** Longitudinal feed member, holds the workpiece.
- **Spindle:** Rotates the milling cutter at high speeds.
- **Arbor:** A shaft on which milling cutters are mounted (for horizontal spindle).
- **Overarm:** Supports the arbor at the far end (in horizontal milling).
- **Indexing head (dividing head):** Used for cutting gears, splines, and equally spaced features.

### Types of Milling Operations

- **Plain (slab) milling:** Producing a flat surface parallel to the cutter axis.
- **Face milling:** Producing a flat surface perpendicular to cutter axis.
- **End milling:** Producing slots, pockets, and contours.
- **Side milling:** Producing vertical flat surfaces.
- **Straddle milling:** Producing two parallel vertical surfaces simultaneously.
- **Angular milling:** Producing surfaces at an angle.

### Milling Formula Sheet

**Cutting Speed in Milling:**

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \times D \times N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

Where:
- $D$ = Cutter diameter (mm)
- $N$ = Spindle speed (rpm)

**Feed Rate (Table Feed):**

$$V_f = f_z \times Z \times N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

Where:
- $f_z$ = Feed per tooth (mm/tooth)
- $Z$ = Number of teeth on cutter
- $N$ = Spindle speed (rpm)

**Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$\text{MRR} = a \times w \times V_f \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

Where $a$ = axial depth of cut, $w$ = radial width of cut.

**Machining Time in Milling:**

$$T_m = \frac{L + L_{approach} + L_{overtravel}}{V_f} \quad \text{(min)}$$

## 2.4 Grinding Machine – Theoretical Analysis

Grinding is a finishing operation that uses an **abrasive wheel** as the cutting tool. The abrasive wheel is composed of abrasive grains held together by a bonding material (e.g., vitrified, resinoid, rubber, or metal bond).

### Key Components

- **Base / Bed:** Provides rigidity.
- **Wheel head:** Holds the grinding wheel and rotates it at high speed.
- **Work head:** Holds the workpiece and rotates it (in cylindrical grinding).
- **Tailstock:** Supports the other end of the workpiece.
- **Table:** Reciprocates the workpiece under the wheel.
- **Coolant supply system:** Floods the grinding zone to prevent thermal damage.

### Selection of Grinding Wheel

The grinding wheel is specified using a standard marking system:

- **A** = Abrasive type (e.g., A = Aluminium Oxide, C = Silicon Carbide, S = Sol Gel Alumina)
- **B** = Grain size (Coarse 8–24, Medium 30–60, Fine 70–220, Very Fine 230 and above)
- **C** = Grade (Hardness, A=Soft to Z=Hard)
- **D** = Structure (density, 1=Close to 15=Open)
- **E** = Bond type (V=Vitrified, R=Rubber, B=Resinoid, M=Metal)

### Grinding Formula Sheet

**Cutting Speed in Grinding (Peripheral Speed of Wheel):**

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \times D \times N}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

**Equivalent Cutter Diameter (in surface grinding):**

$$D_{eq} = \frac{D \times d}{D \pm d}$$

Where:
- $D$ = Wheel diameter
- $d$ = Workpiece diameter
- '+' for external / surface grinding; '-' for internal grinding

**Specific Energy in Grinding:**

$$U = \frac{P}{Z_w} \quad \text{(J/mm}^3\text{)}$$

Where $P$ = power (W), $Z_w$ = metal removal rate.

## 2.5 Lathe – Theoretical Analysis

The lathe is the **mother of all machine tools**. Every cylindrical component in any machine—from a needle to a large propeller shaft—is first machined on a lathe.

### Key Components of a Centre Lathe

- **Bed:** Long, heavy, precision-ground base; supports headstock, tailstock, and carriage.
- **Headstock:** Houses the main spindle, gear box, and chuck. Spindle rotates the workpiece.
- **Tailstock:** Supports the long workpiece at the free end; also holds drills and reamers.
- **Carriage:** Moves the cutting tool along the bed. Includes saddle, cross-slide, compound slide, and tool post.
- **Feed mechanism:** Provides power-feed to carriage and cross-slide.
- **Lead screw / Apron:** Used for thread cutting.
- **Chuck / Face plate:** Holds the workpiece (3-jaw self-centring, 4-jaw independent, magnetic, or collet).

### Lathe Operations

- Turning, Facing, Parting (cut-off), Threading, Knurling, Drilling, Boring, Reaming, Taper turning.

### Lathe Formula Sheet

**Cutting Speed in Turning:**

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \times D \times N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

Where:
- $D$ = Workpiece diameter (mm)
- $N$ = Spindle speed (rpm)

**Spindle Speed (Rearranged):**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D} \quad \text{(rpm)}$$

**Feed Rate:**

$$V_f = f \times N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

Where $f$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev).

**Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$\text{MRR} = V_f \times d \times f \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

Where $d$ = depth of cut (mm).

**Machining Time:**

$$T_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} \quad \text{(min)}$$

**Power Required:**

$$P = \frac{\text{MRR} \times K_c}{60 \times 10^6} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

Where $K_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²).

## 2.6 Drilling Machine – Theoretical Analysis

Drilling uses a **multi-point tool (drill bit)** with cutting edges on its conical point. The drill rotates and is fed axially into the stationary workpiece.

### Key Components of a Pillar / Bench Drilling Machine

- **Base:** Heavy, provides stability.
- **Pillar / Column:** Vertical column supporting the head and table.
- **Head:** Houses the spindle and motor, with a feed handle.
- **Spindle and chuck:** Holds the drill bit and rotates it.
- **Drill chuck / Collet:** Holds the drill bit.
- **Worktable:** Adjustable, supports the workpiece.
- **Feed lever:** Provides manual downward feed.
- **Depth stop:** Limits the depth of the hole.

### Drilling Operations

- Drilling, Reaming, Boring, Counter-boring, Counter-sinking, Tapping, Spot facing.

### Drilling Formula Sheet

**Cutting Speed in Drilling:**

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \times D \times N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

Where $D$ = drill diameter (mm), $N$ = spindle speed (rpm).

**Spindle Speed (Rearranged):**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D} \quad \text{(rpm)}$$

**Feed Rate:**

$$V_f = f \times N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

Where $f$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev).

**Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi \times D^2}{4} \times V_f \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

**Thrust Force (Approximate):**

$$F_t = K_c \times \text{MRR} \quad \text{(N)}$$

**Machining Time:**

$$T_m = \frac{L}{V_f} = \frac{L}{f \times N} \quad \text{(min)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The formulas above are used by manufacturing engineers in **process planning sheets** to determine cycle time, set CNC machine parameters, and estimate production cost. For example, MRR directly drives the productivity analysis on a shop floor.

## 2.7 Comprehensive KTU Formula Cheat Sheet Table

| Operation | Cutting Speed $V_c$ (m/min) | Spindle Speed $N$ (rpm) | Feed Rate $V_f$ (mm/min) | MRR (mm³/min) | Machining Time $T_m$ (min) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Turning | $\frac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | $\frac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$ | $f N$ | $V_f d f$ | $\frac{L}{fN}$ |
| Drilling | $\frac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | $\frac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$ | $f N$ | $\frac{\pi D^2}{4} V_f$ | $\frac{L}{fN}$ |
| Milling | $\frac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | $\frac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$ | $f_z Z N$ | $a w V_f$ | $\frac{L+L_a+L_o}{V_f}$ |
| Shaping | $\frac{L(1+r)N_s}{1000}$ | Given by stroke lever | $f N_s$ | $L f d N_s$ | $\frac{L}{f N_s}$ |
| Grinding | $\frac{\pi D N}{60 \times 1000}$ (m/s) | – | $V_f = f_t \times f_r$ | $a V_w V_t$ | $\frac{L}{V_w}$ |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cutting Speed in a Lathe (Turning Operation)

The cutting speed $V_c$ is defined as the **peripheral surface speed** of the workpiece at the cutting zone, measured in m/min.

### Step 1: Express the Distance Travelled per Minute by the Workpiece Surface

When the workpiece of diameter $D$ rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute (rpm), every point on its surface traces a circle of circumference $\pi D$ per revolution. Therefore, the linear distance covered in one minute is:

$$d_{per\ min} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

### Step 2: Convert to Standard Unit (m/min)

Since 1 m = 1000 mm, we divide by 1000:

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

This is the standard KTU formula for cutting speed in turning.

### Step 3: Solve for Spindle Speed $N$

Multiplying both sides by 1000:

$$1000 \cdot V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$

Dividing by $\pi D$:

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \text{(rpm)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** This derivation directly gives the relationship used in process planning to select the right spindle speed for any given workpiece diameter and recommended cutting speed (from the tool manufacturer's handbook).

## 3.2 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Turning

MRR is the volume of material removed per unit time. In turning, the cross-section of the chip is rectangular with:
- Width = depth of cut ($d$)
- Thickness = feed per revolution ($f$)

### Step 1: Cross-Sectional Area of the Chip

$$A_{chip} = d \times f \quad \text{(mm}^2\text{)}$$

### Step 2: Volume Removed per Revolution

For one revolution, the tool moves along the workpiece by a distance equal to the feed $f$. The volume removed is:

$$V_{per\ rev} = A_{chip} \times \pi \cdot D = d \cdot f \cdot \pi \cdot D \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/rev)}$$

### Step 3: Volume per Minute

For $N$ revolutions per minute:

$$\text{MRR} = V_{per\ rev} \times N = d \cdot f \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

### Step 4: Substitute $V_f = f N$

We can rewrite as:

$$\text{MRR} = V_f \cdot d \cdot f \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this important?** MRR is the **single most important productivity metric** in any machining operation. Higher MRR means faster cycle time and lower cost per part.

## 3.3 Derivation: Machining Time in Turning a Cylindrical Surface

### Step 1: Identify the Length to be Machined

The total length the tool must travel to turn a workpiece of length $L$ is $L$ mm (assuming tool starts at the face).

### Step 2: Identify the Feed Rate

The tool advances by $f$ mm per revolution, and the spindle rotates at $N$ rpm. Therefore, axial feed per minute is:

$$V_f = f \cdot N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

### Step 3: Time = Length / Feed Rate

$$T_m = \frac{L}{V_f} = \frac{L}{f \cdot N} \quad \text{(min)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** $L$ in mm is divided by $V_f$ in mm/min, which directly yields time in minutes. This formula is used in every KTU numerical problem on cycle time estimation.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Lathe Turning Problem (KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** A mild steel shaft of initial diameter 60 mm is to be turned down to 52 mm in a single pass. The recommended cutting speed is 25 m/min and feed is 0.3 mm/rev. Length of cut is 200 mm.

**Find:** (a) Spindle speed (b) Feed rate (c) MRR (d) Machining time.

### Step 1: Spindle Speed (using average diameter)

For multi-pass turning, KTU standard practice uses the **average diameter**:

$$D_{avg} = \frac{D_{initial} + D_{final}}{2} = \frac{60 + 52}{2} = 56 \text{ mm}$$

Calculate $N$:

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D_{avg}} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 56}$$

$$N = \frac{25000}{175.929} = 142.1 \text{ rpm}$$

### Step 2: Feed Rate

$$V_f = f \times N = 0.3 \times 142.1 = 42.64 \text{ mm/min}$$

### Step 3: Material Removal Rate

Depth of cut $d = (60 - 52)/2 = 4$ mm

$$\text{MRR} = V_f \times d \times f = 42.64 \times 4 \times 0.3 = 51.17 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

### Step 4: Machining Time

$$T_m = \frac{L}{V_f} = \frac{200}{42.64} = 4.69 \text{ min}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Drilling Formulae

### Step 1: Cutting Speed in Drilling

The drill of diameter $D$ rotates at $N$ rpm. The peripheral speed of the cutting edge is:

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

(Same form as lathe, but $D$ is the drill diameter and the cutting happens at the periphery of the drill.)

### Step 2: MRR in Drilling

In drilling, the area of the chip cross-section is the **full circular area** of the hole:

$$A_{chip} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \quad \text{(mm}^2\text{)}$$

The drill advances by $f$ mm per revolution. So volume removed per minute:

$$\text{MRR} = A_{chip} \times V_f = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f \times N \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Difference from Turning:** In turning, the chip cross-section is a thin rectangle ($d \times f$). In drilling, the chip cross-section is the full circular area of the hole because the drill removes material across its full diameter.

### Step 3: Machining Time in Drilling

For a hole of depth $L$ (assuming drill starts at the top surface):

$$T_m = \frac{L}{f \cdot N} \quad \text{(min)}$$

In practice, the drill must travel slightly beyond $L$ for breakthrough, and may need to be retracted for chip clearing (peck drilling). The KTU formula for **deep hole drilling** uses the effective engagement:

$$T_m = \frac{L \times (\text{number of pecks})}{f \cdot N} \quad \text{(min)}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix of the Five Machines

| Parameter | Lathe | Drilling | Milling | Shaping | Grinding |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Motion** | Workpiece rotates | Tool rotates | Tool rotates | Tool reciprocates | Tool (wheel) rotates |
| **Feed Motion** | Tool (linear) | Tool (linear axial) | Worktable (linear) | Worktable (intermittent) | Workpiece or wheel |
| **Type of Tool** | Single-point | Multi-point (drill) | Multi-point (cutter) | Single-point | Abrasive wheel |
| **Workpiece Type** | Cylindrical | Any | Any | Flat/Slot | Any (hard surfaces) |
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | 1.6 – 6.3 µm | 3.2 – 12.5 µm | 1.6 – 6.3 µm | 3.2 – 12.5 µm | 0.2 – 1.6 µm |
| **MRR (Relative)** | High | Medium | Very High | Low | Very Low |
| **Main Application** | Shafts, threads | Holes | Flat surfaces, slots | Flat surfaces, keyways | Finishing, hardening |

## 3.7 Quick Numericals for Practice

### Quick Numerical 1: Milling

**Problem:** A 50 mm diameter milling cutter with 8 teeth is used at 240 rpm. Feed per tooth is 0.15 mm/tooth. Width of cut is 30 mm, depth of cut is 4 mm, and length of cut is 120 mm. Calculate cutting speed, feed rate, MRR, and machining time.

**Solution:**

Cutting speed:

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \times 50 \times 240}{1000} = 37.7 \text{ m/min}$$

Feed rate:

$$V_f = f_z \times Z \times N = 0.15 \times 8 \times 240 = 288 \text{ mm/min}$$

MRR:

$$\text{MRR} = a \times w \times V_f = 4 \times 30 \times 288 = 34560 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

Machining time (ignoring approach/overtravel):

$$T_m = \frac{120}{288} = 0.417 \text{ min} \approx 25 \text{ s}$$

### Quick Numerical 2: Drilling

**Problem:** Drill a 12 mm diameter hole in mild steel. Cutting speed = 30 m/min, feed = 0.15 mm/rev, depth of hole = 40 mm. Find spindle speed, feed rate, MRR, and machining time.

**Solution:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 12} = 795.77 \approx 796 \text{ rpm}$$

$$V_f = 0.15 \times 796 = 119.4 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi \times 12^2}{4} \times 119.4 = 113.04 \times 119.4 = 13497 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$T_m = \frac{40}{119.4} = 0.335 \text{ min} \approx 20.1 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that although the milling MRR is much higher than the drilling MRR in absolute terms, the **specific energy** (energy per unit volume removed) is much higher in grinding, making grinding slow but accurate. This is why grinding is classified as a **finishing** process, not a roughing process.

## 4.7 Sequence Diagram: Operational Sequence on a Lathe (Turning Operation)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant O as Operator
    participant L as Lathe
    participant T as Tool
    participant W as Workpiece

    O->>L: Mount workpiece in chuck
    L->>W: Clamp and rotate
    O->>L: Set spindle speed N
    L->>W: Rotate at N rpm
    O->>T: Position tool at starting point
    T->>W: Engage with depth of cut
    O->>L: Start power feed
    L->>T: Feed at f mm/rev
    T->>W: Material removal
    O->>L: Retract tool at end of pass
    O->>T: Return to start
    L->>W: Stop spindle
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]: Define a shaping machine and state its primary and feed motions.

**Model Answer:**

A **shaping machine** is a reciprocating-type machine tool in which a single-point cutting tool is held on a ram that moves linearly back and forth (reciprocates). The metal is removed only during the forward (cutting) stroke; the return (idle) stroke consumes no material.

- **Primary Motion:** Reciprocating linear motion of the ram carrying the tool.
- **Feed Motion:** Intermittent cross-feed of the worktable at the end of each return stroke.

**Key Identification Features:** The drive is given by a **crank-and-slotted-lever (Whitworth) quick-return mechanism**, which makes the return stroke faster than the cutting stroke to increase productivity.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]: List any six operations performed on a lathe.

**Model Answer:**

The six basic operations performed on a lathe are:

1. **Turning** – Reducing the diameter of a cylindrical workpiece.
2. **Facing** – Producing a flat surface perpendicular to the axis.
3. **Parting (cut-off)** – Cutting off a finished part from the parent stock.
4. **Threading** – Cutting external or internal threads using a thread-cutting tool.
5. **Knurling** – Producing a patterned (diamond/straight) surface for grip.
6. **Drilling** – Using a drill bit held in the tailstock to make a hole.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Many students miss writing **'Boring'** (enlarging an existing hole) and **'Reaming'** (finishing a hole to accurate size) — these are also frequently asked operations.

## 5.2 KTU 2024 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) Describe the construction and working of a centre lathe with a neat block diagram. List at least six lathe operations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**

A centre lathe consists of the following major parts:

- **Bed:** Heavy, precision-ground cast iron base. Provides alignment for the headstock, carriage, and tailstock. Has V-ways and flat ways for guiding the carriage.
- **Headstock:** Located at the left end of the bed. Houses the **main spindle**, gear box, and **chuck**. The spindle is driven by an electric motor through a multi-step gear train to provide various spindle speeds.
- **Tailstock:** Located on the right end of the bed. Can be slid along the bed and clamped. Has a **quill** that can be moved axially to support the free end of the workpiece or hold a drill bit.
- **Carriage:** Mounted on the bed ways between headstock and tailstock. Carries the cutting tool and provides longitudinal (Z-axis) feed. Sub-parts:
  - **Saddle:** Mounted on the bed.
  - **Cross-slide:** Mounted on the saddle, provides cross (X-axis) feed.
  - **Compound slide:** Swivel-mounted on the cross-slide, used for taper turning and threading.
  - **Tool post (four-way type):** Mounted on the compound slide, holds the cutting tool.
- **Feed mechanism and lead screw:** Provides automatic feed to carriage and cross-slide. Lead screw drives the carriage for thread cutting.
- **Apron:** Attached to the front of the saddle, contains feed clutches, levers, and the split nut for thread cutting.

**Working Principle:**

The workpiece is held in the chuck and rotated by the spindle at a chosen speed. The single-point cutting tool, fixed in the tool post, is gradually fed into the workpiece. The relative motion between the rotating workpiece and the advancing tool produces chips and reduces the diameter. The depth of cut controls the chip thickness, and the feed rate controls the surface finish.

**Six Lathe Operations:**

1. Turning (plain, taper)
2. Facing
3. Parting
4. Threading (external/internal)
5. Knurling
6. Drilling / Boring / Reaming

#### (b) In a turning operation on a mild steel shaft, the initial diameter is 80 mm and the final diameter is 72 mm. Cutting speed = 30 m/min, feed = 0.25 mm/rev, length of cut = 250 mm. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Feed rate, (iii) MRR, (iv) Machining time. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Spindle Speed:**

Average diameter:

$$D_{avg} = \frac{80 + 72}{2} = 76 \text{ mm}$$

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 76} = \frac{30000}{238.76} = 125.66 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate:**

$$V_f = f \times N = 0.25 \times 125.66 = 31.42 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate:**

Depth of cut:

$$d = \frac{80 - 72}{2} = 4 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{MRR} = V_f \times d \times f = 31.42 \times 4 \times 0.25 = 31.42 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(iv) Machining Time:**

$$T_m = \frac{L}{V_f} = \frac{250}{31.42} = 7.957 \text{ min}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (7-mark numerical):** Many students forget to use the **average diameter** for spindle speed in a single-pass turning problem. Using initial diameter 80 mm gives $N = 119.4$ rpm — a 5% error that will cost at least 1 mark. Always check the problem statement for "single pass" vs. "multi-pass."

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

#### (a) Explain the construction and working principle of a milling machine. Compare horizontal and vertical milling machines. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of a Column-and-Knee Type Milling Machine:**

- **Base:** Heavy cast iron foundation, supports the column and absorbs vibration.
- **Column:** Vertical member at the back, houses the spindle driving mechanism and motor. Has dovetail slides for the knee.
- **Knee:** Mounted on the column, can be moved vertically by an elevating screw. Carries the saddle and worktable.
- **Saddle:** Mounted on top of the knee, moves transversely (cross-feed) by a screw.
- **Worktable:** Mounted on the saddle, has T-slots for clamping the workpiece, moves longitudinally (longitudinal feed).
- **Spindle:** Rotates the milling cutter; speed varied through gear box.
- **Arbor and Overarm:** Used in horizontal milling to support the cutter arbor at the far end.
- **Indexing head:** Used to rotate the workpiece by precise angular amounts for gear cutting, splines, etc.

**Working Principle:**

The milling cutter is mounted on the spindle and rotated at a high speed (primary motion). The workpiece is clamped on the worktable and fed against the rotating cutter (feed motion). Each tooth of the multi-tooth cutter removes a small chip per revolution, producing a high material removal rate. Both straight and formed surfaces can be produced.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Horizontal Milling | Vertical Milling |
|---|---|---|
| Spindle axis | Horizontal | Vertical |
| Cutter mounting | Arbor + Overarm | Spindle nose directly |
| Cutter types | Slab, side, straddle, plain | End mill, face mill, slitting saw |
| Chip evacuation | Gravity-assisted, better | Poor, may need coolant |
| Rigidity | Higher for large cutters | Lower |
| Typical jobs | Large flat surfaces, slots | Pockets, contours, drilling |

#### (b) A drilling operation is to be performed on a mild steel plate using a 20 mm diameter drill. The cutting speed is 25 m/min, feed is 0.20 mm/rev, and the depth of hole is 50 mm. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Feed rate, (iii) MRR, (iv) Time required. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Spindle Speed:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 20} = \frac{25000}{62.83} = 397.89 \approx 398 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate:**

$$V_f = 0.20 \times 398 = 79.6 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate:**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times V_f = \frac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} \times 79.6 = 314.16 \times 79.6 = 25007 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(iv) Machining Time:**

$$T_m = \frac{L}{V_f} = \frac{50}{79.6} = 0.628 \text{ min} \approx 37.7 \text{ s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Drilling 7-mark numerical):** Common mistake is to use the **chip cross-section** as $D \times f$ (like turning). In drilling, the chip cross-section is the **full circular area** $\frac{\pi D^2}{4}$. Using the wrong area for MRR costs 1–2 marks. Remember: **drilling removes the entire hole volume per unit time.**

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Module-Wide Warning)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Common Mistakes in KTU Workshop Lab Viva on this Module:**
> 1. **Mixing up primary and feed motion** — The primary motion is always the highest-speed motion (e.g., rotation in lathe, reciprocation in shaper). The feed motion is the slower, controlled motion.
> 2. **Confusing shaping with slotting** — Both use reciprocating tools, but shaping has a horizontal ram and slotting has a vertical ram. The slotting machine is used for **internal** features; shaping for **external** features.
> 3. **Confusing milling with drilling** — Both have rotating tools, but milling uses a **multi-tooth cutter** for surface generation, while drilling uses a **two-flute twist drill** to make holes. Milling MRR formula uses $a \times w \times V_f$; drilling MRR uses $\frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times V_f$.
> 4. **Forgetting coolant in grinding** — Grinding generates intense heat; dry grinding cracks the workpiece. Always mention **flood coolant** in any grinding-related answer.
> 5. **Calling a shaper a "planer"** — A planer is a much larger machine where the **workpiece reciprocates** and the tool is stationary. A shaper is the smaller version where the **tool reciprocates** and the workpiece is stationary.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Shaping Machine:** Reciprocating tool (horizontal ram); quick-return mechanism; primary motion = ram reciprocation; feed = worktable cross-feed.
- [x] **Slotting Machine:** Vertical ram; used for internal keyways, slots, and dies; uses a rotary table for circular work.
- [x] **Milling Machine:** Multi-tooth rotating cutter; primary motion = cutter rotation; feed = worktable linear motion; horizontal and vertical types.
- [x] **Grinding Machine:** Abrasive wheel removes material; finishing operation; surface finish 0.2–1.6 µm Ra; requires coolant.
- [x] **Lathe:** Workpiece rotates; primary motion = spindle rotation; tool feed is secondary; can perform turning, facing, threading, knurling, parting, drilling.
- [x] **Drilling Machine:** Multi-point tool (drill bit) rotates and feeds axially; MRR formula uses $\frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times V_f$.
- [x] **Universal Cutting Speed Formula:** $V_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000}$ (m/min) — applicable to all rotating workpiece or rotating tool operations.
- [x] **MRR Formula Variants:** Turning → $V_f \times d \times f$; Drilling → $\frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times V_f$; Milling → $a \times w \times V_f$; Shaping → $L \times f \times d \times N_s$.
- [x] **Machining Time Universal Formula:** $T_m = \frac{L}{V_f}$.
- [x] **Quick Return:** Used in shaper and slotter; cuts cycle time by making return stroke faster than cutting stroke.
- [x] **Three-jaw chuck:** Self-centring; used for round/hex workpieces in lathe.
- [x] **Indexing head:** Used in milling for angular positioning (gears, splines, flutes).
- [x] **Standard Feed Directions in Milling:** Longitudinal (along table), Cross (across table), Vertical (up/down on knee).
- [x] **Work Material Removals:** Lathe (cylindrical), Shaper (flat), Miller (flat/slot), Driller (hole), Grinder (finishing).
- [x] **Coolant Use:** Essential in grinding and drilling (deep holes); increases tool life and improves surface finish.
- [x] **Tool Material Reminder:** HSS for low-speed; Carbide for high-speed; Diamond/CBN for grinding wheels on hardened steel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU Exam:** The examiner will always try to ask the **distinguishing feature** of each machine. Memorize the line: *"In a lathe, the workpiece rotates; in a shaper, the tool reciprocates; in a milling machine, the multi-tooth cutter rotates; in a drilling machine, the drill rotates and feeds; in a grinding machine, the abrasive wheel rotates."* If you remember this, you can answer 80% of viva questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern manufacturing methods (Fab lab/IDEA Lab - Demonstration only): Power tools, CNC machine tools, 3D printing, Soft Materials cutting using special machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCESL106 - ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Modern manufacturing methods (Fab lab/IDEA Lab - Demonstration only): Power tools, CNC machine tools, 3D printing, Soft Materials cutting using special machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Modern Manufacturing Methods — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
> *Modern manufacturing methods* refer to a set of **computer-integrated, digitally-controlled, and additive/subtractive fabrication techniques** that have largely replaced or supplemented traditional hand-tool-based workshop practices. In the KTU 2024 workshop syllabus, this cluster of technologies is *demonstrated* (not hands-on operated) within the institutional **Fab Lab** or **IDEA Lab** infrastructure.

The four pillars covered under Module Experiment 2 are:

| # | Pillar | Category | Material Domain |
|---|--------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Power Tools | Subtractive / Forming | Metals, Wood, Plastics |
| 2 | CNC Machine Tools | Subtractive (Automated) | Metals, Plastics, Wood |
| 3 | 3D Printing | **Additive** | Polymers, Resins, Metals |
| 4 | Soft Material Cutting | Subtractive (Special) | Fabrics, Foam, Leather, Paper, Acrylic sheets |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU 2024 NEP-Aligned Note:**
> This module is **Demonstration Only**. Students are *not* expected to operate the machines, but they **must** understand the working principle, classification, and safety protocols. The CO (Course Outcome) usually tagged to this module is *CO2 — Understand modern manufacturing processes.*

---

## 1.2 What is a Fab Lab / IDEA Lab?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Fab Lab):**
> A **Fabrication Laboratory (Fab Lab)** is a small-scale workshop offering **digital fabrication** services, originally conceptualized by **Prof. Neil Gershenfeld** at **MIT (Massachusetts Institute of Technology), USA**, under the *Center for Bits and Atoms (CBA)*. It is a globally networked prototype of a digital manufacturing studio equipped with tools for **personal fabrication**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (IDEA Lab):**
> An **IDEA (Innovation, Design, Entrepreneurship, and Action) Lab** is the **Indian adaptation** of the Fab Lab concept, promoted by **MHRD (Ministry of Human Resource Development) / AICTE** and implemented across Indian technical universities (including KTU-affiliated colleges) to promote innovation, prototyping, and the *Make in India* initiative.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a Fab Lab as a **"kitchen for inventors."**

- A chef uses a stove, oven, mixer, and fridge — the **chef is the human**, the appliances are the **tools**.
- An inventor/designer in a Fab Lab uses a 3D printer, CNC router, laser cutter, and vinyl cutter — the **inventor is the human**, the machines are the **tools**.
- Just as a kitchen allows you to *cook* (transform raw ingredients into a finished dish), a Fab Lab allows you to *fabricate* (transform digital files into physical objects).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Global Benchmark (MIT CBA Specs):**
> A fully-certified Fab Lab contains **roughly 10–20 core machines**, occupies a floor area of **approximately 1000–2000 sq ft**, and operates on a **digital workflow** where the *input* is a CAD file and the *output* is a physical part.

---

## 1.3 The Four Pillars — One-Line Definitions

| Pillar | One-Line Definition | Standard Process |
|--------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Power Tools** | Portable or stationary **electric / pneumatic / hydraulic** tools that amplify human effort to perform cutting, drilling, grinding, and forming operations. | Subtractive |
| **CNC Machine Tools** | **Computer Numerical Control** machine tools where motions (X, Y, Z axes) are automated through G-code & M-code instructions interpreted by a controller. | Subtractive (Automated) |
| **3D Printing** | **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** process that builds a 3D object layer-by-layer from a digital CAD model using materials like PLA, ABS, or resins. | Additive |
| **Soft Material Cutting** | Specialized **die-cutting, laser-cutting, and vinyl-cutting** machines for sheet materials such as fabric, foam, cardboard, leather, and acrylic. | Subtractive (2.5D) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Speed Metric:**
> A typical CNC spindle operates in the range of **0 – 24,000 rpm**, while desktop FDM 3D printers extrude at **0.1 – 0.4 mm layer height** with print speeds of **40 – 150 mm/s**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power Tools — Detailed Working Classification

Power tools convert **electrical / pneumatic / hydraulic energy** into **mechanical rotational / linear motion** through a motor-driven spindle.

### 2.1.1 Classification Logic (Bulleted Hierarchy)

**A. By Energy Source**
- **Electric Power Tools** — powered by AC mains (220V / 415V) or DC battery (e.g., cordless drills).
- **Pneumatic Power Tools** — powered by compressed air (typical shop pressure: **6 – 8 bar / 0.6 – 0.8 MPa**).
- **Hydraulic Power Tools** — powered by pressurized oil (typical working pressure: **70 – 200 bar**).
- **Powder-Actuated Tools** — use a small explosive cartridge (e.g., Hilti DX series for fixing nails to concrete).

**B. By Function**
- **Cutting Tools** — angle grinder, circular saw, jigsaw, band saw, reciprocating saw.
- **Drilling Tools** — hand drill, hammer drill, magnetic drill.
- **Grinding / Finishing Tools** — bench grinder, die grinder, orbital sander.
- **Fastening Tools** — impact driver, nail gun, rivet gun.
- **Forming Tools** — nibbler, shear, brake press.

**C. By Portability**
- **Stationary Power Tools** — bench grinder, drill press, band saw (fixed to workshop floor).
- **Portable / Hand-Held Power Tools** — angle grinder, jigsaw, rotary tool (e.g., Dremel).
- **Semi-Portable** — magnetic base drill, pipe threading machine.

### 2.1.2 Real-World Engineering Utility
- Automotive assembly lines (pneumatic nut runners, impact wrenches).
- Shipbuilding (hydraulic torque wrenches, plate shears).
- Construction (powder-actuated nailers for concrete, hammer drills).
- Aerospace (precision torque-controlled electric screwdrivers).

---

## 2.2 CNC Machine Tools — Deep Working Principle

A **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) machine tool** is a *soft-automated* manufacturing system where every motion of the cutting tool and workpiece is commanded by a **NC program** consisting of alphanumeric codes (G-codes for geometry, M-codes for miscellaneous functions).

### 2.2.1 The Operational Chain (Why & How)

1. **CAD (Computer-Aided Design)** — A 2D drawing or 3D model is created in software like AutoCAD, SolidWorks, or CATIA.
   - *Why?* — The CNC machine cannot think; it only follows geometry.
2. **CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing)** — The CAD file is converted into a toolpath using software like MasterCAM, Fusion 360, or SolidCAM. The CAM software outputs an **NC file** (usually `.nc` or `.gcode`).
   - *Why?* — The machine needs a *recipe* of moves; CAM is the cookbook.
3. **Post-Processing** — The generic G-code is *customized* for a specific CNC controller (Fanuc, Siemens, Haas, Mitsubishi) by a **post-processor**.
   - *Why?* — Different CNC brands have slightly different code dialects.
4. **Controller / MCU** — A microprocessor-based unit (often an industrial PC) interprets each line of G-code, computes axis trajectories using **interpolation algorithms** (linear, circular, helical), and sends real-time pulse signals to the servo drives.
5. **Servo / Stepper Drives** — Convert low-power control signals into **high-power electrical currents** to drive the motors.
6. **Feedback System** — **Encoders** (rotary on motor shaft, linear on axis) provide closed-loop position feedback for accuracy.
7. **Servo Motors + Ball Screws** — Convert rotary motion to precise linear motion on each axis (X, Y, Z) and rotational motion on the spindle.
8. **Cutting Tool & Workpiece** — Material is removed as the *relative motion* between tool and workpiece is executed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed-Loop vs Open-Loop:**
> Industrial CNC machines are **closed-loop** (with encoders giving real-time feedback). Hobbyist / DIY machines are often **open-loop** (stepper motors, no feedback) — cheaper but less accurate.

### 2.2.2 Types of CNC Machines (For Workshop Demonstration)
- **CNC Milling Machine** — 3-axis to 5-axis; tools rotate, workpiece is stationary.
- **CNC Lathe (Turning Center)** — 2-axis; workpiece rotates, tool is stationary.
- **CNC Router** — typically 3-axis, used for wood, plastic, foam, soft metals.
- **CNC Plasma / Laser / Waterjet Cutter** — for sheet cutting.
- **CNC Wire-EDM** — uses electrical discharge in dielectric fluid to cut hardened steel.

---

## 2.3 3D Printing (Additive Manufacturing) — Theoretical Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (ISO/ASTM 52900:2021 Standard):**
> *Additive Manufacturing (AM)* is the process of **joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer**, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methodologies. Synonymous with *3D printing*.

### 2.3.1 The Seven Standard AM Process Categories (ISO/ASTM 52900)

| Category | Process | Material | Layer Joining Mechanism |
|----------|---------|----------|-------------------------|
| **Vat Photopolymerization** | SLA, DLP | Photopolymer resin | UV light cures liquid resin |
| **Material Extrusion** | FDM / FFF | Thermoplastic filament (PLA, ABS, PETG) | Heated nozzle extrudes molten plastic |
| **Material Jetting** | PolyJet, MultiJet | Photopolymer droplets | Inkjet head deposits UV-cured droplets |
| **Binder Jetting** | Powder + binder | Sand, metal, ceramic powder | Liquid binder selectively glues powder |
| **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** | SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM | Polymer / Metal powder | Laser or electron beam fuses powder |
| **Sheet Lamination** | LOM (Laminated Object Mfg) | Paper, metal foil, plastic sheet | Sheets bonded and cut layer by layer |
| **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** | LMD, EBF³ | Metal powder or wire | Focused thermal energy melts as deposited |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024 Workshop Demonstration, the most commonly shown process is FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)**, as it is affordable, safe, and visually intuitive.

### 2.3.2 FDM Working Principle — Step by Step (Why & How)
1. **Filament Loading** — A spool of thermoplastic filament (typical diameters: **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm**) is loaded into the extruder.
2. **Feeding** — A *drive gear* (often paired with an *idler*) pushes the filament into the *hot end* (heater block).
3. **Heating & Melting** — The hot end heats the filament to its **glass transition + melt temperature**:
   - PLA: $\approx 180 - 220^\circ C$
   - ABS: $\approx 220 - 250^\circ C$
   - PETG: $\approx 220 - 250^\circ C$
4. **Extrusion & Deposition** — The molten plastic is squeezed out through a brass / hardened steel nozzle (typical diameter: **0.2 mm, 0.4 mm, 0.6 mm, 0.8 mm**).
5. **Layer-by-Layer Build** — The print head moves in X-Y (controlled by stepper motors + belts); the bed moves down in Z (typical layer height: **0.1 – 0.3 mm**).
6. **Solidification** — The deposited plastic cools and bonds with the previous layer.
7. **Support Structures** — Overhangs (angles > **45°** from vertical) require support material that is removed post-print.

---

## 2.4 Soft Material Cutting — Theoretical Background

### 2.4.1 Special Machines Used
- **Vinyl Cutter / Plotter** — A tangential knife moves on a 2D path, cutting adhesive vinyl, paper, cardstock (e.g., Roland CAMM-1, Silhouette Cameo).
- **Laser Cutter / Engraver** — A focused **CO₂ laser beam** (typical wavelength: **10.6 μm**) vaporizes or burns through fabric, wood, acrylic (not metal).
- **Die Cutting Machine** — A shaped steel blade (die) is pressed onto the sheet material with a mechanical press.
- **Drag Knife Cutter** — A non-rotating blade dragged behind a CNC router for thin sheet materials.
- **Digital Fabric Cutter** — A computer-controlled blade cuts through multiple layers of fabric (e.g., Gerber, Zünd).

### 2.4.2 Real-World Engineering / Design Utility
- **Textile & Apparel Industry** — Pattern cutting, marker making.
- **Signage Industry** — Vinyl lettering, logo cutting.
- **Packaging Industry** — Cardboard box prototyping.
- **Architecture Model Making** — Foam board, cardboard, acrylic models.
- **Aerospace & Automotive Interiors** — Fabric, leather, composite trim cutting.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Workshop Module)

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Usage Note:** Below are the *only* numerical formulas a student is expected to know for Module 2. They are derived from basic machining physics. Use `\vert` for absolute value separators in your workings.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | **Cutting Speed** | m/min | Lathe, milling, drilling |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | **Spindle Speed** | rpm | Selecting CNC spindle RPM |
| 3 | $f_n = N \cdot f_r$ | **Feed Rate** (table feed) | mm/min | CNC feed parameter |
| 4 | $MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot a_p$ | **Material Removal Rate** | mm³/min | Productivity estimation |
| 5 | $P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta}$ | **Cutting Power** | kW | Motor sizing |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{L + L_a + L_u}{f_n \cdot N_r}$ | **Machining Time (drilling)** | min | Cycle time calculation |
| 7 | $V_{filament} = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot L_{extruded}$ | **FDM Extrusion Volume** | mm³ | 3D print material estimate |
| 8 | $T_{print} \approx \dfrac{V_{model} \cdot 60}{V_{flow}}$ | **3D Print Time Estimate** | min | Print job planning |
| 9 | $E_{layer} = \rho_{filament} \cdot V_{layer}$ | **Layer Energy / Mass** | g | Mass-based cost estimate |
| 10 | $C_{total} = C_{material} + C_{machine} + C_{labor}$ | **Manufacturing Cost** | ₹ / $ | Project costing |

### Variable Definitions

- $V_c$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $D$ = tool / workpiece diameter (mm)
- $N$ = spindle speed (rpm)
- $f_r$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev)
- $f_n$ = table feed rate (mm/min)
- $a_p$ = depth of cut (mm)
- $F_c$ = cutting force (N)
- $\eta$ = machine efficiency (typical $\eta = 0.7 - 0.85$, dimensionless)
- $L, L_a, L_u$ = drill approach + cutting + overtravel length (mm)
- $N_r$ = number of teeth / flutes (dimensionless)
- $V_{flow}$ = volumetric flow rate of FDM extruder (mm³/s)
- $\rho_{filament}$ = density of filament (g/cm³) — PLA $\approx 1.24$, ABS $\approx 1.04$, PETG $\approx 1.27$

### Standard Boundary Conditions (Examiner's Reference)
- Cutting Speed (HSS tool on mild steel): $V_c \approx 25 - 35$ m/min
- Cutting Speed (carbide tool on mild steel): $V_c \approx 100 - 200$ m/min
- FDM Print Speed: $40 - 150$ mm/s
- Laser Cutter Power (fabric): $40 - 80$ W CO₂
- Vinyl Cutter Force: $50 - 400$ g

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cutting Speed Formula $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$

### Starting Point — Physical Definition
Cutting speed is defined as the **instantaneous linear velocity at which the cutting edge engages the workpiece surface** at the point of contact. For a rotating tool or workpiece, this is mathematically equivalent to the *tangential velocity* at the diameter of cut.

### Step 1: State the Definition of Rotational Speed
The spindle rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute. In one minute, every point on the rotating surface travels a **circumferential path** $N$ times.

$$
\text{Distance per minute} = N \times \pi D
$$

Here, $\pi D$ is the circumference of the circle of diameter $D$. The unit is **mm/min** since $D$ is in mm.

### Step 2: Convert mm/min to m/min
To convert mm to meters, we divide by 1000:

$$
V_c = \frac{N \times \pi D}{1000} \quad \left[ \text{in m/min} \right]
$$

### Step 3: Final Rearranged Form
Multiplying out:

$$
V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for cutting speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** "*Pi-D-N over thousand*" — the three variables in the numerator are circular (π), linear (D), and rotational (N).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) — Milling

### Step 1: Geometric Setup
In a milling operation, the cutter has a diameter $D$, the feed per tooth is $f_z$ (mm/tooth), the number of teeth is $z$, and the axial depth of cut is $a_p$ (mm). The radial width of cut is $a_e$ (mm).

### Step 2: Volume Removed in One Revolution
In one spindle revolution, the cutter advances by $f_r = f_z \cdot z$ mm. The cross-sectional area engaged in cutting is $a_e \cdot a_p$.

$$
\text{Volume per rev} = f_r \cdot a_e \cdot a_p \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{rev}]
$$

### Step 3: Convert to Volume per Minute
Multiply by the number of revolutions per minute, $N$:

$$
MRR = f_r \cdot a_e \cdot a_p \cdot N \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $f_r = f_z \cdot z$

$$
MRR = f_z \cdot z \cdot a_e \cdot a_p \cdot N
$$

### Step 5: Substitute $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ (from § 3.1)

$$
MRR = f_z \cdot z \cdot a_e \cdot a_p \cdot \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

### Step 6: Final Compact Form (Often Seen in CNC CAM)

$$
MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot a_p
$$

where $f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ is the *table feed* in mm/min. **This is the same form as for turning** — a beautiful unification that examiners love to test.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of 3D Print Time Estimate (FDM)

### Step 1: Total Volume of the Model
The 3D model's geometry, when sliced, yields $n$ layers. The volume of each layer is $V_{layer,i}$ (mm³).

$$
V_{model} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_{layer,i}
$$

For a uniform rectangular block of dimensions $L \times W \times H$:

$$
V_{model} = L \cdot W \cdot H
$$

### Step 2: Volumetric Flow Rate of the Extruder
The volumetric flow rate is the volume of plastic the extruder pushes out per second:

$$
V_{flow} = \pi \cdot r_{nozzle}^2 \cdot v_{print}
$$

Here, $r_{nozzle}$ is the nozzle radius (typical = 0.2 mm for a 0.4 mm nozzle), and $v_{print}$ is the print head travel speed in mm/s.

### Step 3: Express Time in Terms of Volume and Flow

$$
T_{print} = \frac{V_{model}}{V_{flow}}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to Minutes (Standard Reporting Unit)

$$
T_{print} \text{ (in min)} = \frac{V_{model} \times 60}{V_{flow}}
$$

### Step 5: Insert the $V_{flow}$ Expression

$$
T_{print} = \frac{V_{model} \times 60}{\pi \cdot r_{nozzle}^2 \cdot v_{print}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Adjustment Factor:** In practice, printers pause for **retraction** (~ 10–20% time overhead) and **acceleration/deceleration** at corners. A more accurate estimate uses $T_{actual} \approx 1.2 \times T_{print}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of the Four Methods

| Parameter | Power Tools | CNC Machine Tools | 3D Printing (FDM) | Soft Material Cutting |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Process Type** | Subtractive / Manual | Subtractive / Automated | Additive | Subtractive (2.5D) |
| **Human Skill Required** | High | Low (operator) | Low | Low |
| **Repeatability** | Low | Very High (≤ ± 0.01 mm) | Moderate (≤ ± 0.1 mm) | High (≤ ± 0.05 mm) |
| **Material Waste** | High | Moderate | **Very Low (5–15%)** | Low |
| **Setup Time** | Minimal | Moderate to High | Low | Low |
| **Batch Suitability** | Low (one-off) | High (mass production) | Low to Moderate | High |
| **Initial Cost** | Low | High (₹ 5 L – 1 Cr) | Low (₹ 10K – 5 L) | Moderate (₹ 50K – 10 L) |
| **Energy Consumption** | Low | High | Low to Moderate | Low |
| **Lead Time (Prototype)** | Hours | Hours to Days | **Hours (often 4–8 h)** | Minutes to Hours |
| **Geometry Complexity** | Low | Moderate to High | **Very High (any shape)** | Low (sheet only) |
| **KTU Demonstration** | Yes (briefly) | Yes (in Fab Lab) | Yes (in IDEA Lab) | Yes (vinyl / laser) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Practice Problem (Module Level):**
> A CNC lathe turns a mild steel workpiece of diameter $D = 50$ mm at a cutting speed $V_c = 30$ m/min. The feed per revolution is $f_r = 0.2$ mm/rev and the depth of cut is $a_p = 2$ mm. Calculate the spindle speed, the table feed, and the MRR.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Spindle Speed**
$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{30000}{157.08} \approx 191.0 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2: Table Feed Rate**
$$
f_n = N \cdot f_r = 191.0 \times 0.2 = 38.2 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 3: Material Removal Rate**
$$
MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot a_p = 30 \times 10^3 \times 0.2 \times 2 \text{ (mm}^3\text{/min, after unit alignment)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Alignment Warning:** Since $V_c$ is in m/min, multiply by **1000** to convert to mm/min:
> $V_c = 30 \text{ m/min} = 30{,}000 \text{ mm/min}$.

$$
MRR = 30000 \times 0.2 \times 2 = 12000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} = 12 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}
$$

### Final Answer Summary
- $N \approx 191$ rpm
- $f_n = 38.2$ mm/min
- $MRR = 12{,}000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — CNC Machine Tool Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CAD Design File] --> A2[CAM Software Toolpath]
    A2 --> A3[Post Processor G-code]
    A3 --> A4[CNC Controller MCU]
    A4 --> A5[Servo Drive X axis]
    A4 --> A6[Servo Drive Y axis]
    A4 --> A7[Servo Drive Z axis]
    A4 --> A8[Spindle Drive]
    A5 --> A9[Servo Motor X]
    A6 --> A10[Servo Motor Y]
    A7 --> A11[Servo Motor Z]
    A8 --> A12[Spindle Motor]
    A9 --> A13[Ball Screw X Linear Motion]
    A10 --> A14[Ball Screw Y Linear Motion]
    A11 --> A15[Ball Screw Z Linear Motion]
    A12 --> A16[Tool Rotation]
    A13 --> A17[Encoder Feedback X]
    A14 --> A18[Encoder Feedback Y]
    A15 --> A19[Encoder Feedback Z]
    A17 --> A4
    A18 --> A4
    A19 --> A4
    A16 --> A20[Workpiece Material Removal]
    A13 --> A20
    A14 --> A20
    A15 --> A20
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read This Diagram:**
> The leftmost column represents *software* (offline), the middle columns represent *control* and *drive electronics*, and the rightmost column represents *mechanical execution* with closed-loop feedback. This is a classic **CNC functional architecture** that appears in nearly every KTU module exam.

---

    B9 --> B10[Nozzle 0.4 mm Deposition]
