# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**List any three onsite field tests performed on bricks at a construction site. Briefly explain the efflorescence test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three onsite field tests for bricks are:
1. **Visual & Dimensional Test** – for shape, size, cracks (1 mark)
2. **Soundness Test** – two bricks struck together give a ringing metallic sound if well-burnt (1 mark)
3. **Water Absorption Test** – 24-hour immersion, absorption < 12 % for 1st class (1 mark)

> **Efflorescence Test (1 mark each = complete answer):**
> The brick is placed vertically in a shallow tray of distilled water (depth ~25 mm) in a well-ventilated shade until all water is absorbed/evaporated. The surface is then observed for white/grey patches of soluble salts. The result is rated as **Nil, Slight, Moderate, Heavy, or Serious** per IS 3495 (Part 3).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Bunch 2]`
**What is the significance of the initial and final setting time of cement? State the IS limits for Ordinary Portland Cement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*   **Initial Setting Time (1 mark):** It is the time elapsed between the moment water is added to the cement and the time the paste begins to lose its plasticity. It determines the **workability window** for mixing, transporting, and placing concrete. **IS limit: ≥ 30 minutes** for OPC.
*   **Final Setting Time (1 mark):** It is the time elapsed until the paste completely loses plasticity and gains sufficient hardness. **IS limit: ≤ 600 minutes (10 hours)** for OPC (1 mark).

> **Engineering significance:** If initial set is too short, concrete stiffens in the transit mixer → cold joints. If final set is too long, formwork stripping and early load application are delayed.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice – 14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **procedure and significance of the water absorption test for bricks** as per **IS 3495 (Part 2)**. State the maximum permissible values for different classes of bricks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **onsite tests conducted to assess the quality of cement** at a construction site. Mention at least five tests. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) – Water Absorption Test on Bricks (7 marks)

**Aim (1 mark):** To determine the percentage of water absorbed by a brick under standard conditions, as an indirect measure of its porosity, compactness, and durability.

**Apparatus (1 mark):** Drying oven (105–115 °C), weighing balance (± 1 g accuracy), water tank/bucket at 15–30 °C, a damp cloth.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Take a representative sample of **5 bricks** at random from the stack.
2. Dry each brick in the oven at **105 °C to 115 °C** until it attains a constant mass.
3. Cool to room temperature and weigh → record as **$W_1$ (oven-dry mass)**.
4. Immerse the brick completely in clean water at **15–30 °C** for **24 hours**.
5. Remove, wipe off surface water with a damp cloth, and weigh → record as **$W_2$ (saturated mass)**.
6. Compute the **absorption percentage** using:

$$\text{Absorption \%} = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_1} \times 100$$

**Significance & IS Limits (2 marks):**

| Class | Max. Water Absorption |
|---|---|
| 1st Class | < 12 % |
| 2nd Class | < 16 % |
| 3rd Class | < 20 % |

**Significance:** Lower absorption → denser, stronger, more weather-resistant brick. A high absorption brick sucks water out of the mortar, weakening the joint.

---

#### Part (b) – Onsite Tests for Cement (7 marks)

**1. Date / Freshness Test (1 mark):** Check manufacture date; cement should be used within 3 months. Bags with hard lumps or feeling warm should be rejected.

**2. Colour Test (1 mark):** Fresh OPC is uniform grey with a slight greenish tinge. Brownish or chalky colour indicates adulteration.

**3. Float / Adulteration Test (1 mark):** A pinch of cement sprinkled on water should **sink**. Floating particles indicate adulteration with sand, dust, or fly-ash.

**4. Consistency Test (1 mark):** Conducted using the **Vicat apparatus** to determine the % of water required for standard consistency (plunger penetration of 33–35 mm).

**5. Setting Time Test (1 mark):** Initial setting time ≥ 30 min and final setting time ≤ 10 hours as per **IS 269 / 8112 / 12269**.

**6. Compressive Strength Test (1 mark):** 70.6 mm mortar cubes (1:3 cement:sand) tested at 3, 7, and 28 days on a **CTM**. Must meet the 3-7-28 strength criteria of the relevant grade.

**7. Soundness Test (1 mark):** Le Chatelier method – expansion ≤ 10 mm for OPC.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`

**(a)** What is **efflorescence in bricks**? Describe the **efflorescence test** in detail. What are the causes and remedial measures? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Vicat apparatus test** for determining the **initial and final setting time of cement**. State the IS limits. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) – Efflorescence in Bricks (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Efflorescence is the whitish crystalline deposit (often sulphate or chloride salts) that appears on the exposed surface of bricks, concrete, or mortar due to the migration and precipitation of soluble salts.

**Causes (2 marks):**
*   Soluble salts in the **clay raw material** (especially if the brick earth is dug from saline soil).
*   **Mixing water** containing dissolved salts.
*   **Moisture penetration** from the back of the wall (rising damp, rain) which dissolves salts and brings them to the face as water evaporates.

**Efflorescence Test Procedure (3 marks) – as per IS 3495 (Part 3):**
1. Place the brick **end-vertical** in a shallow tray.
2. Pour **distilled water** into the tray so that the brick is immersed to a depth of about **25 mm**.
3. Keep the entire setup in a **well-ventilated, warm room (20–30 °C)** until **all the water is absorbed and the brick dries out completely** (about 7 days).
4. Examine the surface for white patches.
5. Rate the result as:

| Rating | Surface Coverage |
|---|---|
| **Nil** | No perceptible salt |
| **Slight** | < 10 % area |
| **Moderate** | 10–50 % area |
| **Heavy** | > 50 % area, no flaking |
| **Serious** | > 50 % + flaking / powdering |

**Remedial Measures (1 mark):** Use sulphate-resisting cement, avoid saline mixing water, apply water-proof plaster, and ensure proper DPC (damp-proof course) at the plinth level.

---

#### Part (b) – Vicat Apparatus Test (7 marks)

**Apparatus Description (2 marks):**
*   A weighted needle holder (total weight 300 g) carrying interchangeable plungers/needles.
*   A **conical Vicat mould** (40 mm top, 90 mm bottom, 80 mm height) resting on a non-porous plate.
*   The plunger/needle penetrates the cement paste vertically.

**Preparation (1 mark):** Mix **400 g of cement** with **0.85 × P × 4 = 0.85P g of water** (where P is the standard consistency %). The paste of standard consistency is filled into the mould.

**Initial Setting Time (2 marks):**
*   Replace the plunger with the **1 mm square Vicat needle**.
*   Release the needle gently every minute.
*   **Initial setting time** = the time elapsed between the moment water was added and the time when the needle fails to penetrate the paste to a point **5–7 mm from the bottom** of the mould.
*   **IS Limit:** ≥ 30 minutes for OPC.

**Final Setting Time (2 marks):**
*   Replace the needle with the **annular collar attachment**.
*   **Final setting time** = the time elapsed between the moment water was added and the time the collar **fails to make an impression on the paste surface**.
*   **IS Limit:** ≤ 600 minutes (10 hours) for OPC.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing "onsite" with "laboratory".** Do not write about XRD, SEM, or ultrasonic pulse velocity for a question that specifically says "onsite". Always restrict your answer to **Vicat, Le Chatelier, immersion tank, 70.6 mm cubes**.
> 2. **Mixing up cube sizes.** A 70.6 mm cube has an area of **5000 mm² (≈ 50 cm²)** – using 150 mm concrete cube dimensions here will cost you full marks. Remember: **cement cubes = 70.6 mm**, **concrete cubes = 150 mm**.
> 3. **Forgetting the W/C formula.** $W/C = P/4 + 3.0$ is **only for cement-mortar strength cubes** – NEVER use it for site concrete. The site concrete W/C is governed by **IS 456 Table 5** based on exposure condition and target strength.
> 4. **Quoting incorrect IS codes.** IS 1077 is for **burnt clay bricks**; IS 3495 is the **test method**; IS 269/8112/12269 are **OPC grades**. Mixing these up loses a full mark.
> 5. **Skipping the engineering interpretation.** KTU examiners give the *final 1–2 marks* for **"So what does this result mean for my site?"** – always end with an accept/reject decision with justification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision – Print This Section & Stick on Your Workshop Wall**

*   **Brick Standard Size:** **190 × 90 × 90 mm** (modular 200 × 100 × 100 mm with 10 mm joint).
*   **Brick Water Absorption Formula:** $(W_2 - W_1)/W_1 \times 100$, **< 12 %** for 1st class.
*   **Brick Crushing Strength:** $P/A$, **≥ 10 N/mm²** for 1st class.
*   **Efflorescence Cause:** Soluble sulphates of Na, K, Mg, Ca migrating to the surface.
*   **Efflorescence Rating:** **Nil, Slight, Moderate, Heavy, Serious** per IS 3495 (Pt 3).
*   **Soundness of Brick:** A **metallic ring** = good, a **dull thud** = under-burnt.
*   **Hardness of Brick:** A **fingernail** should leave **no scratch** on a 1st-class brick.
*   **Cement Standard Consistency:** Plunger penetration of **33–35 mm** using the **10 mm plunger**.
*   **Cement W/C for Lab Cubes:** $(P/4) + 3.0$.
*   **OPC Lab Cube Size:** **70.6 mm** → area = **5000 mm²**.
*   **OPC Grade Strengths (N/mm²) at 28 days:** 33 grade = 33, 43 grade = 43, 53 grade = 53.
*   **Initial Setting Time:** ≥ **30 min** (Vicat 1 mm square needle, 5–7 mm penetration criterion).
*   **Final Setting Time:** ≤ **600 min / 10 hr** (Vicat annular collar, no impression criterion).
*   **Le Chatelier Soundness:** ≤ **10 mm** expansion.
*   **Cement Fineness:** ≤ **10 %** residue on **90 µm IS sieve**.
*   **Cement Freshness:** Use within **3 months** of mfg date; cool, smooth bag with no lumps.
*   **Float Test for Adulteration:** Pure cement **sinks**; floating particles → reject.
*   **KTU Mnemonic for the 5 Cement Tests:** **"D C F S H"** → **D**ate, **C**olour, **F**loat, **S**etting, **H**ardness (consistency).
*   **KTU Mnemonic for the 5 Brick Tests:** **"V S H W E"** → **V**isual, **S**oundness, **H**ardness, **W**ater absorption, **E**fflorescence.
*   **Golden Rule:** *Onsite tests are NOT a substitute for laboratory tests. They are a* ***pre-screening filter*** *to catch blatantly defective consignments before they enter the permanent works.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Construct a 1 and 1 ½ thick brick wall with a height of 50 cm and a minimum length of 60 cm using English bond. Check the verticality of the wall

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**English Bond** is a brick bonding pattern in which the wall is built using **alternating courses of headers and stretchers**, where a header course (bricks placed with their ends facing outward, i.e., the 110 mm face visible) is laid over a stretcher course (bricks placed lengthwise, i.e., the 230 mm face visible), and vice versa. This is the standard bonding pattern recommended for walls of thickness **20 cm (1 brick thick)** and **30 cm (1.5 brick thick)** in load-bearing masonry construction as per IS 2212 and standard KTU Civil Engineering practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **English Bond Principle:** The *header course* breaks the vertical joints of the *stretcher course* below it, thereby distributing the load uniformly and preventing the development of continuous vertical (through) joints, which are structurally the weakest planes in a brick wall.

### 1.2 Wall Thickness Conventions (Indian Standard)

| Wall Type | Nominal Thickness | Brick Arrangement (per course) |
|---|---|---|
| **1 Brick Thick Wall** | **200 mm (20 cm)** | Single row of stretchers in every odd course, single row of headers in every even course |
| **1½ Brick Thick Wall** | **300 mm (30 cm)** | One row of stretchers + one row of headers in the same course (for stretcher courses); composed arrangement in header courses |

> [!NOTE]
> The **nominal brick size with mortar** is standardized at **200 mm × 100 mm × 100 mm** (Length × Width × Height). The actual burnt-clay brick size as per IS 1077 is **230 mm × 110 mm × 70 mm**, with 10 mm mortar joint on bed and 10 mm on head — giving the working size of 200 mm × 100 mm × 100 mm for layout purposes.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a brick wall built in English bond like a **weave in a jute sack or a basket**. If you tried to weave only horizontal strips (stretchers only), the weave would split apart lengthwise at the slightest tug. By alternating the orientation of every other row (headers), the strips "lock" into one another and the wall becomes a single, cohesive structural unit. 

Another simple analogy: imagine a **layered cake where each alternate layer is rotated 90°** — the cake is far stronger and less prone to cracking along horizontal planes. English bond applies this exact rotational principle to bricks.

Without this alternation, the wall would have continuous vertical joints from top to bottom, and a crack could travel straight down the entire height of the wall along those joints — a phenomenon called **straight joint failure**, which English bond is specifically designed to prevent.

## 1.4 Aim of the Experiment

To construct two short masonry wall panels:
1. A **1 brick thick (20 cm)** wall of minimum length **60 cm** and height **50 cm**.
2. A **1½ brick thick (30 cm)** wall of minimum length **60 cm** and height **50 cm**.

Both walls are to be built using the **English bond** pattern, and the **verticality** of the finished walls is to be checked using a **plumb bob** (and/or a **spirit level**).

> [!TIP]
> The height of 50 cm corresponds to exactly **5 courses of bricks** (5 × 100 mm nominal height = 500 mm), which is a deliberately clean academic number to make layout calculations simple for student-level practice.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Tools and Equipment Required

| Category | Item | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Measuring / Marking** | Measuring tape (3 m / 5 m) | Marking length, height, and course positions |
| | Mason's square (right-angle tool) | Checking 90° corners |
| | Line thread (chuna-dhaaga) + nails | Maintaining straight, level courses |
| **Alignment Tools** | Spirit level (300 mm) | Checking horizontal and vertical alignment |
| | Plumb bob (brass, ~250 g) | Checking true verticality of wall face |
| | Straight edge (wooden / aluminium) | Checking straightness of course |
| **Bricklaying Tools** | Trowel (pointing / brick trowel) | Laying, spreading, and cutting mortar |
| | Brick hammer / bolster chisel | Cutting and trimming bricks |
| | Mortar pan / board | Holding mortar near the work spot |
| | Jointing tool (iron / spoon) | Finishing mortar joints (raked / flush / concave) |
| **Safety** | Hand gloves, safety goggles, gumboots | Personal protection |

## 2.2 Materials Required

| Material | Specification (KTU Standard) | Quantity (Approx. for one wall panel) |
|---|---|---|
| **Bricks** | First-class, well-burnt, uniform red clay, free from cracks; IS 1077 | ~ 50–55 bricks for 1-brick wall; ~ 75–80 bricks for 1½-brick wall |
| **Cement** | Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC 43 or 53 grade), IS 269 / IS 8112 | As per mortar ratio |
| **Sand** | Fine aggregate, M-sand or river sand, zone II, IS 383 | As per mortar ratio |
| **Water** | Clean, potable, free from salts & organic matter | For mixing and curing |
| **Mortar** | Cement–sand mortar in the ratio **1 : 6** (by volume) for non-load-bearing / workshop practice walls | ~ 0.05 m³ per wall panel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mortar Ratio for Workshop Walls:** For KTU 2024 workshop practice walls (non-structural, short-duration, 50 cm height), the standard recommended mix is **Cement : Sand = 1 : 6** by volume. For load-bearing walls, the mix may be richer (1:4 or 1:5). The mortar should be used within **30 minutes of mixing** (initial setting time of cement ≈ 30 min) to avoid loss of workability.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind English Bond — Theoretical Justification

1. **Elimination of Vertical Joints in Adjacent Courses:** A header course (bricks placed perpendicular to the wall face) interrupts the continuous vertical joints of the stretcher course above and below. This prevents the formation of a "splitting plane" through the wall.
2. **Improved Load Distribution:** The header course helps distribute the vertical compressive load across the width of the wall, particularly important in 1½ brick walls.
3. **Better Transverse Strength:** Header bricks tie the outer and inner faces of the wall together, increasing its resistance to lateral forces (wind, accidental impact).
4. **Reduction of Differential Settlement Cracks:** Even settlement across the wall thickness is ensured by the through-tie action of header bricks.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Verticality Check

Verticality is one of the **three fundamental alignment checks** in brick masonry:

- **Horizontal (Level)** — top of every course must be perfectly level.
- **Vertical (Plumb)** — wall face must be perfectly vertical; deviation causes instability and unsightliness.
- **Alignment (Straight)** — wall must be in one straight line (no bulges or concavities).

> [!WARNING]
> As per IS 2212 (Code of Practice for Brickwork), the permissible verticality tolerance for a brick wall is **± 5 mm per 3 m height**. For our 50 cm workshop wall, this tolerance scales down to **± 0.83 mm**, but practically a deviation of **± 2 mm** is acceptable for student work.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Quick Reference Table

| Parameter | Value (with units) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal brick size (with mortar) | **200 mm × 100 mm × 100 mm** | L × W × H |
| Actual brick size (IS 1077) | **230 mm × 110 mm × 70 mm** | L × W × H |
| Mortar bed joint thickness | **10 mm** | Standard |
| Mortar head (vertical) joint thickness | **10 mm** | Standard |
| 1 brick wall thickness | **200 mm** | = 1 brick length |
| 1½ brick wall thickness | **300 mm** | = 1 brick length + 1 brick width |
| Stretcher face | **200 mm × 100 mm** (visible) | Length × Height |
| Header face | **100 mm × 100 mm** (visible) | Width × Height |
| Course height | **100 mm** | Brick height + 10 mm bed joint |
| Wall height in experiment | **500 mm** | = 5 courses |
| Min. wall length in experiment | **600 mm** | = 3 stretcher bricks |
| Permissible verticality deviation | **± 5 mm per 3 m** | IS 2212 |

# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

> The procedure below is divided into **Phase A: Pre-Construction**, **Phase B: Construction of 1-Brick Thick Wall**, **Phase C: Construction of 1½ Brick Thick Wall**, and **Phase D: Quality Checks**. Every step is written in explicit detail as required by the KTU 2024 OBE pattern.

## 3.1 Phase A — Pre-Construction Steps

### Step A1: Selection and Wetting of Bricks
- Select **first-class bricks** with uniform red colour, clear ringing sound when two bricks are struck together, and no visible cracks.
- **Immerse the bricks in clean water** for a minimum of **2 to 4 hours** before use.
- **Why?** Dry bricks absorb water rapidly from the fresh mortar, depriving the cement of the moisture needed for hydration, which weakens the mortar joint. Pre-wetting ensures that the brick-mortar interface develops full bond strength.

### Step A2: Preparation of the Foundation / Floor Surface
- The workshop floor (or a pre-laid concrete base) is cleaned with a wire brush and wetted.
- A **dry layout (setting out)** of the wall is marked on the floor using chalk and a measuring tape.
- The **outer-to-outer dimensions** for the 1-brick wall are **600 mm (length) × 200 mm (thickness)**.
- The **outer-to-outer dimensions** for the 1½-brick wall are **600 mm (length) × 300 mm (thickness)**.

### Step A3: Mixing of Mortar
- Mortar is mixed in the ratio **Cement : Sand = 1 : 6** by volume.
- The ingredients are first dry-mixed on a clean platform (or in a mixing tray) until a uniform grey colour is achieved.
- Clean potable water is then added gradually and the mix is turned over with a trowel until a **workable, plastic consistency** is obtained (the mortar should just hold its shape when a trowel is drawn through it).
- **Mortar consumption for one wall panel** ≈ 0.05 m³ (50 litres of mixed mortar).

$$
V_{\text{mortar per wall}} \approx 0.05 \ \text{m}^{3}
$$

### Step A4: Quantity Estimation — Number of Bricks per Course

**For the 1-Brick Thick Wall (stretcher course):**
- Course layout: 3 stretchers in a row → 3 × 200 mm = **600 mm length**, with 2 vertical joints of 10 mm each at the ends.
- Effective length per stretcher course = (3 × 200) + (2 × 10) = **620 mm nominal layout span**.

$$
N_{\text{stretcher course, 1-brick wall}} = 3 \ \text{stretchers}
$$

**For the 1-Brick Thick Wall (header course):**
- Each header occupies 100 mm of wall length. For a 600 mm length, we need 6 headers.
- Effective length per header course = (6 × 100) + (5 × 10) = **650 mm nominal layout span**.

$$
N_{\text{header course, 1-brick wall}} = 6 \ \text{headers}
$$

**For the 1½ Brick Thick Wall:**
- A stretcher course has one row of headers (inside) + one row of stretchers (outside).
- A header course is laid with two rows of headers.
- Per course of 1½-brick wall: **3 stretchers + 6 headers = 9 bricks per stretcher course**, and **12 headers per header course** (2 rows × 6 headers).

### Step A5: Course Calculation — Total Bricks for 500 mm Height

Total number of courses (for 500 mm wall height):

$$
N_{\text{courses}} = \dfrac{H_{\text{wall}}}{h_{\text{course}}} = \dfrac{500 \ \text{mm}}{100 \ \text{mm}} = 5 \ \text{courses}
$$

For a 1-brick wall, courses are **alternating stretcher–header–stretcher–header–stretcher** → 3 stretcher courses + 2 header courses.

$$
N_{\text{bricks, 1-brick wall}} = (3 \times 3) + (2 \times 6) = 9 + 12 = 21 \ \text{bricks}
$$

For a 1½-brick wall, courses are **alternating stretcher-combo–header-combo–stretcher-combo–header-combo–stretcher-combo** → 3 stretcher-type courses + 2 header-type courses.

$$
N_{\text{bricks, 1.5-brick wall}} = (3 \times 9) + (2 \times 12) = 27 + 24 = 51 \ \text{bricks}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A small surplus of ~10% extra bricks should always be kept aside to account for breakage, cutting, and the inevitable wastage during handling.

## 3.2 Phase B — Construction of the 1-Brick Thick Wall

### Step B1: Lay the First Course (Stretcher Course — Course 1)
- Spread a bed of mortar ~ 10–15 mm thick along the marked line for the full length of the wall.
- Place the **first brick** at one corner, pressing it firmly into the mortar so that the bed joint thickness is exactly 10 mm.
- Apply mortar on the head (vertical) face of the second brick and place it next to the first, leaving a 10 mm vertical joint.
- Continue until **3 stretchers** are placed.
- Tap each brick gently with the trowel handle to settle it.
- **Check the course** with the spirit level along its length and across its width.

### Step B2: Lay the Second Course (Header Course — Course 2)
- Before laying this course, the top of Course 1 should be lightly brushed clean and a fresh 10 mm mortar bed is spread.
- Place **6 header bricks**, each with its 100 mm × 100 mm face outward, perpendicular to the wall face.
- The vertical joints of this header course should **not align** with the vertical joints of the stretcher course below (overlap of at least 25 mm is required).
- Check the course for level and alignment using the line thread stretched between two corner bricks.

### Step B3: Lay Courses 3, 4, 5 (Stretcher, Header, Stretcher)
- Repeat the alternate pattern, with each header course offset by **half a brick (≈ 50–100 mm)** from the header course directly below it, to ensure proper breaking of joints.
- For each course, perform the following checks **before the mortar sets**:
  1. Top surface is **horizontal** (spirit level).
  2. Wall face is **vertical** (plumb bob / spirit level on the side).
  3. Wall is **in straight alignment** (line thread + straight edge).

### Step B4: Jointing
- Once the mortar in the joints becomes "thumbprint hard" (firm but not fully set, ~ 15–30 minutes after laying), finish the joints using the jointing tool.
- **Standard joint finish for workshop walls:** **raked joint** (raked to a depth of 10–15 mm) or **flush joint** (struck flush with the wall face).
- Jointing improves the aesthetic appearance and compacts the mortar for better weather resistance.

## 3.3 Phase C — Construction of the 1½ Brick Thick Wall

### Step C1: Lay the First Course (Stretcher + Header Combo)
- Spread a 10 mm mortar bed for the full 300 mm width.
- Lay the **outer row of 3 stretchers** along the front face, then lay the **inner row of 6 headers** perpendicular to them, all in the same course.
- The headers and stretchers interlock at the corners using **queen closers** (cut bricks of ½ width) if the bond requires them.
- For 1½ brick walls, it is standard to use a **queen closer** (a brick cut lengthwise into two equal halves) at the start of the header row to maintain the bond.

### Step C2: Lay the Second Course (Header Combo)
- Two rows of headers, one in front and one in back, with the headers in the back row offset by half a header length from the front row.
- Maintain 10 mm bed and head joints throughout.

### Step C3: Lay Courses 3, 4, 5
- Continue the alternate pattern (stretcher-combo / header-combo) for 5 courses total.
- Each course is checked for level, verticality, and alignment.

## 3.4 Phase D — Quality Checks (Critical for Marks)

### Check 1: Verticality Check using Plumb Bob
1. Hold the plumb bob by its string at the top edge of the wall.
2. Let the bob hang freely and settle.
3. The string should be exactly parallel to the wall face.
4. Measure the horizontal gap between the string and the wall at the **top**, **middle**, and **bottom** of the wall using a steel scale.
5. **Acceptance criterion:** Top, middle, and bottom gaps must be equal (within ± 2 mm for workshop tolerance).
6. If gaps are unequal, the wall leans — minor correction is possible while mortar is still plastic, otherwise it must be rebuilt.

### Check 2: Verticality Check using Spirit Level
1. Hold the spirit level vertically against the wall face.
2. The bubble should be **centred between the two indicator lines** in the vertical vial.
3. Check at multiple points along the wall height.

### Check 3: Horizontal (Level) Check
1. Place the spirit level on top of each course along its length and across its width.
2. The horizontal bubble should be centred.

### Check 4: Alignment / Straightness Check
1. Stretch a line thread (chuna-dhaaga) tightly between two nails driven at the ends of the wall.
2. The outer face of every course should just touch the thread — no brick should project outward or inward.

### Check 5: Squareness Check
1. At the corners, check the 90° angle using a mason's square or by the 3-4-5 method (i.e., 600 mm along one face, 800 mm along the other, the diagonal should measure 1000 mm).

## 3.5 Phase E — Curing

- After the mortar has set (typically after 24 hours), the wall must be **kept wet (cured) for a minimum of 7 days** by sprinkling water at regular intervals.
- Curing is essential to allow the cement to hydrate fully and develop its design strength.
- The walls should be protected from direct sunlight and heavy impact during the curing period.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Errors and Their Consequences

| Error | Likely Consequence | KTU Mark Deduction |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous vertical joints between adjacent courses | Loss of bond; future crack propagation | Severe (3–4 marks) |
| Wall leaning inward or outward | Verticality failure; structural instability | Severe (3–4 marks) |
| Bed joint thickness > 15 mm | Excess mortar → shrinkage cracks | Moderate (1–2 marks) |
| Dry bricks used (not pre-wetted) | Poor mortar–brick bond, weak joints | Moderate (1–2 marks) |
| Skipping plumb/level check | Out-of-plumb wall; rework required | Severe (2–3 marks) |
| Improper mixing ratio | Weak mortar; crumbly joints | Moderate (2 marks) |
| Joints not finished (no jointing) | Poor aesthetics, exposed joints | Minor (1 mark) |
| No curing / premature loading | Strength loss, surface dusting | Moderate (1–2 marks) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define English bond. State any two advantages of using English bond in brick masonry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** English bond is a brick bonding arrangement in which the wall is constructed using **alternating courses of headers and stretchers**, with the header course placed over a stretcher course and vice versa. This pattern ensures that no two consecutive courses have aligned vertical joints, providing structural integrity to the wall.

**Advantages (1.5 marks — any two):**
1. The header course acts as a tie, bonding the front and back faces of the wall together, increasing its **transverse strength**.
2. It **eliminates continuous vertical joints**, which are the weakest planes in a masonry wall, thereby preventing crack propagation.
3. It distributes **vertical loads more evenly** across the wall thickness.
4. It is **simple to construct** and is the most commonly used bond for walls of 1, 1½, and 2 brick thickness.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**List the standard nominal dimensions of a brick (with mortar). What is the thickness of a 1-brick wall and a 1½ brick wall?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Nominal brick size with mortar (1.5 marks):** Length × Width × Height = **200 mm × 100 mm × 100 mm**

**Wall thickness (1.5 marks):**
- 1-brick thick wall = 1 × length = **200 mm (20 cm)**
- 1½ brick thick wall = 1 × length + 1 × width = 200 + 100 = **300 mm (30 cm)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** between two questions on the same module. Below are two alternative model questions, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, fully solved.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain with a neat sketch the construction procedure of a 1-brick thick wall of length 60 cm and height 50 cm using English bond. List the tools required.**

**Model Answer:**

**Tools Required (2 marks):** Measuring tape, spirit level, plumb bob, brick trowel, line thread (chuna-dhaaga), mason's square, jointing tool, mortar pan, water bucket, and a straight edge.

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Material Preparation:** First-class bricks are selected, immersed in water for 2–4 hours, and stacked near the work spot. Mortar is mixed in the ratio **1:6 (cement:sand)** to a workable consistency.

2. **Layout Marking:** The base is cleaned and wetted. The wall dimensions (600 mm × 200 mm outer-to-outer) are marked on the floor using chalk and measuring tape.

3. **Course 1 (Stretcher Course):** A 10–15 mm mortar bed is spread along the marked line. Three stretcher bricks are laid end-to-end with 10 mm head joints. The course is checked with a spirit level for horizontality.

4. **Course 2 (Header Course):** A fresh mortar bed is spread. Six header bricks are placed perpendicular to the wall face, with their vertical joints offset by at least 25 mm from those in Course 1. The course is again checked for level.

5. **Courses 3, 4, 5:** Alternating stretcher and header courses are laid, with each header course offset by half a brick from the header course directly below.

6. **Verticality Check:** A plumb bob is held at the top edge of the wall, and the gap between the string and the wall is measured at top, middle, and bottom. The wall is accepted if the gaps are equal (within ± 2 mm).

7. **Jointing & Curing:** Joints are finished with a jointing tool once the mortar is thumbprint-hard, and the wall is cured with water for a minimum of 7 days.

---

**Q3(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**How is the verticality of a brick wall checked using a plumb bob? What is the permissible deviation as per IS 2212?**

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. Tie a string to the plumb bob and hold the string at the **top edge** of the freshly constructed wall.
2. Allow the plumb bob to hang freely in still air until it comes to rest.
3. The plumb line (string) should be **parallel** to the wall face if the wall is truly vertical.
4. Measure the **horizontal distance** between the string and the wall face at **three points** — top, middle, and bottom — using a steel scale.
5. The wall is **truly vertical (plumb)** if all three measured gaps are **equal**.
6. If the top gap is greater than the bottom, the wall **leans outward** (overhanging).
7. If the top gap is less than the bottom, the wall **leans inward**.

**Permissible Deviation (2 marks):** As per **IS 2212**, the maximum permissible verticality deviation is **± 5 mm per 3 m height** of the wall. For the 50 cm workshop wall, this tolerance scales to approximately **± 0.83 mm**, but a workshop tolerance of **± 2 mm** is generally accepted for student work.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between English bond and Flemish bond. State any four differences in a tabular form.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | English Bond | Flemish Bond |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Course pattern | Alternating full courses of headers and stretchers | Headers and stretchers alternate **within the same course** |
| 2 | Appearance | Distinct, alternating course appearance | Uniform, mixed appearance in every course |
| 3 | Strength | **Stronger** in load-bearing walls | Comparatively weaker due to many internal joints |
| 4 | Complexity | Simpler to lay | More complex, requires more cutting (closers) |
| 5 | Number of joints | Fewer head joints per unit area | More head joints per unit area |
| 6 | Use | Preferred for **load-bearing** walls | Preferred for **aesthetic / partition** walls |
| 7 | Cost | More economical | Costlier due to brick cutting labour |

*(Award 1 mark per correct difference, with a maximum of 4 marks. The table itself scores 0.5 mark for the correct tabular structure.)*

---

**Q4(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Calculate the number of bricks required to construct a 1-brick thick wall of length 3 m and height 2 m using English bond. Assume nominal brick size as 200 mm × 100 mm × 100 mm and 10% wastage.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Course height calculation (1 mark):**

$$
h_{\text{course}} = 100 \ \text{mm (brick height)} + 10 \ \text{mm (mortar joint)} = 110 \ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of courses in 2 m height (1 mark):**

$$
N_{\text{courses}} = \dfrac{H}{h_{\text{course}}} = \dfrac{2000 \ \text{mm}}{110 \ \text{mm}} = 18.18 \approx 19 \ \text{courses}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of bricks per course (2 marks):**
- **Stretcher course:** Wall length = 3000 mm, each stretcher = 200 mm + 10 mm joint = 210 mm. So,
$$
N_{\text{stretcher}} = \dfrac{3000}{210} = 14.28 \approx 15 \ \text{stretchers per course}
$$
- **Header course:** Wall length = 3000 mm, each header = 100 mm + 10 mm joint = 110 mm. So,
$$
N_{\text{header}} = \dfrac{3000}{110} = 27.27 \approx 28 \ \text{headers per course}
$$

**Step 4 — Bricks per course for 1-brick English bond (1 mark):** A 1-brick wall has **only one row of bricks per course** — so each course is either entirely stretchers or entirely headers.
- 19 courses → 10 stretcher + 9 header (alternating, starting with stretcher) OR 9 stretcher + 10 header.

**Step 5 — Total bricks (1 mark):**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = (10 \times 15) + (9 \times 28) = 150 + 252 = 402 \ \text{bricks (without wastage)}
$$

**Step 6 — Adding 10% wastage (1 mark):**

$$
N_{\text{final}} = 402 \times 1.10 = 442.2 \approx 443 \ \text{bricks}
$$

**Final Answer: 443 bricks (approx.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in This Experiment:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dry layout / marking** — Examiners specifically look for whether you "set out" the wall before laying bricks. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not wetting the bricks** — A pet peeve of examiners; failure to mention this shows incomplete knowledge of the procedure. Deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Mentioning only "plumb bob" but not "spirit level"** — Examiners expect *both* methods; mentioning only one is incomplete. Deduct 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing nominal vs actual brick size** — A common error. Use **200 × 100 × 100 mm (nominal)** for layout calculations and **230 × 110 × 70 mm (actual)** for ordering. Mixing them up loses 1 mark.
> 5. **No mention of curing** — Often overlooked; examiners deduct 1 mark for omitting curing.
> 6. **In numericals, ignoring mortar joints** — Always include the 10 mm joint in the per-brick effective length; failing to do so produces a wrong answer.
> 7. **Wrong mortar ratio** — Writing 1:2 or 1:4 (which are concrete/plaster ratios) instead of the correct **1:6** for brickwork.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **English bond = alternating stretcher and header courses.** Simple, strong, standard for 1 and 1½ brick walls.
- **Nominal brick size (with mortar) = 200 mm × 100 mm × 100 mm.** Actual size (IS 1077) = 230 mm × 110 mm × 70 mm.
- **Mortar joint thickness = 10 mm** (bed and head).
- **1-brick wall thickness = 200 mm; 1½ brick wall thickness = 300 mm.**
- **Course height = 100 mm (brick) + 10 mm (joint) = 110 mm** (theoretical), but workshop layout uses **100 mm nominal** for simplicity.
- **Wall height in this experiment = 500 mm = 5 courses.**
- **Min. wall length in this experiment = 600 mm = 3 stretchers.**
- **Mortar mix = 1:6 (Cement:Sand) by volume** for non-load-bearing workshop walls.
- **Pre-wet bricks for 2–4 hours** to ensure proper mortar–brick bond.
- **Three alignment checks:** (1) Horizontal — spirit level on top of course, (2) Vertical — plumb bob / vertical spirit level, (3) Alignment — line thread stretched between two ends.
- **Plumb bob procedure:** Hold at top, measure gap at top/middle/bottom, all three must be equal for a plumb wall.
- **IS 2212 verticality tolerance:** ± 5 mm per 3 m height; workshop tolerance for 500 mm wall ≈ ± 2 mm.
- **Jointing** is done when mortar is "thumbprint-hard" (~15–30 min after laying), using raked or flush finish.
- **Curing** is mandatory for **minimum 7 days** by sprinkling water.
- **English bond advantages:** Eliminates continuous vertical joints, distributes load evenly, ties wall faces together, and is simple to construct.
- **English bond vs Flemish bond:** English bond has alternating *courses*; Flemish bond has alternating *bricks within the same course*.
- **Queen closer:** A brick cut lengthwise in half, used at corners of 1½ brick walls to maintain the bond.
- **Tools to remember for the exam:** Plumb bob, spirit level, brick trowel, line thread, mason's square, measuring tape, jointing tool, mortar pan.
- **Materials to remember:** Bricks (1st class), cement (OPC 43/53), sand (M-sand / river sand, zone II), water (clean, potable).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construct a 1 and 1 ½ thick brick wall with a height of 50 cm and a minimum length of 60 cm using Flemish bond. Check the verticality of the wall
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCESL106 - ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Construct a 1 and 1 ½ thick brick wall with a height of 50 cm and a minimum length of 60 cm using Flemish bond. Check the verticality of the wall

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Brick Masonry** is a highly durable form of construction built by laying **burnt clay bricks** in a systematic manner with **cement mortar** (or lime mortar) as the binding agent, following a defined geometric pattern called a **bond**. The bond ensures structural stability, even distribution of loads, and an aesthetically pleasing appearance.

A **Bond** in brickwork is the arrangement of bricks in successive courses (layers) such that the vertical joints of one course do not align with the vertical joints of the courses immediately above and below it. The vertical joint alignment is offset by a minimum of **¼ brick length** (about 5 cm) to prevent continuous vertical weak planes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flemish Bond (Definition – KTU Standard)**
> Flemish bond is a composite bond in which every course (layer) consists of **alternating headers and stretchers**, with headers placed centrally over the stretchers in the course below. The headers in alternate courses align vertically with each other, and the same is true for the stretchers.

### Two Sub-Variants of Flemish Bond
1. **Single Flemish Bond** – Flemish bond is provided on the *face (front) side* of the wall only, while the *back side* is built in English bond. Adopted for walls of thickness ≥ 1½ bricks where economy on the face is desired.
2. **Double Flemish Bond** – Flemish bond is provided on *both faces* of the wall, with the interior filled with stretchers. Adopted for walls of thickness ≥ 1 brick where both faces need to look decorative. **This is the variant used in the KTU experiment** because both 1-brick and 1½-brick walls need to be built on a small platform.

### Wall Thickness Terminology
| Specification | Meaning | Nominal Thickness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1 Brick Thick** | Wall one brick laid on its flat (stretcher position) deep | **20 cm** (≈ 9 inches with 10 mm mortar joint) |
| **1½ Brick Thick** | Wall equivalent to 1½ brick lengths deep | **30 cm** (≈ 13.5 inches with 10 mm mortar joint) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The LEGO Analogy** – Think of bricklaying exactly like stacking LEGO blocks. If you stack them with all the studs perfectly aligned vertically, the wall becomes wobbly and can split along the long vertical seams. To make it strong, you offset every alternate row by half a brick — this is the very essence of a bond.

**Flemish Bond Analogy – "The Weaving Pattern"**
Imagine a **basket-weave** or a **finger-locked handshake**. In Flemish bond, headers (the shorter face) and stretchers (the longer face) are placed alternately in the *same* row, just like alternating over-and-under in a woven mat. This creates an **interlocking pattern within a single course**, in addition to the interlock between successive courses — a double-locking system that gives Flemish bond its superior visual finish and structural integrity.

A **header** = brick laid with its *end* (head) visible on the face (length 20 cm, height 10 cm).  
A **stretcher** = brick laid with its *long side* (stretch) visible on the face (length 20 cm, height 10 cm).

## 1.3 Standard Brick & Mortar Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Modular Brick (IS 1077)** : **Length = 20 cm**, **Width = 10 cm**, **Height = 10 cm** (with nominal mortar joint of 10 mm → effective size = **20 × 10 × 10 cm**).

Standard mortar for this experiment: **Cement Mortar 1:6** (1 part cement : 6 parts sand by volume), which is the IS-recommended mix for general brickwork. Water-cement ratio is maintained so that the mortar is *plastic* and *workable* but does not ooze out under brick weight.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Rules Governing Flemish Bond (KTU Board-Examiner Favourite)

The following rules **must be strictly followed** in the exam answer and during the practical. Loss of marks occurs if any rule is missed.

- **Rule 1** – Every course must contain **alternating headers and stretchers** placed next to each other in the same horizontal layer.
- **Rule 2** – The **header of a course must be centrally placed** over the stretcher of the course immediately below (and vice-versa).
- **Rule 3** – **Headers in alternate courses must align vertically** with each other; similarly, **stretchers in alternate courses must align vertically**.
- **Rule 4** – **No continuous vertical joint** should be formed either in the face of the wall or in the body of the wall. The minimum lap (offset) between joints of successive courses must be **¼ of the brick length** (≈ 5 cm).
- **Rule 5** – The **thickness of mortar joints** should be uniform throughout and should **not exceed 10 mm**.
- **Rule 6** – In **Double Flemish Bond**, the same pattern (alternating header-stretcher) is shown on *both* faces of the wall. The interior is filled with stretchers, so the headers in one face are stretchers on the back and vice-versa.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Flemish Bond is harder than English Bond:** In English bond, each course is *purely* headers OR *purely* stretchers — the carpenter has to change brick orientation only between courses. In Flemish bond, the orientation *alternates within the same course* — this requires careful selection of bricks and is more time-consuming, which is why Flemish bond is generally more expensive.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Volume of wall (1-brick thick) | $V_1 = L \times H \times T$ | m³ | $V_1 = 0.60 \times 0.50 \times 0.20 = 0.060$ m³ |
| Volume of wall (1½-brick thick) | $V_{1.5} = L \times H \times T$ | m³ | $V_{1.5} = 0.60 \times 0.50 \times 0.30 = 0.090$ m³ |
| Effective volume of one brick (with 10 mm joint) | $V_b = 0.20 \times 0.10 \times 0.10$ | m³ | $= 0.002$ m³ |
| Number of bricks (nominal) | $N = V_{wall} \,/\, V_b$ | nos. | Multiply by **1.15** wastage factor → 35 & 52 respectively |
| Dry volume of mortar | $V_{dm} = 1.30 \times V_{wet\,mortar}$ | m³ | 30 % extra added for voids & wastage |
| Verticality tolerance | $\vert \Delta h \vert \le 5$ mm per 1 m height | mm | As per IS 1905 & KTU Lab Manual |
| Plumb-bob angle (ideal) | $\theta = 0^{\circ}$ from vertical | degrees | Plumb line must coincide with face of wall |
| Mortar mix (general brickwork) | $1 : 6$ (Cement : Sand) | ratio | By volume, IS 2250 |
| Mortar joint thickness (modular) | $t_j = 10$ mm | mm | Uniform throughout the wall |
| Minimum lap (offset) between joints | $\ell = \dfrac{1}{4} L_{brick} = 5$ cm | cm | KTU examination rule |

## 2.3 Practical Utility in Civil Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Flemish Bond is Used in Real Life**
> - *Public buildings* — schools, colleges, government offices — where the architectural face is visible.
> - *Boundary walls* of residential and commercial plots where aesthetics matter.
> - *Front elevation* walls of residential buildings (single Flemish — back is English for economy).
> - *Load-bearing walls* up to 2 storeys when brick strength is ≥ 7.5 N/mm².
> Flemish bond is **NOT** used for thick retaining walls or high-stress structural walls — English bond is preferred there because it gives a stronger internal mass.

## 2.4 Pre-Experiment Material Estimation (for Viva)

**For 1-brick thick wall (60 cm × 50 cm × 20 cm):**
- Gross volume = **0.060 m³**
- Approx. bricks required (15 % wastage) = $0.060 \,/\, 0.002 \times 1.15 \approx$ **35 bricks**
- Approx. dry mortar = **0.015 m³** → Cement ≈ **2.5 kg**, Sand ≈ **15 kg**

**For 1½-brick thick wall (60 cm × 50 cm × 30 cm):**
- Gross volume = **0.090 m³**
- Approx. bricks required (15 % wastage) = $0.090 \,/\, 0.002 \times 1.15 \approx$ **52 bricks**
- Approx. dry mortar = **0.022 m³** → Cement ≈ **3.7 kg**, Sand ≈ **22 kg**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Tools, Equipment & Materials Required

### Tools & Equipment
- **Spirit Level (300 mm)** – checks horizontal level of each course.
- **Plumb Bob (with 2 m thread)** – checks verticality of the wall face.
- **Mason's Trowel (pointed & flat)** – to pick, spread, and place mortar.
- **Wooden Float** – to finish mortar joints.
- **Brick Hammer** – to cut bricks to halves/quarters.
- **Chisel & Bolster** – for splitting bricks cleanly.
- **Straight Edge / Aluminium Level (1 m)** – for plane check.
- **Mason's Line (Nylon thread)** – stretched between profiles to guide course alignment.
- **Measuring Tape (3 m)**, **Steel Rule**, **Right Angle (try square)**.
- **Mixing Tray / Pan & Water Bucket** – for mortar preparation.
- **Sieve (4.75 mm)** – to sieve sand free of debris.

### Materials
- **Modular Bricks (IS 1077)** – soaked in water for **at least 30 minutes** before use.
- **Portland Pozzolana Cement (PPC / OPC 53 grade)**.
- **River Sand (Medium grade, passing 4.75 mm sieve)**.
- **Clean Potable Water**.

## 3.2 Pre-Construction Setup (Mandatory)

**Step A – Surface Preparation**
The platform / ground must be cleaned, levelled, and wetted to prevent the foundation from absorbing water from the mortar (which would weaken the bond).

**Step B – Profile / Corner Setup**
Erect two **profiles** (wooden boards with brick-course markings) at the two ends of the proposed 60 cm wall. Stretch a **mason's line** between them at the level of the first course. The line acts as the alignment guide for *all* bricks in the course.

**Step C – Mortar Preparation (1:6 Mix)**
The cement and sand are dry-mixed on a clean platform until a uniform grey colour is obtained. Water is added in small quantities and mixed thoroughly to produce a plastic, workable mortar of *slump ~ 70–100 mm* (just wet enough to spread but not so wet that it runs off the trowel).

$$
V_{dry\,mortar} = 1.30 \times V_{wet\,mortar}
$$

The **30 % bulkage factor** accounts for voids in dry sand, water addition, and the small wastage during application.

**Step D – Brick Soaking**
Bricks are dipped in a water tank for **30 minutes** until air bubbles stop rising. Soaking is critical because dry bricks absorb water from the mortar, leading to poor adhesion and cracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 30 minutes?** A properly soaked brick has its pores saturated. When mortar is applied, the brick takes *no* water from the mix, allowing cement to hydrate fully and develop bond strength.

## 3.3 Construction Procedure – 1 Brick Thick Flemish Wall

The wall is built in **5 courses** (each course = 10 cm), giving a total height of **50 cm**.

### Course 1 (First Course – Foundation Course)
1. Lay a mortar bed of ~ 12 mm thickness along the line.
2. Place the first brick as a **stretcher**, pressed firmly into the mortar and tapped to a level.
3. Continue alternating **stretcher → header → stretcher → header** along the 60 cm length.
4. Total bricks in the course = 3 (each brick is 20 cm long, but since the wall is 60 cm long, 3 stretchers fit; headers contribute 10 cm each but in 1-brick wall headers form the depth, not the length). For a 60 cm long × 20 cm thick 1-brick wall, lay **3 stretchers face-on** for the first course (purely stretcher base course for stability).
5. Check the course with the **spirit level** along its length and across its width.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Practice for 1-Brick Wall**
> Although pure Flemish bond would have alternating header-stretcher in every course, a 1-brick thick (20 cm) wall physically can only show either header or stretcher on the face at one time. Hence a 1-brick Flemish wall is technically built as a **Double Flemish** wall with *header face on one side and stretcher face on the other*. For exam purposes, students must clearly show the face arrangement in the diagram.

### Course 2 to Course 5
1. Apply a uniform 10 mm mortar bed on top of Course 1.
2. Lay Course 2 with the **header** face visible on the front. The header in Course 2 must be **centrally placed over the stretcher** of Course 1.
3. The wall face alternates between header and stretcher views course-by-course. For 1-brick wall, you may adopt the simplified pattern where every *odd* course is a stretcher face and every *even* course is a header face.
4. After every course, check **horizontal level** and **verticality** using spirit level and plumb bob respectively.
5. Tool the mortar joints (recess / flush / keyed) as per requirement using a wooden float or jointer.
6. Clean off excess mortar spillage from the face with a damp sponge.

## 3.4 Construction Procedure – 1½ Brick Thick Flemish Wall

This wall is built in 5 courses, total height = 50 cm, with **Double Flemish Bond** (Flemish face on both sides).

### Course 1 (Foundation Course)
1. Lay a continuous 12 mm mortar bed.
2. Place bricks in a Double Flemish pattern: the front face shows alternating **header–stretcher**, the rear face shows the **mirror image** so the pattern matches on both sides.
3. The interior of the wall is filled with stretchers (headers of front = stretchers of back).
4. The 60 cm length will accommodate **3 brick units on the face** (each unit = 1 header + 1 stretcher = 30 cm × 2 = 60 cm), or alternatively **2 full bricks + 1 cut brick** depending on starting point.

### Course 2 to Course 5
1. Each course begins with a header on the front face, then a stretcher, then a header, etc.
2. The vertical joints in Course 2 are **offset by 10 cm** (half-brick) from Course 1. This means the *header of Course 2 sits centrally over the stretcher of Course 1*.
3. Stretchers in Course 2 align *vertically* with stretchers in Course 4 (alternate course rule), and headers in Course 2 align vertically with headers in Course 4.
4. Check **verticality with plumb bob** at the end of every course.
5. Use the **straight edge** to check the wall face is planar (no bulges or depressions).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Course-wise Brick Layout (60 cm length, 1½ brick wall)

| Course No. | Face Brick 1 (0–20 cm) | Face Brick 2 (20–40 cm) | Face Brick 3 (40–60 cm) | Mortar Joint | Verticality |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Header (10 cm) + Stretcher (10 cm) | Stretcher (20 cm) | Stretcher (20 cm) → Header at end | 10 mm | Check ✓ |
| 2 | Stretcher (20 cm) | Header (10 cm) + Stretcher (10 cm) | Stretcher (20 cm) | 10 mm | Check ✓ |
| 3 | Header (10 cm) + Stretcher (10 cm) | Stretcher (20 cm) | Stretcher (20 cm) → Header at end | 10 mm | Check ✓ |
| 4 | Stretcher (20 cm) | Header (10 cm) + Stretcher (10 cm) | Stretcher (20 cm) | 10 mm | Check ✓ |
| 5 | Header (10 cm) + Stretcher (10 cm) | Stretcher (20 cm) | Stretcher (20 cm) → Header at end | 10 mm | Check ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> The pattern repeats every **two courses** in Flemish bond. Courses 1, 3, 5 are identical in their face pattern, and Courses 2, 4 are identical but offset by 10 cm. The header position alternates course by course, providing the characteristic **interlocking weave** of Flemish bond.

## 3.6 Verticality Check Procedure (Derivation of Tolerance)

**Plumb Bob Method:**
1. Hold the plumb bob by its thread at the top edge of the wall corner.
2. Allow the bob to settle to a stationary position.
3. The bob's tip should touch the ground directly below the suspension point.
4. The **plumb line** (thread) should be **perfectly parallel** to the face of the wall (offset distance = wall thickness from the thread to the inner face should be uniform at top and bottom).
5. Measure the **deviation** $\Delta h$ between the plumb line and the wall face at the top, middle, and bottom of the wall.

**Acceptance Criterion (IS 1905 / KTU Lab Manual):**

$$
\Delta h_{max} \le 5 \text{ mm per 1 m height of wall}
$$

For our 50 cm wall, the absolute deviation allowed is:

$$
\Delta h_{max} = 5 \text{ mm} \times \dfrac{0.50 \text{ m}}{1.00 \text{ m}} = 2.5 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta h_{max} = 2.5 \text{ mm (for 50 cm wall)}}
$$

If the deviation exceeds 2.5 mm, the wall must be **dismantled and rebuilt** in the affected region — corrections to set plumb are not permitted because they create weak planes.

## 3.7 Safety Precautions (KTU Mandatory Section)

- Always wear **safety gloves** while handling wet cement mortar to prevent skin irritation.
- Wear **safety goggles** when cutting bricks with a chisel to protect eyes from flying chips.
- Keep the **work area wet** (lightly sprinkled) to suppress cement dust.
- **Wash hands and face** thoroughly with soap after the experiment.
- **Do not** apply mortar in direct strong sunlight or rain; both affect curing.
- All **tools must be cleaned** immediately after use (cement hardens quickly and ruins tools).
- A **first-aid kit** must be present in the workshop.

## 3.8 Post-Construction Curing

The completed walls must be **cured for at least 7 days** by sprinkling water 3 to 4 times daily. Curing allows the cement to hydrate fully, reaching its design strength.

$$
\text{Curing duration} = 7 \text{ to } 14 \text{ days (as per IS 456)}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams follow the KTU diagram protocol. They use the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** style — physical 2D/3D pictorial drawings are avoided because they are not natively supported; instead, the functional layout is mapped precisely to convey the construction pattern.

## 4.1 Block Diagram – Flemish Bond Course Pattern (60 cm Wall)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Course1["COURSE 1 - Top View"]
        A1["H Header 10cm"] --- B1["S Stretcher 20cm"] --- C1["S Stretcher 20cm"] --- D1["H Header 10cm"]
    end
    subgraph Course2["COURSE 2 - Offset by 10cm"]
        A2["S Stretcher 20cm"] --- B2["H Header 10cm"] --- C2["S Stretcher 20cm"] --- D2["S Stretcher 20cm"]
    end
    subgraph Course3["COURSE 3 - Same as Course 1"]
        A3["H Header 10cm"] --- B3["S Stretcher 20cm"] --- C3["S Stretcher 20cm"] --- D3["H Header 10cm"]
    end
    Course1 === Course2
    Course2 === Course3
    C1 -. "vertical joint offset by 10 cm" .-> A2
    B2 -. "vertical joint offset by 10 cm" .-> A3
```

```

    end

    FF --> Core
    Core --> BF

```

## 4.4 Block Diagram – Verticality Check Procedure
