### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional operation of a Digital Multimeter (DMM)**. Explain the role of the **rotary selector switch, signal-conditioning blocks, ADC, and the LCD display**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A circuit has a node of source impedance $R_s = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$ and a true DC voltage of $V = 5.00 \text{ V}$. Calculate the **loading error** when measured with (i) an analog VOM having input impedance of $R_m = 200 \text{ k}\Omega$, and (ii) a digital DMM with $R_m = 10 \text{ M}\Omega$. Comment on which instrument is more suitable. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** A DMM is a multi-function electronic instrument that measures voltage, current, and resistance, displaying the result numerically on an LCD.

2. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** The signal from the test probe is routed by the **rotary selector switch** to one of the four signal-conditioning blocks:
   - **Voltage Divider Attenuator** – for V and mV ranges (input impedance 10 MΩ).
   - **Shunt Resistor Network** – for current ranges (µA, mA, A).
   - **Test-Current Source** – for resistance (Ω) and continuity modes.
   - **AC-to-DC Rectifier** – converts AC to its RMS-equivalent DC for accurate ADC reading.

3. **ADC Role (2 marks):** The conditioned DC signal is fed to a **dual-slope integrating ADC** (e.g., ICL7106) that converts the analog magnitude into a digital BCD count over a fixed integration period. This count is decoded by a display driver into 7-segment signals.

4. **LCD & Microcontroller Role (2 marks):** The microcontroller formats the count, applies range multipliers, and drives the **3½-digit or 4½-digit LCD** to display the numerical value with the appropriate SI prefix (mV, V, µA, kΩ, MΩ). A separate buzzer driver activates the audible continuity tone.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 – State the loading error formula (1 mark):**

$$E_{loading} = \frac{R_s}{R_m + R_s}$$

$$V_{measured} = V_{actual} \cdot \frac{R_m}{R_m + R_s}$$

**Step 2 – Substitute for Analog VOM (2 marks):**

$$R_s = 100 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad R_m = 200 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$V_{measured} = 5.00 \cdot \frac{200\,000}{100\,000 + 200\,000} = 5.00 \cdot \frac{200}{300} = 5.00 \cdot 0.6667 = 3.33 \text{ V}$$

$$E_{loading} = \frac{100}{300} = 0.3333 = 33.33 \,\%$$

**Step 3 – Substitute for Digital DMM (2 marks):**

$$R_m = 10 \text{ M}\Omega = 10\,000 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$V_{measured} = 5.00 \cdot \frac{10\,000\,000}{100\,000 + 10\,000\,000} = 5.00 \cdot \frac{10\,000}{10\,100} = 5.00 \cdot 0.9901 = 4.95 \text{ V}$$

$$E_{loading} = \frac{100}{10\,100} = 0.0099 = 0.99 \,\%$$

**Step 4 – Comparison & Comment (2 marks):**

The DMM introduces a loading error of less than **1 %**, whereas the analog VOM gives an error of over **33 %**. The DMM is therefore **clearly more suitable** for high-impedance electronic circuits. The analog VOM is acceptable only for low-impedance power circuits (e.g., 12 V battery monitoring across a thick copper busbar) where the source impedance $R_s$ is a few milliohms.

**Tabulated Final Answer (self-check, 0 marks but useful):**

| Instrument | $V_{measured}$ | $E_{loading}$ | Verdict |
|------------|----------------|----------------|---------|
| Analog VOM (200 kΩ) | 3.33 V | 33.33 % | Unacceptable |
| Digital DMM (10 MΩ) | 4.95 V | 0.99 % | Suitable ✓ |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of a soldering iron**. Discuss the role of the *heating element*, the *tip (bit)*, the *temperature sensor (in a soldering station)*, and the importance of *tinning the tip*. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** List and briefly explain the **safety precautions** to be observed while (i) soldering, (ii) using a DMM to measure mains voltage, and (iii) operating a hot air rework station. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

1. **Principle of Operation (2 marks):** A soldering iron works on **Joule heating**. When current passes through a high-resistance **nichrome wire** wound on a ceramic former, electrical energy is converted into heat:

$$P = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}$$

This heat is conducted through a thermally conductive **ceramic insulator** to the **copper bit** (tip), raising its temperature to the working range of **200 °C – 480 °C**.

2. **Heating Element (1 mark):** Nichrome (Ni-Cr alloy) is used because it has a high resistivity (~1.0–1.5 × 10⁻⁶ Ω·m), forms a protective Cr₂O₃ oxide layer that prevents burn-out, and can withstand temperatures above 1000 °C.

3. **Tip / Bit (2 marks):** The bit is made of **copper** (high thermal conductivity ≈ 400 W/m·K) plated with **iron and nickel** to prevent the copper from dissolving into the molten solder (a phenomenon called *iron dissolution*). The tip's geometry (conical, chisel, knife, hoof) is selected to match the joint type.

4. **Temperature Sensor in Soldering Station (1 mark):** A **thermocouple (Type K)** embedded in the tip generates a small mV signal proportional to tip temperature. This is fed back to a **PID controller**, which adjusts the power via a **zero-crossing TRIAC** to maintain the set temperature within **±5 °C**.

5. **Tinning the Tip (1 mark):** A freshly cleaned tip is coated with a thin layer of molten solder. This *tinned* layer acts as a **heat-transfer bridge** between the iron and the joint, increasing thermal conductance by **5×–10×** and preventing oxidation of the copper substrate.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**(i) Soldering Safety (2 marks):**
- Always work in a **well-ventilated area** or use a **fume extractor** to avoid inhaling rosin fumes.
- Wear **safety goggles** to protect against solder splashes.
- Use an **ESD wrist strap** to prevent damage to sensitive components.
- Place the iron in a **safety stand** when not in use — never lay it on the workbench.
- Allow the iron to **cool naturally**; never quench in water (thermal shock cracks the ceramic).

**(ii) DMM Mains-Voltage Safety (3 marks):**
- Use **CAT III 600 V / CAT IV 1000 V rated test leads** with shrouded banana plugs and finger guards.
- **Inspect leads** for cracked insulation before every use.
- Use the **one-hand rule** — keep one hand behind the back or in a pocket to prevent the body from becoming a current path if the second probe slips.
- **Never** attempt to measure current on a mains circuit by setting the DMM to volts and connecting in parallel — this causes a **short circuit** and blows the internal fuse or explodes the meter.
- Confirm the DMM is set to the correct **ACV range** (typically ≥ 600 V) before probing.

**(iii) Hot Air Station Safety (2 marks):**
- Never point the hot nozzle at skin, hair, or flammable materials (the air at 400 °C causes instant burns).
- Hold the nozzle **2–5 cm** away from the PCB to avoid **delaminating the FR-4 substrate**.
- Ensure the **airflow is ON** before activating the heater — running a heater with no airflow causes the ceramic PTC to overheat and fail.
- After switching off, let the **fan continue to run** for at least 60 s to cool the heater element.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Based on KTU 2024 Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing DMM series vs. parallel connection (loses 2–3 marks):** Voltage = parallel, Current = series. Examiners *specifically* test this misconception.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the unit of the DMM reading (loses 0.5 mark):** A bare number `4.95` is *wrong*; the correct answer is `4.95 V`.
> 3. **Calling a soldering iron's tip "iron" or "steel" (loses 1 mark):** The tip is *copper with iron plating*. This is a frequently tested descriptor.
> 4. **Omitting the "tinning the tip" step (loses 1 mark):** Examiners treat tinning as a *mandatory* step in the soldering procedure.
> 5. **Writing `|x|` inside a markdown table (breaks the table renderer and may lose 0.5 mark for unreadable answer):** Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX and reserve `|` for column separation.
> 6. **Forgetting ESD precautions:** IPC-A-610 examiners specifically deduct marks for absence of ESD-safe handling discussion.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet-Form Essentials:**
>
> - **DMM** measures V, A, Ω; has **10 MΩ** input impedance; uses **dual-slope ADC**; **continuity buzzer** triggers below ~30 Ω.
> - **Voltage** measurement is **parallel**; **current** is **series**; **resistance** must be measured on a **de-energized** circuit.
> - **Soldering iron** uses **Joule heating** in a **nichrome element**; tip is **copper + iron plating**; working temp **200 °C–480 °C**.
> - **Solder alloy** 60/40 Sn-Pb melts at **183 °C** (eutectic); lead-free SAC305 melts at **217 °C**.
> - **Always tin the tip** before soldering — increases heat transfer by **5×–10×**.
> - **De-soldering pump** provides a vacuum of **≈ 66 kPa**; uses a **PTFE nozzle** to avoid sticking.
> - **Hot air station** uses **PTC ceramic heater** and **diaphragm blower**; air **200 °C–500 °C**; airflow **1–50 L/min**.
> - **Crimping tool** produces a **gas-tight joint** with resistance **< 5 mΩ**; meets **MIL-DTL-22520**.
> - **Wire strippers** use **gauged notches (AWG 10–30)**; **flush cutters** give HRC 58–62 cut; **needle-nose pliers** have HRC 55–60 jaws.
> - **ESD-safe tweezers** use **stainless steel or CFRP**; tip flatness ≤ 0.05 mm for SMD placement.
> - **Screw drivers** must have **ESD-safe handles** with resistivity **10⁶–10⁹ Ω**.
> - **Universal safety**: ESD wrist-strap, anti-static mat, safety goggles, fume extractor, **one-hand rule** for mains probing.
> - **Industrial standards referenced**: IPC-A-610, IPC J-STD-001, MIL-DTL-22520, IEC 61010-031.
> - **Key temperatures to remember**: Soldering (315–370 °C), Hot Air (350–450 °C), SMD Reflow SAC305 (245–260 °C peak), Eutectic 60/40 (183 °C).
> - **Mathematical must-knows**: $P = I^2 R$ (iron heating), $E_{loading} = R_s / (R_m + R_s)$ (DMM loading), $F_{min} = k \cdot A$ (crimp tensile strength), $T(t) = T_a + (T_s - T_a)e^{-t/\tau}$ (joint cooling).
> - **Pictorial mnemonics**: Iron = heated pen; Pump = medical syringe; Crimping = mechanical welder; Hot air = blowtorch; DMM = stethoscope.
